> - **Density** = $\dfrac{N_{nz}}{m \times n}$ (typically $\leq 0.05$ for sparse matrices)
> - **Sparsity** = $1 - \text{Density} = \dfrac{N_z}{m \times n}$
> - **Space Saving** = $1 - \dfrac{3(N_{nz}+1)}{m \times n}$ (in tuple form)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polynomial Representation — Operational Breakdown

### (A) Dense Polynomial Representation

**Why this works:** In dense representation, the *exponent* is implicitly encoded by the *array index*. This makes random access $O(1)$ but wastes memory for polynomials with many zero coefficients.

**Logical Steps:**

1. Let the polynomial be of maximum degree $n$.
2. Declare a 1-D array `A[0..n]` of size $n+1$.
3. For each term $a_i x^i$ in the polynomial, set $A[i] = a_i$.
4. For any missing term (zero coefficient), the array slot remains **0**.

**C-style Structure:**
```c
#define MAX_DEGREE 100
float polyA[MAX_DEGREE + 1];
int  n;  // highest degree
```

**Pros & Cons:**

| Aspect | Dense Form |
|---|---|
| Memory | $O(n+1)$ always |
| Random access | $O(1)$ |
| Best for | Polynomials with few zero terms |
| Worst for | High-degree polynomials with few non-zero terms (e.g., $x^{1000} + 1$) |

### (B) Sparse Polynomial Representation

**Why this works:** A polynomial like $P(x) = 5x^{1000} + 3x^{500} + 7$ has only 3 non-zero terms but degree 1000. Storing it densely would waste 998 memory slots. Sparse representation stores only the 3 meaningful pairs.

**Logical Steps:**

1. Define a `struct term { float coeff; int expon; }`.
2. Allocate an array `poly[1..MAX_TERMS]` of such structures.
3. Maintain a counter `n` for the number of non-zero terms.
4. Terms are typically stored in **descending order of exponent** to ease operations.

**C-style Structure:**
```c
#define MAX_TERMS 100
struct Polynomial {
    float coeff;
    int   expon;
} poly[MAX_TERMS];
int n = 0;  // number of non-zero terms
```

**Pros & Cons:**

| Aspect | Sparse Form |
|---|---|
| Memory | $O(k)$ where $k$ = number of non-zero terms |
| Random access | $O(k)$ linear scan |
| Best for | Polynomials with very few non-zero terms |

---

## 2.2 Sparse Matrix — Tuple Representation Breakdown

### Logical Steps to Convert a Matrix into Tuple Form:

1. **Scan** every element of the original $m \times n$ matrix row by row.
2. If an element $A[i][j] \neq 0$, append the triple $(i, j, A[i][j])$ to the tuple list.
3. Store the metadata in row 0: `(m, n, N_{nz})`.
4. Continue until the full matrix is scanned.

### Sparse Matrix Transpose (KTU High-Yield Operation)

**Definition:** Transpose of a sparse matrix is obtained by swapping rows and columns: $B[j][i] = A[i][j]$.

**Algorithm 1 — Naive Approach (Using Tuple Form):**
- For each tuple entry $(i, j, val)$ in the original matrix, simply swap $i$ and $j$ to produce $(j, i, val)$ in the transposed matrix.
- **Issue:** The resulting tuples will **NOT** be in row-major order. A sort is required.

**Algorithm 2 — Fast Transpose (KTU Favorite — Asked Repeatedly):**
- Pre-compute the number of non-zero elements in each column of the original matrix.
- Use these counts to determine the starting index of each row in the transposed tuple.
- This achieves $O(N_{nz} + n)$ time complexity instead of $O(N_{nz} \cdot n)$.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This Table for the Exam — These Formulas Appear Almost Every Year.**

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Polynomial degree | $n$ | Highest exponent with non-zero coefficient |
| Dense array size | $n + 1$ | One slot per exponent from $0$ to $n$ |
| Sparse poly terms | $k$ | Number of non-zero coefficients |
| Memory in dense form | $(n+1) \cdot \text{sizeof}(coeff)$ | In bytes |
| Memory in sparse form | $k \cdot (\text{sizeof}(coeff) + \text{sizeof}(expon))$ | In bytes |
| Matrix sparsity | $\dfrac{N_z}{m \cdot n}$ | Fraction of zero entries |
| Matrix density | $\dfrac{N_{nz}}{m \cdot n}$ | Fraction of non-zero entries |
| Tuple form rows | $N_{nz} + 1$ | Including header row |
| Tuple form cols | $3$ | row, column, value |
| Fast Transpose | $O(N_{nz} + n)$ | where $n$ = number of columns |
| Naive Transpose | $O(N_{nz} \cdot n)$ | Sorting required afterwards |
| Poly Addition (sparse) | $O(m + n)$ | $m, n$ = number of non-zero terms in each |
| Poly Multiplication | $O(m \cdot n \cdot k)$ | Where $k$ = result terms |
| Horner's evaluation | $O(n)$ | For degree-$n$ polynomial |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why Engineers Care About These Representations:**
> - **Polynomial Representation:** Used in **curve fitting** for CAD/CAM software, **signal processing** (filter design), **machine learning** (regression models), and **scientific computing** libraries like BLAS/LAPACK.
> - **Sparse Matrix Representation:** Critical in **Google's PageRank algorithm**, **finite element analysis** (FEA) in structural engineering, **computational fluid dynamics (CFD)**, **circuit simulation** (SPICE), **recommendation systems**, and **graph algorithms** (adjacency matrix of large networks like social media).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Polynomial in Dense Form

**Given:** $P(x) = 6x^5 + 4x^3 - 2x^2 + 7$

**Step 1:** Identify the highest degree.  
$n = 5$

**Step 2:** Determine the size of the coefficient array.  

$$\text{Array size} = n + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6$$

**Step 3:** Initialize all entries to 0 (default in C):  

$$\text{poly}[0..5] = \{0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0\}$$

**Step 4:** Map each non-zero coefficient to its index.

| Term | Coefficient | Exponent | Array Index | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $6x^5$ | 6 | 5 | 5 | poly[5] = 6 |
| $4x^3$ | 4 | 3 | 3 | poly[3] = 4 |
| $-2x^2$ | -2 | 2 | 2 | poly[2] = -2 |
| $7$ | 7 | 0 | 0 | poly[0] = 7 |

**Step 5:** Final array representation.

$$\text{poly} = \{7, \ 0, \ -2, \ 4, \ 0, \ 6\}$$

**Memory used:** $(5+1) \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 24$ bytes (for `float` type).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Polynomial in Sparse Form

**Given:** $Q(x) = 8x^{12} - 4x^7 + 5x^2 - 3$

**Step 1:** Identify non-zero terms: $k = 4$.

**Step 2:** Construct the array of `struct term` with `coeff` and `expon`, sorted by decreasing exponent.

| Index `i` | Coefficient `coeff` | Exponent `expon` |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | 12 |
| 2 | -4 | 7 |
| 3 | 5 | 2 |
| 4 | -3 | 0 |

**Step 3:** `n` (global count) = 4.

**Memory used:** $4 \times (4 + 4) = 32$ bytes (assuming 4 bytes each for `float` and `int`).

**Comparison:** If stored in dense form, we would need $(12+1) \times 4 = 52$ bytes. **Savings = 20 bytes** for this small example. For $x^{1000} + 1$, savings become dramatic.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Sparse Matrix in Tuple Form

**Given Matrix $A$ (5 × 6):**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 8 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 7 & 0 & 0 \\
5 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 4 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 6 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1:** Count non-zero elements by scanning.

| Row | Non-zero entries |
|---|---|
| 0 | 1 (at col 4) |
| 1 | 1 (at col 3) |
| 2 | 1 (at col 0) |
| 3 | 1 (at col 2) |
| 4 | 1 (at col 1) |

Total: $N_{nz} = 5$

**Step 2:** Build tuple representation $(N_{nz} + 1) \times 3$ table.

| Row (in tuple) | Col Index | Row Index | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 5 | Header: 5 rows, 6 cols, 5 non-zeros |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 6 | Header: rows = 5, cols = 6 |
| 0 | 2 | 0 | 5 | Header: non-zeros = 5 |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 4 | $A[0][4] = 8$ |
| 1 | 1 | 4 | 8 | $A[0][4] = 8$ |
| 1 | 2 | 8 | 0 | $A[0][4] = 8$ |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 3 | $A[1][3] = 7$ |
| 2 | 1 | 3 | 7 | $A[1][3] = 7$ |
| 2 | 2 | 7 | 0 | $A[1][3] = 7$ |
| 3 | 0 | 2 | 0 | $A[2][0] = 5$ |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | 5 | $A[2][0] = 5$ |
| 3 | 2 | 5 | 0 | $A[2][0] = 5$ |
| 4 | 0 | 3 | 2 | $A[3][2] = 4$ |
| 4 | 1 | 2 | 4 | $A[3][2] = 4$ |
| 4 | 2 | 4 | 0 | $A[3][2] = 4$ |
| 5 | 0 | 4 | 1 | $A[4][1] = 6$ |
| 5 | 1 | 1 | 6 | $A[4][1] = 6$ |
| 5 | 2 | 6 | 0 | $A[4][1] = 6$ |

**Step 3:** Simplified (only meaningful columns):

| (rows, cols, N_{nz}) | Tuple entries |
|---|---|
| Header | $(5, 6, 5)$ |
| 1 | $(0, 4, 8)$ |
| 2 | $(1, 3, 7)$ |
| 3 | $(2, 0, 5)$ |
| 4 | $(3, 2, 4)$ |
| 5 | $(4, 1, 6)$ |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation — Fast Transpose Algorithm

**Goal:** Given sparse matrix $A$ stored as tuple with $N_{nz}$ entries and $n$ columns, produce its transpose $B$ in row-major order, efficiently.

**Step 1:** Compute `row_terms[i]` = number of non-zero elements in **column $i$** of $A$, for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n-1$.

**Step 2:** Compute `starting_pos[i]` = cumulative sum indicating the **starting row in $B$** for entries that were in column $i$ of $A$.

$$\text{starting\_pos}[0] = 1$$

$$\text{starting\_pos}[i] = \text{starting\_pos}[i-1] + \text{row\_terms}[i-1] \quad \text{for } i \geq 1$$

**Step 3:** For each tuple entry $(i, j, val)$ in $A$:

$$\text{Place } (j, i, val) \text{ at } B[\text{starting\_pos}[j]]$$

$$\text{Increment } \text{starting\_pos}[j] \text{ by 1}$$

**Worked Example with the matrix above:**

`row_terms` (column-wise non-zero count of $A$):

| Column $j$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Count | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

`starting_pos`:

$$\text{starting\_pos}[0] = 1$$
$$\text{starting\_pos}[1] = 1 + 1 = 2$$
$$\text{starting\_pos}[2] = 2 + 1 = 3$$
$$\text{starting\_pos}[3] = 3 + 1 = 4$$
$$\text{starting\_pos}[4] = 4 + 1 = 5$$
$$\text{starting\_pos}[5] = 5 + 1 = 6$$

**Processing each tuple:**

- $(0, 4, 8)$ → place $(4, 0, 8)$ at $B[5]$, increment `starting_pos[4]` to 6.
- $(1, 3, 7)$ → place $(3, 1, 7)$ at $B[4]$, increment `starting_pos[3]` to 5.
- $(2, 0, 5)$ → place $(0, 2, 5)$ at $B[1]$, increment `starting_pos[0]` to 2.
- $(3, 2, 4)$ → place $(2, 3, 4)$ at $B[3]$, increment `starting_pos[2]` to 4.
- $(4, 1, 6)$ → place $(1, 4, 6)$ at $B[2]$, increment `starting_pos[1]` to 3.

**Result $B$ (header = $(6, 5, 5)$):**

| $B[1]$ | $B[2]$ | $B[3]$ | $B[4]$ | $B[5]$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(0, 2, 5)$ | $(1, 4, 6)$ | $(2, 3, 4)$ | $(3, 1, 7)$ | $(4, 0, 8)$ |

**Verification:** Original $A$ has non-zero at $(0,4)$. Transpose $A^T$ should have non-zero at $(4, 0)$. Indeed $B[5] = (4, 0, 8)$. ✓

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

$$T(n) = O(n) \text{ for computing row\_terms} + O(n) \text{ for starting\_pos} + O(N_{nz}) \text{ for placing}$$

$$T(n) = O(n + N_{nz})$$

This is **far superior** to the naive $O(n \cdot N_{nz})$ approach.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Representation

| Scenario | Recommended Representation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Degree 5 polynomial, mostly non-zero | Dense | Less overhead, fast access |
| Degree 1000 polynomial, only 5 non-zero terms | Sparse | Massive memory savings |
| Dense $1000 \times 1000$ matrix with $\geq 5\%$ non-zeros | 2-D Array | Tuple overhead not justified |
| $1000 \times 1000$ matrix with $\leq 5\%$ non-zeros | Tuple | Saves $> 95\%$ memory |
| Graph adjacency of social network (millions of nodes) | Tuple/CSR | Networks are extremely sparse |
| Image pixel data | 2-D Array | Most pixels non-zero |
| Finite Element stiffness matrix | Tuple/CSR | Engineering matrices highly sparse |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Polynomial Representation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Polynomial P of x] --> B{Degree n Analysis}
    B --> C[Identify Non-Zero Terms]
    C --> D{Density Check}
    D -->|High Density| E[Dense Representation]
    D -->|Low Density| F[Sparse Representation]
    E --> E1[Array A of size n+1]
    E1 --> E2[Index = Exponent]
    E2 --> E3[Value = Coefficient]
    F --> F1[Array of Struct term]
    F1 --> F2[Field 1: coeff]
    F1 --> F3[Field 2: expon]
    F3 --> G[Storage Ready for Operations]
    E3 --> G
    G --> H[Operations Add Multiply Evaluate]
    H --> I[Output Result]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Sparse Matrix Tuple Representation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Input Matrix A m x n] --> S[Scan All Elements Row by Row]
    S --> Q{Is Element Zero}
    Q -->|Yes| S
    Q -->|No| T[Append Triple row col value to Tuple]
    T --> S
    S --> R[Store Header row m col n non-zeros]
    R --> P[Final Tuple Form N nz + 1 rows x 3 cols]
    P --> O1[Operation Transpose]
    P --> O2[Operation Addition]
    P --> O3[Operation Multiplication]
    O1 --> OUT[Convert Back if Needed]
    O2 --> OUT
    O3 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — Fast Transpose Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start] --> INIT[Initialize row terms array of size n to 0]
    INIT --> LOOP1[For each tuple entry k from 1 to N nz]
    LOOP1 --> INC1[Increment row terms of A k column 1]
    INC1 --> LOOP1
    LOOP1 --> CALC[Compute starting pos array cumulatively]
    CALC --> LOOP2[For each tuple entry k from 1 to N nz]
    LOOP2 --> SWAP[Set j = A k column 1]
    SWAP --> PLACE[Place entry at B starting pos j]
    PLACE --> UPD[Increment starting pos j]
    UPD --> LOOP2
    LOOP2 --> FIN[Set B header and return]
```

## 4.4 Memory Layout — Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Dense
        D0[poly 0: 7]
        D1[poly 1: 0]
        D2[poly 2: -2]
        D3[poly 3: 4]
        D4[poly 4: 0]
        D5[poly 5: 6]
    end
    subgraph Sparse
        S1[term1: coeff 6, exp 5]
        S2[term2: coeff 4, exp 3]
        S3[term3: coeff -2, exp 2]
        S4[term4: coeff 7, exp 0]
    end
    Dense --- Sparse
```

## 4.5 Sparse Matrix Tuple Form — Visual Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Header
        H1[rows 5]
        H2[cols 6]
        H3[nonzeros 5]
    end
    subgraph Data
        R1[0 4 8]
        R2[1 3 7]
        R3[2 0 5]
        R4[3 2 4]
        R5[4 1 6]
    end
    Header --> Data
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In **polynomial sparse representation**, students often forget to maintain terms in **descending order of exponent** — this is mandatory for the addition algorithm to work correctly. Marks deducted: 2 marks typically.
> - In **tuple representation**, the **header row** is frequently omitted or written incorrectly. Always include `rows`, `cols`, `N_{nz}` in row 0. Marks deducted: 1–2 marks.
> - In **fast transpose**, students often confuse `row_terms` (which counts entries per *column* of $A$) with row-wise counts. Remember: `row_terms[i]` = how many non-zero elements are in **column $i$** of $A$.
> - Always state the **time complexity** of your algorithm in Part B answers. KTU examiners award a separate mark for complexity analysis.

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a sparse matrix? How do you represent it using tuple form? Give a small example.**

**Model Answer:**

A sparse matrix is one in which the number of zero elements is much larger than the number of non-zero elements (density $\leq 5\%$). In tuple (triplet) representation, we store only the non-zero elements as a list of triples of the form `(row, column, value)`, prefixed with a header row `(m, n, N_{nz})`.

**Example:** For matrix

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Tuple form:

| Row | Col | Val |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | 3 | 3 | ← header |
| 0 | 1 | 5 |
| 1 | 0 | 3 |
| 2 | 2 | 4 |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Compare dense and sparse polynomial representations.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dense | Sparse |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | Array of size $n+1$ | Array of $k$ structures |
| Memory | $O(n)$ | $O(k)$ |
| Suitable for | Polynomials with few zero terms | Polynomials with few non-zero terms |
| Access | Direct ($O(1)$) | Sequential ($O(k)$) |
| Example | $5x^4 + 3x^3 - 2x + 1$ | $5x^{1000} + 1$ |

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a) Represent the polynomial $P(x) = 7x^5 + 4x^4 - 3x^2 + 6$ using:**
**(i) Dense array representation** — *3 marks*
**(ii) Sparse (array of structures) representation** — *4 marks*

**(b) Write the algorithm (or C code) to add two polynomials represented in sparse form and explain its time complexity.** — *7 marks*

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a) (i): Dense Representation**

Degree $n = 5$, so array size $= n + 1 = 6$.

Initialize `poly[0..5] = {0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0}`.

Map coefficients to indices:

| Exponent | Coefficient | Array Index |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 6 | poly[0] = 6 |
| 2 | -3 | poly[2] = -3 |
| 4 | 4 | poly[4] = 4 |
| 5 | 7 | poly[5] = 7 |

**Final dense array:**

$$\text{poly} = \{6, \ 0, \ -3, \ 0, \ 4, \ 7\}$$

**Memory:** $6 \times 4 = 24$ bytes (for `float`).

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a) (ii): Sparse Representation**

`struct term` array of size $k = 4$ (number of non-zero terms), sorted by descending exponent:

| Index `i` | `coeff` | `expon` |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7 | 5 |
| 2 | 4 | 4 |
| 3 | -3 | 2 |
| 4 | 6 | 0 |

Global variable `n = 4`. Memory: $4 \times 8 = 32$ bytes (assuming 4 bytes per field).

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b): Polynomial Addition Algorithm in Sparse Form**

**Given:** Two polynomials $A$ (with $m$ non-zero terms) and $B$ (with $n$ non-zero terms), both sorted in descending order of exponent. Result $C$ is initially empty with pointer `c = 1`.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Initialize `i = 1, j = 1, c = 1`.
2. **Repeat while** $i \leq m$ **and** $j \leq n$:
   - **Case 1:** If $A[i].\text{expon} == B[j].\text{expon}$:
     - $\text{coeff} = A[i].\text{coeff} + B[j].\text{coeff}$
     - If $\text{coeff} \neq 0$, append $(\text{coeff}, A[i].\text{expon})$ to $C$, increment `c`.
     - Increment both $i$ and $j$.
   - **Case 2:** If $A[i].\text{expon} > B[j].\text{expon}$:
     - Append $(A[i].\text{coeff}, A[i].\text{expon})$ to $C$, increment $i$ and $c$.
   - **Case 3:** If $A[i].\text{expon} < B[j].\text{expon}$:
     - Append $(B[j].\text{coeff}, B[j].\text{expon})$ to $C$, increment $j$ and $c$.
3. **Copy remaining** terms of whichever polynomial was not fully traversed into $C$.

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

Each term of $A$ and $B$ is examined exactly once:

$$T(m, n) = O(m + n)$$

**Space Complexity:** $O(m + n)$ for the result.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**(a) Consider the sparse matrix:**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 0 & 2 & 0 \\
6 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 7 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 5 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 3 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**(i) Write its tuple representation.** — *3 marks*
**(ii) Explain the Fast Transpose algorithm and apply it to obtain the transposed tuple form.** — *4 marks*

**(b) Write a complete C program (or detailed algorithm) to add two sparse matrices stored in tuple form and discuss its time complexity.** — *7 marks*

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a) (i): Tuple Representation**

Scan $A$ row by row and collect non-zero entries:

| Row | Col | Val |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | 5 | 5 | ← header |
| 0 | 3 | 2 |
| 1 | 0 | 6 |
| 1 | 4 | 7 |
| 3 | 1 | 5 |
| 4 | 2 | 3 |

$N_{nz} = 5$.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a) (ii): Fast Transpose**

**Algorithm Concept:** The fast transpose avoids sorting the result by pre-computing the exact destination row for each entry.

**Step 1 — Compute `row_terms[i]` (column-wise count in $A$):**

| Column $j$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Count | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 2 — Compute `starting_pos[i]`:**

$$\text{starting\_pos}[0] = 1$$
$$\text{starting\_pos}[1] = 1 + 1 = 2$$
$$\text{starting\_pos}[2] = 2 + 1 = 3$$
$$\text{starting\_pos}[3] = 3 + 1 = 4$$
$$\text{starting\_pos}[4] = 4 + 1 = 5$$

**Step 3 — Place each entry:**

- $(0, 3, 2)$ → place $(3, 0, 2)$ at $B[4]$; `starting_pos[3] → 5`
- $(1, 0, 6)$ → place $(0, 1, 6)$ at $B[1]$; `starting_pos[0] → 2`
- $(1, 4, 7)$ → place $(4, 1, 7)$ at $B[5]$; `starting_pos[4] → 6`
- $(3, 1, 5)$ → place $(1, 3, 5)$ at $B[2]$; `starting_pos[1] → 3`
- $(4, 2, 3)$ → place $(2, 4, 3)$ at $B[3]$; `starting_pos[2] → 4`

**Resulting Transpose $B$ (header $(5, 5, 5)$):**

| B Index | Row | Col | Val |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 6 |
| 2 | 1 | 3 | 5 |
| 3 | 2 | 4 | 3 |
| 4 | 3 | 0 | 2 |
| 5 | 4 | 1 | 7 |

**Verification:** Original $A[0][3] = 2$ → Transpose $A^T[3][0] = 2$ ✓

**Time Complexity:** $O(N_{nz} + n)$ where $n$ = number of columns of $A$.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b): C Program to Add Two Sparse Matrices in Tuple Form**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#define MAX 100

typedef struct {
    int row, col, val;
} Triple;

void readSparse(Triple a[], int *n) {
    int i;
    printf("Enter rows, cols, non-zeros: ");
    scanf("%d %d %d", &a[0].row, &a[0].col, &a[0].val);
    *n = a[0].val;
    printf("Enter %d triples (row col val):\n", *n);
    for (i = 1; i <= *n; i++)
        scanf("%d %d %d", &a[i].row, &a[i].col, &a[i].val);
}

void addSparse(Triple a[], Triple b[], Triple c[]) {
    int i = 1, j = 1, k = 1;
    int m = a[0].val, n = b[0].val;
    if (a[0].row != b[0].row || a[0].col != b[0].col) {
        printf("Incompatible matrices\n");
        c[0].val = 0;
        return;
    }
    c[0].row = a[0].row;
    c[0].col = a[0].col;
    while (i <= m && j <= n) {
        if (a[i].row < b[j].row ||
           (a[i].row == b[j].row && a[i].col < b[j].col)) {
            c[k++] = a[i++];
        } else if (a[i].row > b[j].row ||
                  (a[i].row == b[j].row && a[i].col > b[j].col)) {
            c[k++] = b[j++];
        } else {
            int sum = a[i].val + b[j].val;
            if (sum != 0) {
                c[k].row = a[i].row;
                c[k].col = a[i].col;
                c[k].val = sum;
                k++;
            }
            i++; j++;
        }
    }
    while (i <= m) c[k++] = a[i++];
    while (j <= n) c[k++] = b[j++];
    c[0].val = k - 1;
}

void display(Triple a[]) {
    int i, n = a[0].val;
    printf("\nRow Col Val\n");
    for (i = 0; i <= n; i++)
        printf("%3d %3d %3d\n", a[i].row, a[i].col, a[i].val);
}

int main() {
    Triple A[MAX], B[MAX], C[MAX];
    readSparse(A, NULL);
    readSparse(B, NULL);
    addSparse(A, B, C);
    printf("\nResult:");
    display(C);
    return 0;
}
```

**Time Complexity:**

Each triple of $A$ and $B$ is visited at most once:

$$T(m, n) = O(m + n)$$

where $m$ and $n$ are the number of non-zero entries in $A$ and $B$ respectively.

**Space Complexity:** $O(m + n)$ for the result matrix $C$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam:**

- ✅ A **polynomial** is uniquely determined by its coefficients and their exponents. There is no need to store zero terms.
- ✅ **Dense polynomial** representation uses an array of size **$n+1$**; index = exponent, value = coefficient.
- ✅ **Sparse polynomial** representation uses an array of `struct term {coeff, expon}`; only non-zero terms stored, count $= k$.
- ✅ A matrix is **sparse** if its **density $\leq 0.05$** (i.e., $\leq 5\%$ non-zero entries).
- ✅ **Tuple representation** stores each non-zero element as a triple `(row, col, val)`. Row 0 is the **header** containing `(rows, cols, N_{nz})`.
- ✅ Total tuple size = $(N_{nz} + 1) \times 3$ entries.
- ✅ **Fast Transpose** uses `row_terms[]` and `starting_pos[]` arrays. Time complexity = $O(N_{nz} + n)$.
- ✅ **Naive Transpose** just swaps coordinates; result needs sorting. Time = $O(N_{nz} \cdot n)$.
- ✅ Polynomial addition in sparse form = $O(m + n)$ by two-pointer technique.
- ✅ Sparse matrix addition in tuple form = $O(m + n)$ assuming row-major sorted input.
- ✅ Always mention **time complexity** in your answer — KTU awards 1 mark for it.
- ✅ In polynomial addition, when exponents match, **add the coefficients**; if the sum is zero, **do not** store that term.
- ✅ Header row in tuple form: **first entry** must be `rows`, then `cols`, then `non-zeros` (in that exact order).
- ✅ Tuple entries are conventionally stored in **row-major order** (sorted by row, then by column within each row).
- ✅ For $P(x) = 5x^{1000} + 1$, dense form wastes 999 zero slots; sparse form uses only 2 entries. **Always use sparse for high-degree sparse polynomials.**
- ✅ Real-world apps: **PageRank (Google)**, **FEA/CFD simulations**, **neural network weight matrices**, **recommendation systems**, **graph adjacency** — all use sparse matrix representations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stacks and Queues – Stacks, Multi-Stacks, Queues, Circular Queues, Double Ended Queues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stacks and Queues – Stacks, Multi-Stacks, Queues, Circular Queues, Double Ended Queues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Stack (LIFO Discipline)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Stack** is a linear, ordered data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** discipline — also called the **FILO (First-In, Last-Out)** discipline. All insertions (push) and deletions (pop) occur at a single designated end known as the **TOP** of the stack.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Spring-Loaded Plate Dispenser**

Imagine the plate dispenser you see in a college canteen. You can only:
1. **Add** a new plate on the **TOP** of the stack.
2. **Remove** a plate only from the **TOP**.

You cannot pull a plate out from the middle or bottom. The plate that was placed **LAST** is the one you will pick up **FIRST**. That is exactly how a stack works in memory.

**Key Terminology Box:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **TOP**: An index/pointer that identifies the current topmost element of the stack. Initially `TOP = -1` (array-based empty stack).
> - **Push**: The operation of inserting a new element onto the stack. *Time complexity: **O(1)***.
> - **Pop**: The operation of removing and returning the topmost element. *Time complexity: **O(1)***.
> - **Peek / Top-Element**: Non-destructive read of the element at TOP. *Time complexity: **O(1)***.
> - **Underflow**: Attempt to pop from an empty stack (error condition).
> - **Overflow**: Attempt to push onto a full stack (error condition).
> - **Stack Capacity (n)**: The maximum number of elements a stack can hold.

---

## 1.2 Multi-Stacks

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Multi-Stack** is a memory-management scheme in which **two or more stacks** share a single, common array (or contiguous memory block) of size **N**, in order to economize on storage. The available space is **partitioned** dynamically among the participating stacks.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Shared Office Bookshelf**

Suppose two students share one bookshelf of fixed length 100 cm. Student A places books from the **LEFT** end moving right, and Student B places books from the **RIGHT** end moving left. They share the middle. The moment one runs out of free shelves, they cannot push — even if the other has space, unless we adopt a more clever scheme.

---

## 1.3 The Queue (FIFO Discipline)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Queue** is a linear, ordered data structure that follows the **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** discipline. Insertions occur at the **REAR** (tail) and deletions occur at the **FRONT** (head).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Movie Ticket Counter Line**

People join a queue at the **tail (REAR)**. The person at the **head (FRONT)** is served and leaves first. The order of arrival determines the order of service — exactly the FIFO principle.

---

## 1.4 The Circular Queue

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Circular Queue** is a variation of the linear queue in which the **REAR** index wraps around to index **0** after reaching the maximum index `N - 1`, via modular arithmetic. This eliminates the **"false overflow"** problem of a linear queue.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Roundabout Traffic Circle**

Cars entering a roundabout (rear) keep circulating, and cars exit (front) at their desired exit. There is no "end of the road" — vehicles can keep flowing in a loop, which is exactly how a circular queue reuses freed cells.

---

## 1.5 Double-Ended Queue (Deque)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Deque (Double-Ended Queue)** is a generalized queue that permits **insertion and deletion at BOTH ends** — the FRONT and the REAR. It is the most flexible of the linear data structures in this module.

**Two Restricted Variants (Frequently Asked in KTU):**

| Variant | Insert Front | Insert Rear | Delete Front | Delete Rear |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Input-Restricted Deque** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Output-Restricted Deque** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |

**Conceptual Analogy — The Double-Sided Loading Bay**

A logistics bay where trucks can be loaded or unloaded from **either end**. Maximum flexibility — every door can act as an entry or exit depending on operational needs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stack — Operational Decomposition

A stack supports exactly **two mutating operations**: `PUSH` and `POP`, plus utility predicates `isEmpty()` and `isFull()`.

### 2.1.1 Array-Based Stack Representation

For a stack of capacity `MAX`:

```
Data : arr[0 ... MAX - 1]   // contiguous memory
State: TOP                   // integer index of topmost element
Empty:  TOP = -1
Full:   TOP = MAX - 1
```

### 2.1.2 Algorithmic Steps for PUSH(item)

1. Check for **Overflow**: If `TOP = MAX - 1`, report overflow and halt.
2. Increment TOP by 1: `TOP = TOP + 1`.
3. Store the item: `arr[TOP] = item`.
4. Report success.

### 2.1.3 Algorithmic Steps for POP()

1. Check for **Underflow**: If `TOP = -1`, report underflow and halt.
2. Read the top: `item = arr[TOP]`.
3. Decrement TOP: `TOP = TOP - 1`.
4. Return the item.

> [!TIP]
> **Why O(1)?** Every operation only manipulates the `TOP` index — no loops, no shifting, no traversal. This is what makes the stack the most efficient data structure for recursive simulations.

---

## 2.2 Multi-Stack — Partitioning Strategies

### 2.2.1 Equal-Sized Partitioning (Two Stacks)

When two stacks share an array of size `N`:
- **Stack 1** grows from **left → right** (`TOP1` starts at `-1`).
- **Stack 2** grows from **right → left** (`TOP2` starts at `N`).

**Overflow condition for the entire multi-stack:** `TOP1 + 1 = TOP2`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This scheme is **asymmetric** in practice. A push-heavy workload on one stack can starve the other. KTU expects you to recognize this trade-off.

### 2.2.2 Generalized m-Stacks in Array of Size N

For `m` stacks sharing a single array:

- **Stack `i`** is allowed the boundaries `[lᵢ ... rᵢ]`.
- For sequential (non-overlapping) allocation: each stack gets `N/m` cells.
- For dynamic (growing) allocation: each stack may grow into a "memory pool" shared among all stacks. The condition `TOP₁ + 1 = TOP₂ = ... = TOPₘ` indicates total overflow.

### 2.2.3 KTU High-Yield Multi-Stack Operations Table

| Operation | Index Update | Check First |
|---|---|---|
| PUSH to Stack i | `TOP[i] = TOP[i] + 1; arr[TOP[i]] = item` | `TOP[i] < boundary(i)` |
| POP from Stack i | `item = arr[TOP[i]]; TOP[i] = TOP[i] - 1` | `TOP[i] >= lowerBound(i)` |

---

## 2.3 Queue — Operational Decomposition

For a linear queue of capacity `MAX`:

```
Data : arr[0 ... MAX - 1]
State: FRONT, REAR
Empty:  FRONT = -1  AND  REAR = -1   (some texts use FRONT = REAR = 0)
Full:   REAR = MAX - 1
```

### 2.3.1 Algorithmic Steps for ENQUEUE(item)

1. If `REAR = MAX - 1`: report **Queue Full (Overflow)**.
2. If `FRONT = -1`: set `FRONT = 0`.
3. Increment `REAR = REAR + 1`.
4. Set `arr[REAR] = item`.

### 2.3.2 Algorithmic Steps for DEQUEUE()

1. If `FRONT = -1` or `FRONT > REAR`: report **Queue Empty (Underflow)**.
2. Read `item = arr[FRONT]`.
3. Increment `FRONT = FRONT + 1`.
4. If `FRONT > REAR`: reset both to `-1` (queue is now empty).
5. Return `item.

> [!WARNING]
> **The "False Overflow" Problem:** Once items are dequeued, the cells at indices `< FRONT` become **permanently wasted** in a linear queue. After several cycles, `REAR` will hit `MAX - 1` and report overflow even though the array has free cells. This is precisely the motivation for the **Circular Queue**.

---

## 2.4 Circular Queue — The Modular Solution

The circular queue eliminates false overflow by allowing `REAR` and `FRONT` to **wrap around** using modular arithmetic.

**Position Update Rule (Universal):**

$$ \text{next\_index} = (\text{current\_index} + 1) \bmod N $$

where `N` is the queue capacity.

### 2.4.1 ENQUEUE (Circular Version)

1. Compute `next_rear = (REAR + 1) mod N`.
2. If `next_rear = FRONT`: report **Queue Full**.
3. Set `REAR = next_rear`.
4. Set `arr[REAR] = item`.
5. If `FRONT = -1` (first insertion): set `FRONT = 0`.

### 2.4.2 DEQUEUE (Circular Version)

1. If `FRONT = -1`: report **Queue Empty**.
2. Read `item = arr[FRONT]`.
3. If `FRONT = REAR` (last element): set `FRONT = REAR = -1` (queue becomes empty).
4. Otherwise: `FRONT = (FRONT + 1) mod N`.
5. Return `item`.

### 2.4.3 Why One Slot is Wasted (The Textbook Trade-off)

To distinguish *empty* (`FRONT = -1`) from *full* (`FRONT = (REAR+1) mod N`), we **sacrifice one slot**. The usable capacity is `N - 1` in an array of size `N`. (An alternative is to maintain a `count` variable to avoid wasting a slot.)

---

## 2.5 Deque — Full-Flexibility Container

A deque supports **four** mutating operations:
- `insertFront(x)`, `insertRear(x)`
- `deleteFront()`, `deleteRear()`

And two observers: `getFront()`, `getRear()`.

### 2.5.1 Circular Array Implementation of Deque

For a deque of capacity `N`:

$$ \text{Front update (left wrap)}: \quad F = (F - 1 + N) \bmod N $$

$$ \text{Rear update (right wrap)}: \quad R = (R + 1) \bmod N $$

**Full condition:** `F = (R + 1) mod N`
**Empty condition:** `F = -1` (or alternatively a dedicated flag).

---

## 2.6 KTU Master Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Time Complexity | Space |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Push** | `TOP = TOP + 1; arr[TOP] = item` | **O(1)** | — |
| **Pop** | `x = arr[TOP]; TOP = TOP - 1` | **O(1)** | — |
| **Stack Empty** | `TOP = -1` | O(1) | — |
| **Stack Full** | `TOP = MAX - 1` | O(1) | — |
| **Multi-Stack Overflow** | $\text{TOP}_1 + 1 = \text{TOP}_2$ (two-stack case) | O(1) | — |
| **Enqueue (Linear)** | `REAR = REAR + 1; arr[REAR] = item` | O(1) | — |
| **Dequeue (Linear)** | `x = arr[FRONT]; FRONT = FRONT + 1` | O(1) | — |
| **Circular next-index** | $(i + 1) \bmod N$ | O(1) | — |
| **Deque left-wrap** | $(i - 1 + N) \bmod N$ | O(1) | — |
| **Usable circular capacity** | $N - 1$ (one slot sacrificed) | — | $N - 1$ |
| **Stack via LL** | Insert/delete at head | O(1) | O(n) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:**
> "**Stack → One End** | **Queue → Two Ends, One Direction** | **Circular → Two Ends, Wrapping** | **Deque → Two Ends, Both Directions**"

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Data Structure | Real-World Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Stack** | Recursion call stack, expression evaluation, browser back-button, undo in editors, parenthesis matching, DFS |
| **Multi-Stack** | Register allocation in compilers, memory sharing in embedded systems, co-routine stacks |
| **Linear Queue** | Disk scheduling (FCFS), printer spooler, CPU scheduling (basic) |
| **Circular Queue** | Round-robin CPU scheduler, memory-mapped buffers, traffic light cycles, streaming data |
| **Deque** | A-Steal work scheduling, sliding window maximum, undo-redo with bidirectional history, palindrome check |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Proof: Why the Linear Queue Suffers from "False Overflow"

Let a linear queue of capacity `N = 5` exist. Trace the operations to demonstrate the flaw.

**Initial state:** `FRONT = -1`, `REAR = -1`, `arr = [_, _, _, _, _]`

**Step 1:** Enqueue A → `FRONT = 0`, `REAR = 0`
**Step 2:** Enqueue B → `REAR = 1`
**Step 3:** Enqueue C → `REAR = 2`

Array state: `[A, B, C, _, _]`, `FRONT = 0`, `REAR = 2`

**Step 4:** Dequeue → returns A. `FRONT = 1`. Array: `[A, B, C, _, _]`
**Step 5:** Dequeue → returns B. `FRONT = 2`. Array: `[A, B, C, _, _]`
**Step 6:** Dequeue → returns C. `FRONT = 3`, `REAR = 2`.

After Step 6, since `FRONT > REAR`, we reset both to `-1`. **Three cells are wasted forever in this naive approach (if we don't reset, the cells at indices 0, 1, 2 are stranded).**

**Step 7:** Enqueue D, E, F, G, H. Sequence:
- Enqueue D: `REAR = 0`, `FRONT = 0` → `arr[0] = D`
- Enqueue E: `REAR = 1` → `arr[1] = E`
- ...
- Enqueue H: `REAR = 4` → `arr[4] = H`

**Step 8:** Try to enqueue I. Check: `REAR = MAX - 1 = 4`. **Queue is reported as FULL.**

But the array is full only because we have no wrap-around — there are zero empty cells. *In this case, the overflow is genuine.* Now consider a more subtle scenario:

**Step 9:** Suppose we do NOT reset `FRONT` after dequeue (some implementations preserve `FRONT`). Dequeue twice. `FRONT` becomes `4`. Enqueue X: `REAR = 5 = MAX`. **Overflow reported, but `arr[0], arr[1]` are completely free.**

This is **false overflow**, and the circular queue cures it.

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation of Circular Index Wrapping

**Claim:** For a queue of capacity `N` and current index `i`, the next index after a forward step is:

$$ i_{\text{next}} = (i + 1) \bmod N $$

**Proof by Enumeration (for N = 5):**

| Current $i$ | $i + 1$ | $(i + 1) \bmod 5$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | **1** |
| 1 | 2 | **2** |
| 2 | 3 | **3** |
| 3 | 4 | **4** |
| 4 | 5 | **0** (wrap!) |

**Generalization:** For any $i \in \mathbb{Z}$ and modulus $N > 0$, the modulo operation yields a result in the range $[0, N - 1]$. Therefore repeated forward stepping produces a sequence that cycles through all valid indices, never exceeding the array boundary. ∎

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** When asked to "show that circular queues eliminate false overflow", trace at least 6–8 enqueue/dequeue operations on a small N (typically 5 or 6) and show that cells freed at the front are **reused** by subsequent enqueues. Examiners reward this explicit trace.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Trace: Circular Queue (N = 5)

| Operation | FRONT | REAR | Array State (size 5) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | -1 | -1 | `[_, _, _, _, _]` | Empty |
| Enqueue 10 | 0 | 0 | `[10, _, _, _, _]` | OK |
| Enqueue 20 | 0 | 1 | `[10, 20, _, _, _]` | OK |
| Enqueue 30 | 0 | 2 | `[10, 20, 30, _, _]` | OK |
| Dequeue | 1 | 2 | `[10, 20, 30, _, _]` | Returned 10 |
| Dequeue | 2 | 2 | `[10, 20, 30, _, _]` | Returned 20 |
| Enqueue 40 | 2 | 3 | `[10, 20, 30, 40, _]` | OK |
| Enqueue 50 | 2 | 4 | `[10, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | OK |
| Enqueue 60 | 2 | 0 | `[60, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | OK (wrap!) |
| Enqueue 70 | 2 | 1 | `[60, 70, 30, 40, 50]` | OK |
| Enqueue 80 | 2 | 3 → **FRONT** | `[60, 70, 80, 40, 50]` | **FULL** — overflow reported |

Note how the **wraparound occurs at Enqueue 60** where REAR moves from 4 to 0.

---

## 3.4 Deque: Bidirectional Wraparound Derivation

**Forward (right) wrap:** $\quad R = (R + 1) \bmod N$

**Backward (left) wrap:** $\quad F = (F - 1 + N) \bmod N$

**Why `+ N` inside the modulo?**

Consider $F = 0$ and we want to wrap left. Naively, $F - 1 = -1$. But $-1 \bmod N$ in C/Java gives a negative result! By adding `+ N` first, we get $(-1 + N) \bmod N = (N - 1) \bmod N = N - 1$. The correct wraparound index.

**Proof for the left-wrap formula:**

$$ F_{\text{prev}} = (F_{\text{curr}} - 1 + N) \bmod N $$

For $N = 5$, $F_{\text{curr}} = 0$:
$$ F_{\text{prev}} = (0 - 1 + 5) \bmod 5 = 4 \bmod 5 = 4 \;\checkmark $$

For $N = 5$, $F_{\text{curr}} = 3$:
$$ F_{\text{prev}} = (3 - 1 + 5) \bmod 5 = 7 \bmod 5 = 2 \;\checkmark $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting the `+ N` is the #1 reason students lose marks on deque trace questions. The formula will silently produce negative indices otherwise.

---

## 3.5 Multi-Stack — Overflow Derivation (Two-Stack Case)

Given an array of size $N = 10$, Stack 1 grows from left (`TOP1`), Stack 2 grows from right (`TOP2`).

**Initial state:** `TOP1 = -1`, `TOP2 = 10`

**Push 1 to Stack 1:** `TOP1 = 0`. Free gap = `TOP2 - TOP1 - 1 = 10 - 0 - 1 = 9`.  
**Push 1 to Stack 2:** `TOP2 = 9`. Free gap = `9 - 0 - 1 = 8`.  
**... continues ...**  
**Push 5 to Stack 1:** `TOP1 = 4`. Free gap = `9 - 4 - 1 = 4`.  
**Push 5 to Stack 2:** `TOP2 = 5`. Free gap = `5 - 4 - 1 = 0`. **OVERFLOW** — both stacks now adjacent.

**Generalized Overflow Condition:**

$$ \text{TOP}_1 + 1 = \text{TOP}_2 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{No free cell} $$

Equivalently:

$$ \text{Free cells} = \text{TOP}_2 - \text{TOP}_1 - 1 $$

When this equals `0`, total overflow occurs.

---

## 3.6 Time Complexity Summary Matrix

| Operation | Stack (Array) | Stack (Linked List) | Linear Queue | Circular Queue | Deque |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Insert | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) |
| Delete | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) |
| Peek/Front | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) |
| Search | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) |
| Space (n elements) | O(N) preallocated | O(n) exact | O(N) preallocated | O(N) preallocated | O(N) preallocated |

> [!NOTE]
> All four structures deliver **O(1)** for their primary operations. The "advantage" of the linked-list stack is that it does **not** suffer overflow (other than running out of heap memory), at the cost of one extra pointer per element.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Stack Module (Array Implementation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Stack Module] --> B[Data Array arr 0 to MAX minus 1]
    A --> C[Top Index Variable]
    A --> D[Operations Layer]
    D --> D1[Push item]
    D --> D2[Pop returns item]
    D --> D3[Peek returns TOP element]
    D --> D4[isEmpty returns boolean]
    D --> D5[isFull returns boolean]
    D1 --> E1[Step 1 Check isFull]
    D1 --> E2[Step 2 Increment TOP]
    D1 --> E3[Step 3 arr TOP equals item]
    D2 --> F1[Step 1 Check isEmpty]
    D2 --> F2[Step 2 Read arr TOP]
    D2 --> F3[Step 3 Decrement TOP]
    C --> G1[Init TOP equals minus 1]
    C --> G2[Overflow when TOP equals MAX minus 1]
    C --> G3[Underflow when TOP equals minus 1]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Multi-Stack (Two Stacks in One Array)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Array of Size N] --> B[Indices 0 to N over 2 minus 1]
    A --> C[Indices N over 2 to N minus 1]
    B --> D[Stack 1 grows RIGHT]
    C --> E[Stack 2 grows LEFT]
    D --> F[TOP1 starts at minus 1]
    E --> G[TOP2 starts at N]
    F --> H[Push moves TOP1 up by 1]
    G --> I[Push moves TOP2 down by 1]
    H --> J{Overflow if TOP1 plus 1 equals TOP2}
    I --> J
    J -->|Yes| K[Report Multi Stack Overflow]
    J -->|No| L[Insertion Permitted]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Circular Queue Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Queue Init]) --> S1[FRONT equals minus 1, REAR equals minus 1]
    S1 --> Op1{User Operation}
    Op1 -->|Enqueue X| E1[Compute next equals REAR plus 1 mod N]
    E1 --> E2{next equals FRONT}
    E2 -->|Yes| E3[Report Queue Full]
    E2 -->|No| E4[REAR equals next]
    E4 --> E5{FRONT equals minus 1}
    E5 -->|Yes| E6[FRONT equals 0]
    E5 -->|No| E7[Done]
    E6 --> E7
    Op1 -->|Dequeue| D1{FRONT equals minus 1}
    D1 -->|Yes| D2[Report Queue Empty]
    D1 -->|No| D3[Read item from arr FRONT]
    D3 --> D4{FRONT equals REAR}
    D4 -->|Yes| D5[Reset both to minus 1]
    D4 -->|No| D6[FRONT equals FRONT plus 1 mod N]
    D5 --> D7[Done]
    D6 --> D7
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Deque Insertion & Deletion at Both Ends

```mermaid
graph LR
    D[Deque Container] --> L[Left End FRONT]
    D --> R[Right End REAR]
    L --> L1[insertFront x]
    L --> L2[deleteFront returns x]
    R --> R1[insertRear x]
    R --> R2[deleteRear returns x]
    L1 --> W1[F equals F minus 1 plus N mod N]
    R1 --> W2[R equals R plus 1 mod N]
    L2 --> W3[Read arr F, advance F via mod]
    R2 --> W4[Read arr R, retreat R via mod]
    W1 --> C{F equals R plus 1 mod N}
    W2 --> C
    C -->|Yes| X[Report Full]
    C -->|No| Y[Insertion Permitted]
    W3 --> Z{F equals minus 1}
    W4 --> Z
    Z -->|Yes| X2[Report Empty]
    Z -->|No| Y2[Deletion Permitted]
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Flow — Push/Pop Visualization (Stack)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PUSH
    P1[Empty Stack TOP equals minus 1] --> P2[Push A TOP becomes 0]
    P2 --> P3[Push B TOP becomes 1]
    P3 --> P4[Push C TOP becomes 2]
    end
    subgraph POP
    P4 --> Q1[Pop returns C TOP becomes 1]
    Q1 --> Q2[Pop returns B TOP becomes 0]
    Q2 --> Q3[Pop returns A TOP becomes minus 1 Empty]
    end
```

## 4.6 Circular Queue Wraparound Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Index 0] --> B[Index 1]
    B --> C[Index 2]
    C --> D[Index 3]
    D --> E[Index 4]
    E -->|next equals 0 mod 5| A
    A -.->|FRONT points here| F1
    E -.->|REAR points here| R1
    F1[FRONT equals 2]
    R1[REAR equals 4]
    F1 --> G1[10 at index 2]
    F1 --> G2[20 at index 3]
    R1 --> G3[30 at index 4]
    A --> H1[Next insert goes to index 0 wrap]
    H1 --> H2[40 lands at index 0]
```
