# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between a stack and a queue. Give one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (Expected 3-mark depth):**

| Aspect | Stack | Queue |
|---|---|---|
| Discipline | LIFO (Last In, First Out) | FIFO (First In, First Out) |
| Insertion End | TOP only | REAR only |
| Deletion End | TOP only | FRONT only |
| Operations | Push / Pop | Enqueue / Dequeue |
| Example | Stack of plates in canteen / Browser back button | Movie ticket counter / Printer spooler |
| Underflow trigger | `TOP = -1` | `FRONT = -1` or `FRONT > REAR` |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Explain any two applications of a stack in computer science.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Expression Evaluation & Conversion:** Stacks convert infix expressions to postfix/prefix and evaluate them using the shunting-yard algorithm. The stack holds operators based on precedence.
2. **Function Call Management:** At runtime, every function call pushes an *activation record* (return address, local variables, parameters) onto the **call stack**. When the function returns, the record is popped, restoring the caller's state.
3. *(Bonus, if time permits)*: Parenthesis matching, depth-first search (DFS), backtracking algorithms, undo operations in text editors.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **circular queue**? Explain with a suitable diagram how it overcomes the limitations of a linear queue. Write the algorithms for the ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Simulate a circular queue of capacity **N = 5** for the following sequence of operations and show the FRONT, REAR, and array state after each step:

`ENQUEUE 10, ENQUEUE 20, ENQUEUE 30, DEQUEUE, DEQUEUE, ENQUEUE 40, ENQUEUE 50, ENQUEUE 60, ENQUEUE 70, ENQUEUE 80`

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition [1 mark]:** A circular queue is a linear data structure that uses a one-dimensional array of size $N$ in which the **REAR** index wraps around to `0` after reaching `N - 1`, via modular arithmetic: $R = (R + 1) \bmod N$. This ensures that cells freed by dequeued elements at the front are **reused** by future enqueues.

**Limitation of Linear Queue [2 marks]:**
- A linear queue of capacity `N` can hold at most `N` elements, but after several enqueue/dequeue cycles, even when free cells exist at lower indices, the queue reports **overflow** because `REAR = N - 1` while `FRONT > 0`.
- This is called **false overflow** — array space is wasted.
- Example: After 3 dequeues, indices 0, 1, 2 are free; we cannot enqueue into them.

**Circular Queue Diagram [1 mark]:**

A circular queue is conceptually a ring: indices $0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$ form a loop. FRONT and REAR move clockwise (forward) on ENQUEUE/DEQUEUE.

**ENQUEUE Algorithm [1.5 marks]:**
```
ENQUEUE(item):
    if (FRONT == -1):
        FRONT = 0
    next = (REAR + 1) % N
    if (next == FRONT):
        print "Queue Full"
        return
    REAR = next
    arr[REAR] = item
```

**DEQUEUE Algorithm [1.5 marks]:**
```
DEQUEUE():
    if (FRONT == -1):
        print "Queue Empty"
        return
    item = arr[FRONT]
    if (FRONT == REAR):
        FRONT = REAR = -1
    else:
        FRONT = (FRONT + 1) % N
    return item
```

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks (Trace Table)

`N = 5`. Initial: `FRONT = -1`, `REAR = -1`, `arr = [_, _, _, _, _]`

| Step | Operation | Computation | FRONT | REAR | Array State (Index 0..4) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ENQUEUE 10 | FRONT=0, REAR=0 | 0 | 0 | `[10, _, _, _, _]` | OK |
| 2 | ENQUEUE 20 | REAR=1 | 0 | 1 | `[10, 20, _, _, _]` | OK |
| 3 | ENQUEUE 30 | REAR=2 | 0 | 2 | `[10, 20, 30, _, _]` | OK |
| 4 | DEQUEUE | Return 10; FRONT=1 | 1 | 2 | `[10, 20, 30, _, _]` | OK |
| 5 | DEQUEUE | Return 20; FRONT=2 | 2 | 2 | `[10, 20, 30, _, _]` | OK |
| 6 | ENQUEUE 40 | REAR=3 | 2 | 3 | `[10, 20, 30, 40, _]` | OK |
| 7 | ENQUEUE 50 | REAR=4 | 2 | 4 | `[10, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | OK |
| 8 | ENQUEUE 60 | next=(4+1)%5=0; 0≠2; REAR=0 | 2 | 0 | `[60, 20, 30, 40, 50]` | **WRAP!** |
| 9 | ENQUEUE 70 | next=(0+1)%5=1; 1≠2; REAR=1 | 2 | 1 | `[60, 70, 30, 40, 50]` | OK |
| 10 | ENQUEUE 80 | next=(1+1)%5=2; **2 == FRONT** | 2 | 1 | `[60, 70, 30, 40, 50]` | **FULL — Overflow** |

**Final State:** Queue contains `[30, 40, 50, 60, 70]` (in order of logical position: 30 at FRONT, 70 at REAR). ENQUEUE 80 fails with overflow.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a **Double-Ended Queue (Deque)**. With neat diagrams, explain the **input-restricted** and **output-restricted** variants. Write the algorithms for inserting and deleting an element at both ends.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is a **multi-stack**? Explain the **two-stack sharing one array** scheme. A single array of size `N = 12` is shared by two stacks. Stack 1 grows from the left end and Stack 2 grows from the right end. Simulate the following operations and report the state after each:

`PUSH 1 to S1, PUSH 2 to S1, PUSH 3 to S1, PUSH A to S2, PUSH B to S2, POP from S1, PUSH 4 to S1, PUSH C to S2, PUSH D to S2, PUSH 5 to S1, PUSH E to S2`

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition [1.5 marks]:** A **Deque (Double-Ended Queue)** is a generalized queue in which insertion and deletion can be performed at **both** the FRONT and the REAR ends. It is the most flexible linear data structure, supporting four mutating operations: `insertFront`, `insertRear`, `deleteFront`, `deleteRear`.

**Variants [2 marks]:**

- **Input-Restricted Deque:** Insertion is allowed only at the REAR; deletion is allowed at both ends.
- **Output-Restricted Deque:** Deletion is allowed only at the FRONT; insertion is allowed at both ends.

**Algorithms [3.5 marks]:**

```
// Using circular array of size N
insertFront(item):
    if (FRONT == -1):                       // empty case
        FRONT = REAR = 0
    else:
        FRONT = (FRONT - 1 + N) % N
    if (FRONT == (REAR + 1) % N):
        print "Deque Full"
        FRONT = (FRONT + 1) % N             // rollback
        return
    arr[FRONT] = item

insertRear(item):
    REAR = (REAR + 1) % N
    if (FRONT == REAR):
        print "Deque Full"
        REAR = (REAR - 1 + N) % N           // rollback
        return
    arr[REAR] = item

deleteFront():
    if (FRONT == -1):
        print "Deque Empty"
        return
    item = arr[FRONT]
    if (FRONT == REAR):
        FRONT = REAR = -1
    else:
        FRONT = (FRONT + 1) % N
    return item

deleteRear():
    if (FRONT == -1):
        print "Deque Empty"
        return
    item = arr[REAR]
    if (FRONT == REAR):
        FRONT = REAR = -1
    else:
        REAR = (REAR - 1 + N) % N
    return item
```

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks (Multi-Stack Trace)

`N = 12`. Initial: `TOP1 = -1`, `TOP2 = 12`, `arr = [_, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _]`

| Step | Operation | TOP1 | TOP2 | Array Snapshot (S1 grows left, S2 grows right) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Init | -1 | 12 | `[_, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _]` | Empty |
| 1 | PUSH 1 to S1 | 0 | 12 | `[1, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _]` | S1[0]=1 |
| 2 | PUSH 2 to S1 | 1 | 12 | `[1, 2, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _]` | S1[1]=2 |
| 3 | PUSH 3 to S1 | 2 | 12 | `[1, 2, 3, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _]` | S1[2]=3 |
| 4 | PUSH A to S2 | 2 | 11 | `[1, 2, 3, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, A]` | S2[11]=A |
| 5 | PUSH B to S2 | 2 | 10 | `[1, 2, 3, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, B, A]` | S2[10]=B |
| 6 | POP from S1 | 1 | 10 | `[1, 2, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, B, A]` | Returned 3 |
| 7 | PUSH 4 to S1 | 2 | 10 | `[1, 2, 4, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, B, A]` | S1[2]=4 |
| 8 | PUSH C to S2 | 2 | 9 | `[1, 2, 4, _, _, _, _, _, _, C, B, A]` | S2[9]=C |
| 9 | PUSH D to S2 | 2 | 8 | `[1, 2, 4, _, _, _, _, _, D, C, B, A]` | S2[8]=D |
| 10 | PUSH 5 to S1 | 3 | 8 | `[1, 2, 4, 5, _, _, _, _, D, C, B, A]` | S1[3]=5 |
| 11 | PUSH E to S2 | 3 | 7 | `[1, 2, 4, 5, _, _, _, E, D, C, B, A]` | S2[7]=E |

**Final State after Step 11:**
- Stack 1 (logical top to bottom): `5 → 4 → 2 → 1` (top is 5 at index 3)
- Stack 2 (logical top to bottom): `E → D → C → B → A` (top is E at index 7)
- **Gap between stacks:** `TOP2 - TOP1 - 1 = 7 - 3 - 1 = 3` free cells (indices 4, 5, 6)
- The next push to S1 (at index 4) or S2 (at index 6) is still possible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the circular queue "Full" condition with the "Empty" condition** — Both are checked by the same modular expression but at different times. If your algorithm doesn't track them separately, you'll get **-2 marks** minimum.
> 2. **Forgetting the `+ N` in the deque left-wrap formula** — `(F - 1) mod N` will produce `-1` in C, not `N - 1`. Always write `(F - 1 + N) mod N`. This is a **favorite trap question**.
> 3. **In multi-stack traces, students often forget to check the overflow condition** before pushing. The condition is `TOP1 + 1 == TOP2`. If you skip this check, your algorithm is incomplete and you lose **-1 to -2 marks**.
> 4. **Resetting FRONT and REAR to 0 vs -1** — Be consistent. KTU prefers `FRONT = -1` for empty, with special handling on the first insertion. If you mix conventions, you will lose **-1 mark**.
> 5. **Not showing the WRAP step explicitly** in trace tables — Examiners award marks for the moment `REAR` moves from `N - 1` to `0`. Always write it out, e.g., `REAR = (4+1) % 5 = 0`.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Stack** is **LIFO**; primary operations are **PUSH** and **POP** — both **O(1)**.
- ✅ Stack empty when `TOP = -1`; full when `TOP = MAX - 1`.
- ✅ A **Queue** is **FIFO**; primary operations are **ENQUEUE** and **DEQUEUE** — both **O(1)**.
- ✅ Linear queue suffers from **false overflow** because freed cells at the front are never reused.
- ✅ A **Circular Queue** uses modular arithmetic: `next = (i + 1) % N` to wrap the REAR back to `0`.
- ✅ Circular queue **sacrifices one slot** to distinguish empty vs full — usable capacity is `N - 1`.
- ✅ Empty circular queue: `FRONT = -1`; Full: `next_rear == FRONT`.
- ✅ A **Deque** allows insertion **and** deletion at **both** ends — four mutating operations.
- ✅ Deque left-wrap formula: `F = (F - 1 + N) % N` (the `+ N` is **mandatory**).
- ✅ **Input-Restricted Deque**: insert only at rear; delete at both ends.
- ✅ **Output-Restricted Deque**: delete only at front; insert at both ends.
- ✅ A **Multi-Stack** shares one array between multiple stacks — saves memory, but needs careful boundary management.
- ✅ Two-stack sharing: S1 grows left→right, S2 grows right→left; overflow when `TOP1 + 1 == TOP2`.
- ✅ Free space in two-stack sharing: `TOP2 - TOP1 - 1`.
- ✅ All four primary operations (push, pop, enqueue, dequeue, deque insert/delete) are **O(1)** in time.
- ✅ Linked-list implementations of stack/queue avoid overflow at the cost of one pointer per node.
- ✅ Real-world apps: **Stack** → recursion, DFS, undo; **Queue** → BFS, FCFS scheduling; **Circular Queue** → round-robin; **Deque** → A-steal scheduling, sliding windows.
- ✅ KTU exam mantra: **"Always trace, always modular-arithmetic, always check empty/full first."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Expressions – Infix to Postfix, Evaluating Postfix Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Evaluation of Expressions – Infix to Postfix, Evaluating Postfix Expressions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Data Structures and Algorithms**, an *expression* is a syntactically valid combination of **operands**, **operators**, and **parentheses** that evaluates to a single value. Expressions can be represented in three principal notations: | Notation | Position of Operator | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Infix** | Between two operands | `A + B` | | **Prefix** (Polish Notation) | Before two operands | `+ A B` | | **Postfix** (Reverse Polish Notation – RPN) | After two operands | `A B +` | While humans naturally write and read expressions in **infix** form (because it is intuitive), computers evaluate them more efficiently in **postfix** form because postfix expressions require **no parentheses** and obey a strict, unambiguous evaluation order determined solely by a linear left-to-right scan. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCST303 / Module 1):** Students must master the conversion from **Infix → Postfix** using a **stack**, and the **evaluation of Postfix expressions** using a **stack**, including proficiency with operator precedence, associativity, and parenthesization rules. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Shelf and Plate" Intuition Imagine a busy kitchen counter where a chef prepares sandwiches: * **The Plates (Stack):** The chef has a single stack of plates. He can only add a plate to the **top** (push) or take a plate from the **top** (pop). He cannot grab a plate from the middle — this is the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** principle. * **The Ingredients (Operands):** Tomatoes, cheese, and bread slices are the *data* — they must be remembered for later use, so they are placed on a side tray. * **The Actions (Operators):** Cutting, toasting, and assembling are *operations*. They need two ingredients to act upon. * **The Recipe (Infix Expression):** `(Tomato + Cheese) * Bread` is read left-to-right. * **The Simplified Order (Postfix):** `Tomato Cheese + Bread *` — every action (`+`, `*`) is written *only after* the two ingredients it needs are ready. The chef never needs to remember precedence or look back. This shelf-based mechanism is exactly how a computer uses a **stack** to evaluate expressions. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants * **Stack Operations:** `push(x)`, `pop()`, `peek()/top()`, `isEmpty()`. * **Time Complexity:** Both Infix→Postfix conversion and Postfix evaluation are **O(n)**, where **n** is the number of symbols in the expression (each symbol is pushed and popped at most once). * **Space Complexity:** **O(n)** in the worst case (when the entire expression consists of operators/parentheses only). > [!NOTE] > **Definition – Stack:** A linear data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** principle, supporting insertion (`push`) and deletion (`pop`) from one end only, called the **top** of the stack. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operator Precedence and Associativity Before any conversion can be performed, you must internalize the **precedence hierarchy** (higher number = higher priority = evaluated first) and the **associativity rule** (decides order when operators have equal priority). ### 2.1.1 KTU-Standard Precedence Table | Priority | Operator(s) | Description | Associativity | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **3 (Highest)** | `^` | Exponentiation | **Right-to-Left** | | **2** | `*`, `/`, `%` | Multiplicative | **Left-to-Right** | | **1 (Lowest)** | `+`, `-` | Additive | **Left-to-Right** | | **—** | `(`, `)` | Parentheses (grouping) | N/A | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Right Associativity Trap:** `2 ^ 3 ^ 2 = 2 ^ (3 ^ 2) = 2 ^ 9 = 512`. A common KTU mistake is to evaluate it as `(2 ^ 3) ^ 2 = 8 ^ 2 = 64`. ### 2.1.2 Rules of Engagement * **Rule 1 — Parentheses:** Anything inside `(` and `)` is evaluated as a self-contained sub-expression first. * **Rule 2 — Higher Precedence Wins:** `*` is performed before `+` in `2 + 3 * 4` → gives `14`, not `20`. * **Rule 3 — Associativity Breaks Ties:** When two operators of equal precedence sit side by side, associativity decides who acts first. * **Rule 4 — Left Operand is LHS:** For a binary operator `op`, the operand on the **left** was pushed **before** the operand on the **right**. ## 2.2 The Infix → Postfix Conversion Algorithm (Shunting-Yard by Dijkstra) This is the **core algorithm** tested in KTU. It uses one **operator stack** and one **output queue** (or just an output string). ### 2.2.1 Logical Steps 1. **Initialize:** Create an empty stack `S` for operators and an empty list/string `Q` for output. 2. **Scan** the infix expression **left to right**, one symbol at a time. 3. **Case A — Operand (a–z, A–Z, 0–9):** Append it directly to `Q`. 4. **Case B — Left Parenthesis `(`:** Push it onto `S`. It acts as a *sentinel* — a marker for sub-expression boundaries. 5. **Case C — Right Parenthesis `)`:** Repeatedly pop from `S` and append to `Q` **until the matching `(` is popped and discarded**. *No `(` or `)` ever appears in the output.* 6. **Case D — Operator `op` (e.g., `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `^`):** * While `S` is **not empty** AND the top of `S` is **not** `(` AND (the precedence of top of `S` is **greater than** `op` OR (precedence is **equal** AND `op` is **left-associative**)) → **pop** top of `S` and append to `Q`. * Push `op` onto `S`. 7. **End of Scan:** Pop and append every remaining operator from `S` to `Q`. ### 2.2.2 Why These Rules Work (The "Why" Behind the Code) * **Operands never wait:** They go straight to the output because they don't depend on any operator. * **`(` is a wall:** It protects operators inside a sub-expression from being popped by operators outside. * **Higher-precedence operators leave first:** They need their operands sooner, so they are emitted earlier. * **Right-associative `^` is special:** It stays on the stack so it can combine with the next `^` from the right. ## 2.3 The Postfix Evaluation Algorithm Once the expression is in postfix form, evaluation is **trivially stack-based** with **one operand stack only**. ### 2.3.1 Logical Steps 1. **Initialize:** Create an empty stack `S`. 2. **Scan** the postfix expression **left to right**. 3. **Case A — Operand:** Convert to its numerical value and **push** onto `S`. 4. **Case B — Operator `op`:** * Pop operand `op2` (this is the **RIGHT** operand). * Pop operand `op1` (this is the **LEFT** operand). * Compute `result = op1 op op2` (e.g., `op1 + op2`). * Push `result` back onto `S`. 5. **End of Scan:** The single value remaining on `S` is the **final answer**. Pop and return it. > [!CAUTION] > **Operand Pop Order Trap:** When evaluating `op1 op op2`, you must pop the **right operand first** and then the **left operand**. Reversing this is the #1 KTU valuation error. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Compilers & Interpreters:** Every language compiler (GCC, Clang, javac) uses these exact algorithms in its **parsing phase** to translate human-readable source code into a machine-friendly intermediate representation (three-address code / bytecode). * **Calculators:** HP scientific calculators and many embedded-system calculators accept direct RPN input because it eliminates the need for an `=` key and parenthesis keys. * **JVM Stack Frames:** The Java Virtual Machine is a stack-based execution engine that evaluates bytecode postfix instructions. ## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | Metric | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Time Complexity (Conversion)** | $T(n) = O(n)$ | Each symbol → ≤1 push + ≤1 pop | | **Time Complexity (Evaluation)** | $T(n) = O(n)$ | Each symbol → ≤1 push + ≤1 pop | | **Space Complexity (Worst Case)** | $S(n) = O(n)$ | All operators stacked (e.g., `((((A))))`) | | **Pop Condition for Operator `op`** | $\text{top} \neq ( \;\;\land\;\; (P(\text{top}) > P(op) \;\;\lor\;\; (P(\text{top}) = P(op) \land op \in \text{LeftAssoc}))$ | Boolean expression | | **Final Stack Size After Evaluation** | $\vert S \vert = 1$ | Exactly one value remains | | **Precedence of `^` vs `*`** | $P(\text{\^{}}) = 3 > P(\text{*}) = 2$ | Higher wins | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Infix to Postfix Conversion **Convert:** `A + B * C - D / (E + F) ^ G` **Operator Precedence Used:** * `^` → Priority 3, Right-Associative * `*`, `/` → Priority 2, Left-Associative * `+`, `-` → Priority 1, Left-Associative ### 3.1.1 Exhaustive Symbol-by-Symbol Trace We maintain: * **Stack (bottom → top)** — operators waiting to be emitted * **Output** — final postfix string being built | Step | Symbol Read | Action Taken | Stack (top → right) | Output | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `A` | Operand → add to output | *(empty)* | `A` | | 2 | `+` | Stack empty → push `+` | `+` | `A` | | 3 | `B` | Operand → add to output | `+` | `A B` | | 4 | `*` | Top `+` has P=1 < P(`*`)=2 → push `*` | `+ *` | `A B` | | 5 | `C` | Operand → add to output | `+ *` | `A B C` | | 6 | `-` | Top `*` has P=2 > P(`-`)=1 → **pop `*`**; new top `+` has P=1 = P(`-`)=1, `-` is left-assoc → **pop `+`**; stack empty → push `-` | `-` | `A B C * +` | | 7 | `D` | Operand → add to output | `-` | `A B C * + D` | | 8 | `/` | Top `-` has P=1 < P(`/`) = 2 → push `/` | `- /` | `A B C * + D` | | 9 | `(` | Left paren → push (acts as wall) | `- / (` | `A B C * + D` | | 10 | `E` | Operand → add to output | `- / (` | `A B C * + D E` | | 11 | `+` | Top `(` → stop popping, push `+` | `- / ( +` | `A B C * + D E` | | 12 | `F` | Operand → add to output | `- / ( +` | `A B C * + D E F` | | 13 | `)` | Pop until `(`: **pop `+`** | `- /` | `A B C * + D E F +` | | 14 | `^` | Top `/` has P=2 < P(`^`)=3 → push `^` | `- / ^` | `A B C * + D E F +` | | 15 | `G` | Operand → add to output | `- / ^` | `A B C * + D E F + G` | | 16 | END | Pop all: **pop `^`**, **pop `/`**, **pop `-`** | *(empty)* | `A B C * + D E F + G ^ / -` | ### 3.1.2 Final Result $$\text{Infix: } A + B \times C - D \,/\, (E + F) \text{ \^{} } G$$ $$\text{Postfix: } A \; B \; C \; * \; + \; D \; E \; F \; + \; G \; \text{\^{}} \; / \; -$$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Evaluating a Postfix Expression **Evaluate:** `6 2 3 + - 3 8 2 / + * 2 ^ 3 +` *(Assume each operand is a single-digit integer for clarity.)* ### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Trace We use a single operand stack. | Step | Symbol | Action | Stack (bottom → top) | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `6` | push 6 | `6` | | 2 | `2` | push 2 | `6, 2` | | 3 | `3` | push 3 | `6, 2, 3` | | 4 | `+` | pop 3, pop 2 → `2+3 = 5` → push 5 | `6, 5` | | 5 | `-` | pop 5, pop 6 → `6-5 = 1` → push 1 | `1` | | 6 | `3` | push 3 | `1, 3` | | 7 | `8` | push 8 | `1, 3, 8` | | 8 | `2` | push 2 | `1, 3, 8, 2` | | 9 | `/` | pop 2, pop 8 → `8/2 = 4` → push 4 | `1, 3, 4` | | 10 | `+` | pop 4, pop 3 → `3+4 = 7` → push 7 | `1, 7` | | 11 | `*` | pop 7, pop 1 → `1*7 = 7` → push 7 | `7` | | 12 | `2` | push 2 | `7, 2` | | 13 | `^` | pop 2, pop 7 → `7^2 = 49` → push 49 | `49` | | 14 | `3` | push 3 | `49, 3` | | 15 | `+` | pop 3, pop 49 → `49+3 = 52` → push 52 | `52` | ### 3.2.2 Final Result $$\text{Evaluation Result} = 52$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic at a Glance This matrix summarizes the **single most-tested decision rule** in KTU exams: *when should an operator be popped during conversion?* | Current Symbol | Top of Stack | Compare | Decision | Reasoning | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | `op` | `( ` | — | **Push** `op` | `(` is a protective wall | | `op` | `op_top` | $P(op) > P(op_{top})$ | **Push** `op` | Lower-priority ops wait | | `op` | `op_top` | $P(op) < P(op_{top})$ | **Pop** `op_top`, then re-compare | Higher-priority ops leave first | | `op` | `op_top` | $P(op) = P(op_{top})$, `op` left-assoc | **Pop** `op_top`, then re-compare | Left-assoc → left acts first | | `op` | `op_top` | $P(op) = P(op_{top})$, `op` right-assoc (`^`) | **Push** `op` | Right-assoc → right waits | | `)` | anything | — | **Pop** until `(` found | Sub-expression closure | | Operand | anything | — | **Output** directly | Operands never wait | > [!NOTE] > **Verification Trick:** After conversion, the number of operands and operators in the infix and postfix expressions must be **identical**. For Example 1: 7 operands (`A B C D E F G`) + 6 operators (`+ * - / + ^`) = **13 symbols** in both forms. ✅ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Infix → Postfix Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD inputA["INFIX EXPRESSION INPUT"] --> scannerA["LEFT-TO-RIGHT SYMBOL SCANNER"] scannerA --> classifierA{"SYMBOL CLASSIFIER"} classifierA -->|"Operand A-Z, 0-9"| outQueueA["OUTPUT QUEUE / STRING"] classifierA -->|"Left Parenthesis ("| stackPushA["PUSH ONTO OPERATOR STACK"] classifierA -->|"Right Parenthesis )"| popUntilA["POP STACK UNTIL ( IS FOUND"] classifierA -->|"Operator +, -, *, /, ^"| precCheckA{"PRECEDENCE AND ASSOCIATIVITY CHECK"} precCheckA -->|"Top has HIGHER or EQUAL priority (left-assoc)"| popEmitA["POP AND EMIT TO OUTPUT"] popEmitA --> precCheckA precCheckA -->|"Otherwise"| stackPushA popUntilA --> nextTokenA{"MORE TOKENS?"} stackPushA --> nextTokenA outQueueA --> nextTokenA nextTokenA -->|"Yes"| scannerA nextTokenA -->|"No"| drainA["DRAIN STACK: POP ALL TO OUTPUT"] drainA --> finalOutA["FINAL POSTFIX EXPRESSION"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Postfix Evaluator ```mermaid flowchart TD inputB["POSTFIX EXPRESSION INPUT"] --> scannerB["LEFT-TO-RIGHT SYMBOL SCANNER"] scannerB --> classifierB{"SYMBOL CLASSIFIER"} classifierB -->|"Operand"| pushOpB["PUSH VALUE ONTO OPERAND STACK"] classifierB -->|"Operator op"| popOp2B["POP op2 (RIGHT OPERAND)"] popOp2B --> popOp1B["POP op1 (LEFT OPERAND)"] popOp1B --> computeB["COMPUTE: result = op1 op op2"] computeB --> pushResB["PUSH result ONTO STACK"] pushResB --> nextTokenB{"MORE TOKENS?"} pushOpB --> nextTokenB nextTokenB -->|"Yes"| scannerB nextTokenB -->|"No"| finalPopB["POP FINAL VALUE FROM STACK"] finalPopB --> finalOutB["EVALUATION RESULT"] ``` ## 4.3 Stack State Snapshot — Conversion of `A + B * C` ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1["After reading A"] direction LR op1["OPERATOR STACK"]:::empty --> out1["OUTPUT: A"]:::out end subgraph S2["After reading +"] direction LR op2["OPERATOR STACK: +"]:::top --> out2["OUTPUT: A"]:::out end subgraph S3["After reading B"] direction LR op3["OPERATOR STACK: +"]:::top --> out3["OUTPUT: A B"]:::out end subgraph S4["After reading *"] direction LR op4["OPERATOR STACK: + *"]:::top --> out4["OUTPUT: A B"]:::out end subgraph S5["After reading C"] direction LR op5["OPERATOR STACK: + *"]:::top --> out5["OUTPUT: A B C"]:::out end subgraph S6["After END: drain stack"] direction LR op6["OPERATOR STACK: empty"]:::empty --> out6["OUTPUT: A B C * +"]:::out end classDef top fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef empty fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#999,stroke-dasharray:5 5,color:#555 classDef out fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Annotated Stack Visualization — Evaluation of `5 3 2 + * 8 -` ```mermaid flowchart TD step1["READ 5
PUSH 5"]:::step --> stack1["STACK: 5"]:::stack step2["READ 3
PUSH 3"]:::step --> stack2["STACK: 5, 3"]:::stack step3["READ 2
PUSH 2"]:::step --> stack3["STACK: 5, 3, 2"]:::stack step4["READ +
POP 2, POP 3
3+2=5, PUSH 5"]:::op --> stack4["STACK: 5, 5"]:::stack step5["READ *
POP 5, POP 5
5*5=25, PUSH 25"]:::op --> stack5["STACK: 25"]:::stack step6["READ 8
PUSH 8"]:::step --> stack6["STACK: 25, 8"]:::stack step7["READ -
POP 8, POP 25
25-8=17, PUSH 17"]:::op --> stack7["STACK: 17"]:::stack final["FINAL RESULT: 17"]:::final stack7 --> final classDef step fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef op fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef stack fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef final fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`** **What is a postfix expression? Why is postfix notation preferred over infix notation for expression evaluation by computers?** **Model Answer:** A **postfix expression** (also called *Reverse Polish Notation*) is a notation in which every operator appears **after** its two operands. Example: `A B +` corresponds to the infix `A + B`. Postfix notation is preferred because: 1. **No parentheses required** — the order of operations is unambiguously determined by operator precedence alone. 2. **Linear, single-pass evaluation** using a stack — **O(n)** time complexity with no backtracking. 3. **No precedence/associativity parsing logic** needed during evaluation — the operator simply waits until both operands are on the stack. 4. **Direct mapping to machine code** — stack-based virtual machines (JVM, CLR) execute bytecode that is essentially postfix. *(Word count: ~90; marks: 3 — award for definition + 2 valid reasons)* ### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`** **Differentiate between infix, prefix, and postfix notations. Convert the infix expression `A + B * C` into postfix and prefix forms.** **Model Answer:** | Notation | Operator Position | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Infix | Between operands | `A + B` | | Prefix (Polish) | Before operands | `+ A B` | | Postfix (Reverse Polish) | After operands | `A B +` | For `A + B * C`: * **Infix:** `A + B * C` * **Postfix:** `A B C * +` (since `*` has higher precedence than `+`) * **Prefix:** `+ A * B C` (highest precedence operator placed first, then operands) *(Marks: 3 — 1 for table/differentiation + 2 for conversions)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:** > * **Operand Pop Order:** In postfix evaluation, the **second popped** value is the **left operand** and the **first popped** is the **right operand**. Reversing this silently changes `-`, `/`, and `^` results. *(Loses 2–3 marks)* > * **Right-Associativity of `^`:** When two `^` operators meet, the second one stays on the stack. Forgetting this flips `2 ^ 3 ^ 2` from `512` to `64`. > * **Output Queue vs. Operator Stack:** Do NOT push operands to the stack during *conversion*. Only operators go on the stack. Conflating the two loses all marks. > * **Final Drain Step:** Marks are explicitly awarded for emptying the operator stack at the end. Skipping this gives an incomplete postfix. --- ### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Convert the following infix expression into postfix using a stack. Show every step of the conversion clearly with the status of the stack and output at each stage. $$\text{Infix: } \;\; (A + B) * (C - D) \,/\, E + F \;\; \text{[7 Marks]}$$ **(b)** Evaluate the following postfix expression using a stack. Show the contents of the stack after each step. $$\text{Postfix: } \;\; 5, \; 6, \; 2, \; +, \; *, \; 12, \; 4, \; /, \; - \;\; \text{[7 Marks]}$$ #### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):** Infix → Postfix Conversion **Expression:** `(A + B) * (C - D) / E + F` | Step | Symbol | Action | Stack (top → right) | Output | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `(` | Push `(` | `(` | *(empty)* | | 2 | `A` | Emit | `(` | `A` | | 3 | `+` | Push `+` | `( +` | `A` | | 4 | `B` | Emit | `( +` | `A B` | | 5 | `)` | Pop until `(`: pop `+` | *(empty)* | `A B +` | | 6 | `*` | Stack empty → push `*` | `*` | `A B +` | | 7 | `(` | Push `(` | `* (` | `A B +` | | 8 | `C` | Emit | `* (` | `A B + C` | | 9 | `-` | Top `(` → push `-` | `* ( -` | `A B + C` | | 10 | `D` | Emit | `* ( -` | `A B + C D` | | 11 | `)` | Pop until `(`: pop `-` | `*` | `A B + C D -` | | 12 | `/` | Top `*` has P=2 = P(`/`) = 2, left-assoc → pop `*`; push `/` | `/` | `A B + C D - *` | | 13 | `E` | Emit | `/` | `A B + C D - * E` | | 14 | `+` | Top `/` has P=2 > P(`+`)=1 → pop `/`; push `+` | `+` | `A B + C D - * E /` | | 15 | `F` | Emit | `+` | `A B + C D - * E / F` | | 16 | END | Drain: pop `+` | *(empty)* | `A B + C D - * E / F +` | **Final Postfix:** `A B + C D - * E / F +` *(7 marks: 1 for table header/initialization + 5 for correct trace + 1 for final answer)* #### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):** Postfix Evaluation **Expression:** `5 6 2 + * 12 4 / -` | Step | Symbol | Operation | Stack (bottom → top) | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `5` | push(5) | `5` | | 2 | `6` | push(6) | `5, 6` | | 3 | `2` | push(2) | `5, 6, 2` | | 4 | `+` | pop 2 (op2), pop 6 (op1) → 6+2 = 8, push(8) | `5, 8` | | 5 | `*` | pop 8 (op2), pop 5 (op1) → 5×8 = 40, push(40) | `40` | | 6 | `12` | push(12) | `40, 12` | | 7 | `4` | push(4) | `40, 12, 4` | | 8 | `/` | pop 4 (op2), pop 12 (op1) → 12/4 = 3, push(3) | `40, 3` | | 9 | `-` | pop 3 (op2), pop 40 (op1) → 40−3 = 37, push(37) | `37` | **Final Evaluated Result:** **37** *(7 marks: 1 for table header + 5 for correct operations + 1 for final answer)* --- ### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Convert the following infix expression into postfix using a stack. Show the stack status at each step. $$\text{Infix: } \;\; A \;\text{\^{}}\; B \;*\; C \;+\; D \;-\; E \,/\, F \;\; \text{[7 Marks]}$$ **(b)** Evaluate the following postfix expression using a stack. $$\text{Postfix: } \;\; 8, \; 2, \; 3, \; \text{\^{}}, \; *, \; 4, \; 2, \; /, \; + \;\; \text{[7 Marks]}$$ #### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):** Infix → Postfix Conversion **Expression:** `A ^ B * C + D - E / F` | Step | Symbol | Action | Stack | Output | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `A` | Emit | *(empty)* | `A` | | 2 | `^` | Push (stack empty) | `^` | `A` | | 3 | `B` | Emit | `^` | `A B` | | 4 | `*` | Top `^` has P=3 > P(`*`)=2 → pop `^`; push `*` | `*` | `A B ^` | | 5 | `C` | Emit | `*` | `A B ^ C` | | 6 | `+` | Top `*` has P=2 > P(`+`)=1 → pop `*`; push `+` | `+` | `A B ^ C *` | | 7 | `D` | Emit | `+` | `A B ^ C * D` | | 8 | `-` | Top `+` has P=1 = P(`-`)=1, left-assoc → pop `+`; push `-` | `-` | `A B ^ C * D +` | | 9 | `E` | Emit | `-` | `A B ^ C * D + E` | | 10 | `/` | Top `-` has P=1 < P(`/`) = 2 → push `/` | `- /` | `A B ^ C * D + E` | | 11 | `F` | Emit | `- /` | `A B ^ C * D + E F` | | 12 | END | Drain: pop `/`, pop `-` | *(empty)* | `A B ^ C * D + E F / -` | **Final Postfix:** `A B ^ C * D + E F / -` *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):** Postfix Evaluation **Expression:** `8 2 3 ^ * 4 2 / +` | Step | Symbol | Operation | Stack | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `8` | push(8) | `8` | | 2 | `2` | push(2) | `8, 2` | | 3 | `3` | push(3) | `8, 2, 3` | | 4 | `^` | pop 3, pop 2 → 2³ = 8, push(8) | `8, 8` | | 5 | `*` | pop 8, pop 8 → 8×8 = 64, push(64) | `64` | | 6 | `4` | push(4) | `64, 4` | | 7 | `2` | push(2) | `64, 4, 2` | | 8 | `/` | pop 2, pop 4 → 4/2 = 2, push(2) | `64, 2` | | 9 | `+` | pop 2, pop 64 → 64+2 = 66, push(66) | `66` | **Final Evaluated Result:** **66** *(7 marks)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 1** * **Three notations:** Infix (operator between operands), Prefix (before), Postfix (after). Postfix needs no parentheses. * **Precedence ladder:** `^` (3) > `*`, `/`, `%` (2) > `+`, `-` (1). Parentheses override all. * **Associativity:** All operators are **left-associative** EXCEPT `^` (exponentiation), which is **right-associative**. * **Conversion Algorithm (Shunting Yard):** Scan left → right. **Operands** → output directly. **`(`** → push. **`)`** → pop until matching `(` removed. **Operator `op`** → pop while top ≥ `op` in precedence (with associativity tiebreak), then push. **End** → drain stack to output. * **Evaluation Algorithm:** Scan left → right. **Operand** → push value. **Operator** → pop right, pop left, compute `left op right`, push result. **End** → final stack element is the answer. * **Order of operands matters:** In postfix evaluation, **pop the right operand first**, then the left. * **Time & Space:** Both algorithms are **O(n)** time, **O(n)** space. * **Verification tip:** Symbol count (operands + operators + parentheses) is invariant between infix and postfix; parentheses only disappear. * **Real-world link:** Compilers, JVM bytecode, and HP calculators all rely on these algorithms. * **Watch out:** Right-associative `^`, missing final drain, swapped operand order, and confusing the output queue with the operator stack.

topics.title: Linked List and Memory Management – Singly Linked List: Operations on Linked List, Stacks and Queues Using Linked List, Polynomial Representation Using Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linked List and Memory Management – Singly Linked List: Operations on Linked List, Stacks and Queues Using Linked List, Polynomial Representation Using Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Linked List?

A **Singly Linked List (SLL)** is a fundamental **linear dynamic data structure** in which elements (called **nodes**) are connected sequentially using **pointers**. Unlike arrays, elements are **not stored in contiguous memory locations**. Instead, each node contains two fields:
1. A **DATA field** that holds the actual value.
2. A **LINK / NEXT field** (pointer) that holds the address of the next node in the sequence.

The list is accessed through an external pointer called **HEAD**, which stores the address of the first node. The last node contains a special value **NULL** (denoted by $\Lambda$ or `0`) to mark the end of the list.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> A linked list is a linear collection of data elements called **nodes**, where each node is split into two parts: the **info field** (data) and the **link/next field** (pointer to the next node). The first node is pointed to by an external pointer **HEAD**, and the last node points to **NULL**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Treasure Hunt

Imagine a **treasure hunt** where:
- Each clue (node) is hidden in a different **random building** (memory address) across the city.
- Each clue contains a **message** (DATA) and **directions to the next building** (LINK).
- You are given only the **address of the first clue** (HEAD pointer).
- The final clue says **"No more clues — you have reached the end"** (NULL).

You cannot jump from clue 1 to clue 5 directly (no random access), but you can follow the chain. This is exactly how a **linked list behaves in memory** — scattered, but logically connected.

## 1.3 Why Linked Lists? The Memory Management Perspective

Arrays suffer from two major drawbacks:
1. **Fixed size** — declared at compile time, wasting or running out of memory.
2. **Contiguous allocation** — insertion/deletion in the middle requires expensive shifting ($O(n)$).

Linked Lists solve these via **dynamic memory allocation** using heap functions like `malloc()` and `calloc()` in C, allowing:
- **Flexible size** — grows and shrinks at runtime.
- **Efficient insertion/deletion** at the beginning in $O(1)$ time.
- **No memory wastage** — allocates exactly as much as needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off to remember:** Linked Lists sacrifice **random access** ($O(n)$ lookup by index) to gain **dynamic memory flexibility** and **efficient pointer-based operations**.

## 1.4 Node Structure — Formal Definition

In C language, a node is declared as a `struct`:

```c
struct Node {
    int data;              // INFO field
    struct Node *next;     // LINK / NEXT field (recursive pointer)
};
```

The `next` pointer is **recursive** — it points to a node of the same type, enabling chain formation.

## 1.5 Memory Layout Diagram (Textual)

| Memory Address | DATA Field | NEXT Field (Address) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1000 | 10 | 2000 |
| 2000 | 20 | 3000 |
| 3000 | 30 | 4000 |
| 4000 | 40 | **NULL (0)** |

`HEAD` (external pointer) $\rightarrow$ **1000** $\rightarrow$ 10 $\rightarrow$ 2000 $\rightarrow$ 20 $\rightarrow$ 3000 $\rightarrow$ 30 $\rightarrow$ 4000 $\rightarrow$ 40 $\rightarrow$ **NULL**

> [!NOTE]
> The addresses are **not necessarily consecutive**. The OS heap allocator returns addresses based on availability, not contiguity. This is the **fundamental difference** between arrays and linked lists.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Singly Linked List

A singly linked list $L$ of $n$ nodes is formally defined as an **ordered tuple**:
$$L = \langle n, \text{HEAD} \rangle$$
where:
- $n \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ is the number of nodes.
- $\text{HEAD} \in \{\text{Node pointer}, \text{NULL}\}$.
- If $n = 0$, the list is **EMPTY**, and $\text{HEAD} = \text{NULL}$.
- For $n \geq 1$, $\text{HEAD} \rightarrow \text{Node}_1 \rightarrow \text{Node}_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow \text{Node}_n \rightarrow \text{NULL}$.

The **length** of a linked list is determined dynamically by traversing from HEAD until NULL is encountered.

## 2.2 Core Operations — The Big Picture

Every linked list operation follows a common pattern:
1. **Create a new node** using `malloc()`.
2. **Assign data** to its `info` field.
3. **Manipulate pointers** (`next` fields) to insert/connect/delete the node.
4. **Update HEAD or previous node's link** as needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Most Tested Operations:** The six operations below cover ~90% of all KTU 2024 scheme questions on linked lists. Master them thoroughly.

### 2.2.1 Operation 1 — Insertion at the Beginning

**Logic Steps:**
- Allocate a new node `newNode`.
- Set `newNode->data = item`.
- Set `newNode->next = HEAD` (point to current first node).
- Update `HEAD = newNode` (new node becomes first).

**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$ — fastest insertion in a linked list.

### 2.2.2 Operation 2 — Insertion at the End

**Logic Steps:**
- Allocate `newNode`; set `newNode->data = item`; set `newNode->next = NULL`.
- If list is empty, `HEAD = newNode`.
- Else, traverse to the last node (where `temp->next == NULL`) and set `temp->next = newNode`.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — requires full traversal.

> [!TIP]
> **Optimization for KTU:** Maintain a **TAIL pointer** alongside HEAD to achieve $O(1)$ insertion at the end. This is a favorite **2-mark trick question** in KTU exams.

### 2.2.3 Operation 3 — Insertion at a Given Position

**Logic Steps:**
- Traverse to position $k-1$ (call it `prev`).
- Allocate `newNode`; set data; set `newNode->next = prev->next`.
- Set `prev->next = newNode`.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — search + insertion.

### 2.2.4 Operation 4 — Deletion at the Beginning

**Logic Steps:**
- Set `temp = HEAD` (save first node).
- Set `HEAD = HEAD->next` (second node becomes first).
- Free `temp` (deallocate memory).

**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$.

### 2.2.5 Operation 5 — Deletion at the End

**Logic Steps:**
- Traverse to the **second-to-last** node (`prev`), where `prev->next->next == NULL`.
- Set `temp = prev->next` (last node).
- Set `prev->next = NULL`.
- Free `temp`.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$.

### 2.2.6 Operation 6 — Traversal & Search

**Logic Steps:**
- Set `temp = HEAD`.
- While `temp != NULL`, process `temp->data`; then `temp = temp->next`.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ for traversal; $O(n)$ for search in the worst case.

## 2.3 Stack Using Linked List

A **Stack** is a **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** data structure. Using a linked list, we implement it with `HEAD` as the **top of the stack**.

- **PUSH(item):** Insert node at the beginning. $O(1)$.
- **POP():** Delete node from the beginning. $O(1)$.
- **PEEK():** Return `HEAD->data`. $O(1)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HEAD as top?** Because insertion/deletion at the HEAD is $O(1)$ in linked lists. Using the tail would require $O(n)$ traversal for both push and pop, defeating the stack's purpose.

## 2.4 Queue Using Linked List

A **Queue** is a **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** data structure. To achieve $O(1)$ enqueue and dequeue, we maintain **two pointers**:
- **FRONT** — points to the first node (for DEQUEUE).
- **REAR** — points to the last node (for ENQUEUE).

- **ENQUEUE(item):** Insert at REAR. $O(1)$.
- **DEQUEUE():** Delete from FRONT. $O(1)$.

## 2.5 Polynomial Representation Using Linked List

A polynomial of degree $n$ is:
$$P(x) = a_n x^n + a_{n-1} x^{n-1} + \dots + a_1 x + a_0$$

Each term (monomial) is stored as a node with **two data fields**:
1. **COEFFICIENT** ($a_i$).
2. **EXPONENT / POWER** ($i$).

```c
struct PolyNode {
    int coeff;
    int exp;
    struct PolyNode *next;
};
```

**Convention:** Nodes are stored in **descending order of exponents** for efficient addition/subtraction.

**Polynomial Addition Algorithm:**
1. Traverse both polynomials `P` and `Q` simultaneously.
2. Compare exponents:
   - If `p->exp == q->exp`: Add coefficients. If result $\neq 0$, create a new node with sum coefficient and same exponent.
   - If `p->exp > q->exp`: Copy `p`'s term to result.
   - If `p->exp < q->exp`: Copy `q`'s term to result.
3. Append any remaining terms of the longer polynomial.
**Time Complexity:** $O(m + n)$ where $m, n$ are the lengths of the two polynomials.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Insertion at Beginning | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insertion at End | $O(1)^{*}$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insertion at Position $k$ | $O(1)$ | $O(k)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Deletion at Beginning | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Deletion at End | $O(1)^{*}$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Deletion at Position $k$ | $O(1)$ | $O(k)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Traversal | — | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Search | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Stack PUSH (Linked) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Stack POP (Linked) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Queue ENQUEUE (Linked) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Queue DEQUEUE (Linked) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Polynomial Addition | — | $O(m+n)$ | $O(m+n)$ | $O(m+n)$ |
| Polynomial Evaluation (Horner) | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |

$^{*}$ *With TAIL pointer optimization maintained.*

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Operating Systems:** Process scheduling queues, memory management free lists.
- **Compiler Design:** Symbol tables, abstract syntax trees (recursion stack).
- **Web Browsers:** Back/forward navigation history.
- **Undo Mechanisms:** Text editors (each edit = a node).
- **Polynomial Arithmetic:** Computer Algebra Systems (Mathematica, MATLAB), curve fitting in CAD.
- **Memory Allocators:** Internal free-block tracking in `malloc`/`free` implementations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Insertion at Position 3 in a Linked List

### Problem Statement
Given the linked list: `HEAD → 10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`. Insert the value **25** at **position 3** (1-indexed, so between 20 and 30).

### Step-by-Step Algorithm Execution

**Step 1:** Initialize traversal pointer.
Let `temp = HEAD`. We need to reach position $k-1 = 2$.

**Step 2:** Traverse to position 2.
- Iteration 1: `temp` points to node(10). Since $i=1 \neq 2$, set `temp = temp->next`.
- Iteration 2: `temp` points to node(20). Since $i=2 = k-1$, **stop**. This is our `prev` node.

**Step 3:** Allocate new node using `malloc`.
```c
struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
```

**Step 4:** Assign data and link.
- `newNode->data = 25`
- `newNode->next = prev->next` (which currently points to node(30))

**Step 5:** Link previous node to new node.
- `prev->next = newNode`

### Pointer Recalculation Proof

Let $P_{old}(i)$ denote the next pointer of node $i$ **before** insertion.
- $P_{old}(\text{node}_{20}) = \text{addr}(\text{node}_{30})$
- $P_{old}(\text{newNode}_{25}) = \text{undefined} \rightarrow \text{set to } P_{old}(\text{node}_{20})$
- $P_{new}(\text{node}_{20}) = \text{addr}(\text{newNode}_{25})$

Final chain:
$$\text{HEAD} \rightarrow \text{node}(10) \rightarrow \text{node}(20) \rightarrow \text{node}(25) \rightarrow \text{node}(30) \rightarrow \text{node}(40) \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

**Time Taken:** 2 pointer reassignments + 2 traversals = $O(k) = O(3)$ for this case.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Deletion of Last Node

### Problem Statement
Delete the last node (40) from: `HEAD → 10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`.

**Step 1:** Traverse to the second-to-last node.
- Start: `temp = HEAD` (points to 10), `prev = NULL`.
- Iteration 1: `prev = temp` (10), `temp = temp->next` (20). Check: `temp->next != NULL` (20→30 exists), continue.
- Iteration 2: `prev = temp` (20), `temp = temp->next` (30). Check: `temp->next != NULL` (30→40 exists), continue.
- Iteration 3: `prev = temp` (30), `temp = temp->next` (40). Check: `temp->next == NULL` (40→NULL). **Stop.**

**Step 2:** `prev` is now node(30), `temp` is node(40) (the target).

**Step 3:** Disconnect last node.
- `prev->next = temp->next` $\rightarrow$ `prev->next = NULL`.

**Step 4:** Free the deleted node.
- `free(temp)` — releases memory back to heap.

Result:
$$\text{HEAD} \rightarrow \text{node}(10) \rightarrow \text{node}(20) \rightarrow \text{node}(30) \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Polynomial Addition

### Problem Statement
Add the two polynomials:
$$P_1(x) = 5x^4 + 3x^2 + 1$$
$$P_2(x) = 4x^3 + 2x^2 + 7$$

Expected result: $P_3(x) = 5x^4 + 4x^3 + 5x^2 + 8$.

### Linked List Representations

| $P_1$ Node | coeff | exp | next |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| N1 | 5 | 4 | →N2 |
| N2 | 3 | 2 | →N3 |
| N3 | 1 | 0 | →NULL |

| $P_2$ Node | coeff | exp | next |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| M1 | 4 | 3 | →M2 |
| M2 | 2 | 2 | →M3 |
| M3 | 7 | 0 | →NULL |

### Algorithm Execution Trace

**Iteration 1:** `p = N1 (5,4)`, `q = M1 (4,3)`.
Compare exponents: $4 > 3$.
- Create result node R1 with `(5, 4)`. Copy from $P_1$.
- Advance `p = p->next` → N2.

**Iteration 2:** `p = N2 (3,2)`, `q = M1 (4,3)`.
Compare exponents: $2 < 3$.
- Create result node R2 with `(4, 3)`. Copy from $P_2$.
- Advance `q = q->next` → M2.

**Iteration 3:** `p = N2 (3,2)`, `q = M2 (2,2)`.
Compare exponents: $2 == 2$.
- Sum of coefficients: $c = 3 + 2 = 5 \neq 0$.
- Create result node R3 with `(5, 2)`.
- Advance both `p` and `q` → N3 and M3.

**Iteration 4:** `p = N3 (1,0)`, `q = M3 (7,0)`.
Compare exponents: $0 == 0$.
- Sum of coefficients: $c = 1 + 7 = 8 \neq 0$.
- Create result node R4 with `(8, 0)`.
- Advance both → NULL.

**Iteration 5:** Both `p` and `q` are NULL. **STOP.**

### Result Linked List

$$\text{HEAD}_3 \rightarrow (5, 4) \rightarrow (4, 3) \rightarrow (5, 2) \rightarrow (8, 0) \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

This represents:
$$P_3(x) = 5x^4 + 4x^3 + 5x^2 + 8$$

**Time Complexity Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T(m,n) &= \min(m,n) \cdot O(1) + \max(m,n) \cdot O(1) \\
T(m,n) &= O(m) + O(n) \\
T(m,n) &= O(m+n)
\end{aligned}$$

Each iteration performs **constant-time** pointer work, and we make at most $m+n$ iterations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Address Tracing

Consider the following scenario during `INSERT_AT_BEGIN(99)` on an existing list `10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`:

| Step | Action | `newNode->data` | `newNode->next` | `HEAD` |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `malloc()` returns address `5000` | uninitialized | uninitialized | `1000` |
| 2 | `newNode->data = 99` | **99** | uninitialized | `1000` |
| 3 | `newNode->next = HEAD` | 99 | **`1000`** | `1000` |
| 4 | `HEAD = newNode` | 99 | `1000` | **`5000`** |

Final list: `HEAD(5000) → 99 → 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** The order of **Step 3 and Step 4 is CRITICAL**. Reversing them (assigning HEAD = newNode first) would **lose the reference** to the original list, causing a **memory leak** — the original list becomes unreachable (orphaned). This is one of the most common mistakes KTU examiners deduct marks for.

---

## 3.5 Stack Implementation — Pointer Math

### PUSH Operation Trace
Stack state before: `TOP → 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`. Call `PUSH(40)`.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{newNode} &= \text{malloc}(\text{sizeof(Node)}) \\
\text{newNode->data} &= 40 \\
\text{newNode->next} &= \text{TOP} \quad (\text{currently points to node}(10)) \\
\text{TOP} &= \text{newNode}
\end{aligned}$$

Final state: `TOP → 40 → 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`.

### POP Operation Trace
Stack state before: `TOP → 40 → 10 → 20 → NULL`. Call `POP()`.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{temp} &= \text{TOP} \quad (\text{node}(40)) \\
\text{value} &= \text{temp->data} = 40 \\
\text{TOP} &= \text{TOP->next} \quad (\text{now points to node}(10)) \\
\text{free}(\text{temp})
\end{aligned}$$

Final state: `TOP → 10 → 20 → NULL`. Returned value: 40.

---

## 3.6 Queue Implementation — Two-Pointer Logic

### ENQUEUE Trace
Queue: `FRONT → 10 → 20 → REAR (NULL)`. Call `ENQUEUE(30)`.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{newNode->data} &= 30 \\
\text{newNode->next} &= \text{NULL} \\
\text{REAR->next} &= \text{newNode} \quad (\text{if list non-empty}) \\
\text{REAR} &= \text{newNode}
\end{aligned}$$

Final: `FRONT → 10 → 20 → 30 (REAR) → NULL`.

### DEQUEUE Trace
Queue: `FRONT(10) → 20 → 30(REAR) → NULL`. Call `DEQUEUE()`.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{temp} &= \text{FRONT} = \text{node}(10) \\
\text{value} &= 10 \\
\text{FRONT} &= \text{FRONT->next} = \text{node}(20) \\
\text{free}(\text{temp}) \\
\text{If FRONT == NULL: REAR = NULL (empty queue check)}
\end{aligned}$$

Final: `FRONT(20) → 30(REAR) → NULL`. Returned: 10.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Basic Node Structure & Memory Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD[HEAD Pointer Address 1000] --> N1[Node at 1000: Data 10]
    N1 --> N2[Node at 2500: Data 20]
    N2 --> N3[Node at 1800: Data 30]
    N3 --> N4[Node at 3200: Data 40]
    N4 --> NULL[NULL Pointer 0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Insight:** Notice how the addresses (1000, 2500, 1800, 3200) are **non-contiguous** — they are scattered across the heap. The logical sequence is maintained purely through pointer linkage, not physical adjacency.
