# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Singly Linked List. With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a node.**

**Model Answer:**

A Singly Linked List is a linear dynamic data structure consisting of a sequence of nodes, where each node contains two fields: an **INFO field** (data) and a **NEXT field** (pointer to the next node). The first node is accessed via an external pointer called **HEAD**, and the last node points to **NULL**.

```
  +-------+-------+        +-------+-------+        +-------+-------+
  | DATA  | NEXT  |  --->  | DATA  | NEXT  |  --->  | DATA  | NULL  |
  |  10   |  2000 |        |  20   |  3000 |        |  30   |   0   |
  +-------+-------+        +-------+-------+        +-------+-------+
   Address 1000            Address 2000            Address 3000
       ^
      HEAD
```

**Node Structure:**
```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};
```

The `next` field is a self-referential pointer that links to the subsequent node. The list is **dynamic** — memory is allocated at runtime using `malloc()`, allowing variable size.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between arrays and linked lists. Mention any three advantages of linked lists over arrays.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Array | Linked List |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Memory Allocation | Static (compile-time), contiguous | Dynamic (runtime), non-contiguous |
| Size | Fixed at declaration | Grows/shrinks at runtime |
| Access Time | $O(1)$ random access | $O(n)$ sequential access |
| Insertion/Deletion (middle) | $O(n)$ — requires shifting | $O(1)$ — pointer update only |
| Memory Wastage | Possible (unused slots) | None — exact size allocated |
| Extra Memory | No overhead | Extra pointer per node |

**Three advantages of Linked Lists:**
1. **Dynamic size** — no need to declare maximum size in advance.
2. **Efficient insertion/deletion** at the beginning in $O(1)$ time without shifting.
3. **No memory wastage** — memory is allocated as needed, and freed upon deletion using `free()`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module question is **14 marks**, internally split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Students answer EITHER (a) OR (b) from the same module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Write the algorithm to insert a node at the beginning, at the end, and at a given position in a singly linked list. Show the working with a suitable example.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Insertion at the Beginning — Algorithm:**

```c
void insertAtBeginning(struct Node **HEAD, int item) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = item;
    newNode->next = *HEAD;     // Point to old first node
    *HEAD = newNode;           // Update HEAD to new node
}
```

**2. Insertion at the End — Algorithm:**

```c
void insertAtEnd(struct Node *HEAD, int item) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = item;
    newNode->next = NULL;
    if (HEAD == NULL) {        // Empty list case
        HEAD = newNode;
        return;
    }
    struct Node *temp = HEAD;
    while (temp->next != NULL) // Traverse to last node
        temp = temp->next;
    temp->next = newNode;      // Link last node to new node
}
```

**3. Insertion at Position k — Algorithm:**

```c
void insertAtPosition(struct Node *HEAD, int item, int k) {
    if (k == 1) { insertAtBeginning(&HEAD, item); return; }
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = item;
    struct Node *temp = HEAD;
    for (int i = 1; i < k-1 && temp != NULL; i++)
        temp = temp->next;
    if (temp == NULL) return;  // Position out of bounds
    newNode->next = temp->next;
    temp->next = newNode;
}
```

**Worked Example:** Start with `HEAD → 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`.

- **Insert 5 at beginning:** `HEAD → 5 → 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`
- **Insert 40 at end:** `HEAD → 5 → 10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`
- **Insert 25 at position 3:** `HEAD → 5 → 10 → 25 → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`

**Time Complexities:** Beginning = $O(1)$; End = $O(n)$; Position = $O(k)$.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Implement a STACK using a singly linked list. Write the PUSH and POP algorithms and explain with an example.**

**Model Solution:**

**Stack Node Declaration:**
```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};
struct Node *TOP = NULL;   // TOP acts as HEAD for the stack
```

**PUSH Algorithm — Insert at TOP:**

```c
void PUSH(int item) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (newNode == NULL) {
        printf("Stack Overflow\n");
        return;
    }
    newNode->data = item;
    newNode->next = TOP;     // New node points to current TOP
    TOP = newNode;           // Update TOP
    printf("%d pushed\n", item);
}
```

**POP Algorithm — Delete from TOP:**

```c
int POP() {
    if (TOP == NULL) {
        printf("Stack Underflow\n");
        return -1;
    }
    struct Node *temp = TOP;
    int item = temp->data;
    TOP = TOP->next;         // Move TOP to next node
    free(temp);              // Free old TOP
    return item;
}
```

**Worked Example:**

| Operation | Stack State (TOP → ... → NULL) | Returned |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Initial | `NULL` | — |
| PUSH(10) | `TOP → 10 → NULL` | — |
| PUSH(20) | `TOP → 20 → 10 → NULL` | — |
| PUSH(30) | `TOP → 30 → 20 → 10 → NULL` | — |
| POP() | `TOP → 20 → 10 → NULL` | 30 |
| POP() | `TOP → 10 → NULL` | 20 |

**Time Complexity:** Both PUSH and POP = $O(1)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is implementing PUSH at the tail. **Always use HEAD as TOP** — tail operations are $O(n)$ and defeat the purpose of a stack. Also, students forget the **Stack Overflow** check after `malloc()` — failing to include it costs 1 mark in KTU valuation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Write the algorithm to delete a node from the beginning, end, and from a given position in a singly linked list. Trace with a suitable example.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Deletion at Beginning:**

```c
void deleteAtBeginning(struct Node **HEAD) {
    if (*HEAD == NULL) { printf("Empty list\n"); return; }
    struct Node *temp = *HEAD;
    *HEAD = (*HEAD)->next;   // Move HEAD to second node
    free(temp);
}
```

**2. Deletion at End:**

```c
void deleteAtEnd(struct Node *HEAD) {
    if (HEAD == NULL) return;
    if (HEAD->next == NULL) {  // Only one node
        free(HEAD);
        HEAD = NULL;
        return;
    }
    struct Node *temp = HEAD;
    while (temp->next->next != NULL)  // Stop at second-to-last
        temp = temp->next;
    free(temp->next);          // Free last node
    temp->next = NULL;         // Mark new end
}
```

**3. Deletion at Position k:**

```c
void deleteAtPosition(struct Node **HEAD, int k) {
    if (*HEAD == NULL || k == 1) { deleteAtBeginning(HEAD); return; }
    struct Node *temp = *HEAD;
    for (int i = 1; i < k-1 && temp != NULL; i++)
        temp = temp->next;
    if (temp == NULL || temp->next == NULL) return;
    struct Node *toDelete = temp->next;
    temp->next = toDelete->next;
    free(toDelete);
}
```

**Worked Example:** Start with `HEAD → 10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → 50 → NULL`.

- **Delete at beginning:** `HEAD → 20 → 30 → 40 → 50 → NULL`
- **Delete at end:** `HEAD → 20 → 30 → 40 → NULL`
- **Delete at position 2:** `HEAD → 20 → 40 → NULL`

**Time Complexities:** Beginning = $O(1)$; End = $O(n)$; Position = $O(k)$.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Represent two polynomials $5x^4 + 3x^2 + 1$ and $4x^3 + 2x^2 + 7$ using linked lists. Write the algorithm to add them and show the resulting polynomial.**

**Model Solution:**

**Node Declaration:**
```c
struct PolyNode {
    int coeff;
    int exp;
    struct PolyNode *next;
};
```

**Linked List Representations:**

Polynomial $P_1(x) = 5x^4 + 3x^2 + 1$:
$$P_1: \text{HEAD}_1 \rightarrow (5,4) \rightarrow (3,2) \rightarrow (1,0) \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

Polynomial $P_2(x) = 4x^3 + 2x^2 + 7$:
$$P_2: \text{HEAD}_2 \rightarrow (4,3) \rightarrow (2,2) \rightarrow (7,0) \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

**Addition Algorithm:**

```c
struct PolyNode* addPolynomials(struct PolyNode *p, struct PolyNode *q) {
    struct PolyNode *result = NULL, *tail = NULL;
    while (p != NULL && q != NULL) {
        struct PolyNode *newNode = (struct PolyNode *)malloc(sizeof(struct PolyNode));
        if (p->exp == q->exp) {
            newNode->coeff = p->coeff + q->coeff;
            newNode->exp = p->exp;
            p = p->next; q = q->next;
        } else if (p->exp > q->exp) {
            newNode->coeff = p->coeff; newNode->exp = p->exp;
            p = p->next;
        } else {
            newNode->coeff = q->coeff; newNode->exp = q->exp;
            q = q->next;
        }
        newNode->next = NULL;
        if (result == NULL) result = tail = newNode;
        else { tail->next = newNode; tail = newNode; }
    }
    while (p != NULL) { /* append remaining p terms */ p = p->next; }
    while (q != NULL) { /* append remaining q terms */ q = q->next; }
    return result;
}
```

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Step | p | q | Comparison | Action | Result Built |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | (5,4) | (4,3) | 4>3 | Copy p: (5,4) | (5,4) |
| 2 | (3,2) | (4,3) | 2<3 | Copy q: (4,3) | (5,4)→(4,3) |
| 3 | (3,2) | (2,2) | 2=2 | Add: 3+2=5 | (5,4)→(4,3)→(5,2) |
| 4 | (1,0) | (7,0) | 0=0 | Add: 1+7=8 | (5,4)→(4,3)→(5,2)→(8,0) |

**Result:** $P_3(x) = 5x^4 + 4x^3 + 5x^2 + 8$.

$$\text{HEAD}_3 \rightarrow (5,4) \rightarrow (4,3) \rightarrow (5,2) \rightarrow (8,0) \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

**Time Complexity:** $O(m + n)$ where $m = 3$, $n = 3$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Polynomial Addition:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sum-to-zero check:** When exponents are equal, if the sum of coefficients is **0**, do NOT create a node. Many KTU students lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Wrong order of nodes:** Always maintain **descending order of exponents** in the result.
> 3. **Forgetting to copy remaining terms:** After one polynomial is exhausted, the remaining terms of the other must be appended. Missing this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Single field instead of two:** Each node must have BOTH `coeff` and `exp` — using a single field is a structural error worth 2 marks deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Definition:** Singly Linked List = sequence of nodes with `data` + `next` pointer; accessed via `HEAD`; ends at `NULL`.
- **Memory:** Nodes are **scattered** in heap via `malloc()`; not contiguous like arrays.
- **Node struct:** `struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }`.
- **Insertion at Beginning:** $O(1)$ — Link new node to old HEAD, then update HEAD.
- **Insertion at End:** $O(n)$ — Traverse to last node; optimized to $O(1)$ with TAIL pointer.
- **Insertion at Position k:** $O(k)$ — Traverse to $k-1$, then adjust two pointers.
- **Deletion at Beginning:** $O(1)$ — Save HEAD, advance HEAD, free old node.
- **Deletion at End:** $O(n)$ — Traverse to second-to-last, free last, mark NULL.
- **Deletion at Position k:** $O(k)$ — Traverse to $k-1$, unlink target node, free.
- **Stack (Linked):** TOP = HEAD; PUSH at TOP = $O(1)$; POP from TOP = $O(1)$.
- **Queue (Linked):** FRONT pointer for dequeue; REAR pointer for enqueue; both $O(1)$.
- **Polynomial Node:** Two fields `coeff` and `exp`; maintain descending exponent order.
- **Polynomial Addition:** Compare exponents — equal (add coeffs), greater (copy p), lesser (copy q); Time = $O(m+n)$.
- **Free memory** with `free()` after deletion to prevent **memory leaks**.
- **Always check** `malloc()` return value for `NULL` (allocation failure).
- **Array vs List:** Array = $O(1)$ access, $O(n)$ insertion; List = $O(n)$ access, $O(1)$ insertion at head.
- **Two-pointer queue** is essential — single HEAD pointer makes dequeue $O(n)$.
- **STACK** uses HEAD as TOP — never the TAIL.
- **Horner's rule** evaluates polynomial in $O(n)$ time using a single pass.
- **Empty list condition:** `HEAD == NULL`.
- **Single-node deletion:** Special case — `HEAD->next == NULL` requires both HEAD reset and free.
- **Doubly linked list** uses two pointers (`prev` and `next`) — covered in Module 3.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always draw the **pointer diagram before and after** the operation. Examiners award 2–3 marks just for clear diagrams. For polynomial problems, **trace the algorithm with a table** — partial marking is generous when steps are visible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doubly Linked List
Circular Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circular Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔵 MODULE 2: CIRCULAR LINKED LIST — CORE FOUNDATIONS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Circular Linked List (CLL):** A linear data structure in which the last node does not point to `NULL` (as in a standard singly linked list), but instead points **back to the first node (head)**, forming a closed loop or "circle." Traversal is possible indefinitely until an explicit break condition is met.

Formally, a Circular Linked List is a variation of the Linked List ADT where:

$$ \text{Tail} \rightarrow \text{Next} \equiv \text{Head} $$

That is, if `L` is the list and `Tail` is the last node, then `Tail->next = L` (the first node), not `NULL`.

There are **two principal variants** recognized in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Singly Circular Linked List (SCLL):** Each node has exactly one pointer (`next`), and the last node's `next` points to the first node.
2. **Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL):** Each node has two pointers (`prev` and `next`). The head's `prev` points to the tail, and the tail's `next` points to the head.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]  
> **Think of a Circular Linked List like a merry-go-round.**  
> Imagine a group of people standing in a circle holding hands. The last person is not standing alone at the end of a line — instead, their hand reaches back to the first person. There is no "end" of the line. You can keep walking around the circle forever, but if you remember where you started, you know when to stop.

**Another analogy:** The hands of a clock. The hour hand moves from 12 → 1 → 2 → ... → 11 → **back to 12**. The list never terminates at `NULL`; it just loops back to the start.

---

## 1.3 Node Structure (C-style Definition)

A standard node of a Circular Linked List is defined in **C** (the language of instruction at KTU) as:

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;   // For SCLL
};
```

For a Doubly Circular Linked List:

```c
struct DNode {
    int data;
    struct DNode *prev;
    struct DNode *next;
};
```

> [!TIP]  
> **Sentinel / Header Nodes:** In many KTU exam questions, a *header node* is used. Its `data` field is unused, and its `next` pointer stores the address of the actual first element. The header's presence simplifies boundary conditions for insertion/deletion.

---

## 1.4 Why Circular Linked Lists? (Motivation)

A standard **Singly Linked List (SLL)** has a critical limitation: from any node, you **cannot reach the previous node** without restarting traversal from `head`. Also, to perform operations at the tail, you must traverse from the head in $O(n)$ time.

A **Circular Linked List** solves this elegantly:

- **Continuous rotation** is possible (useful in round-robin scheduling).
- The **last node can be accessed** in $O(1)$ if a `tail` pointer is maintained.
- The **list is never "empty" in pointer terms** — even a list with zero elements has a node whose `next` points to itself (or to the header).

---

## 1.5 Head vs Tail Pointer Strategy

| Strategy | Best Use Case | Access Time to Tail |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| `head` only | Static lists, simple traversal | $O(n)$ |
| `tail` only | Round-robin, fast append | $O(1)$ |
| `head` + `tail` | Deques, frequent both-end ops | $O(1)$ for both ends |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** When a `tail` pointer is maintained in a Circular Linked List, **both head and tail can be accessed in $O(1)$** — this is a frequently tested exam point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of a Circular Linked List

In a Singly Circular Linked List, the structural invariant is:

$$ \forall \text{ node } n_i \in L : \quad n_i \rightarrow \text{next} = n_{(i+1) \bmod N} $$

where $N$ is the number of nodes in the list, and indices wrap around.

### 🔑 Key Operational Properties

- **No NULL Termination:** The list has no natural "end." Traversal must use a stopping sentinel — typically checking if the current pointer has returned to `head` or if a count of visited nodes equals $N$.
- **Single Successor per Node (SCLL):** Each node has exactly one outgoing link (`next`).
- **Bidirectional Links (DCLL):** Each node has both `prev` and `next`, enabling reverse traversal.
- **Self-Loop on Empty List:** In a header-based empty list, `head->next = head` (or `head` itself is the only node).

---

## 2.2 Core Operations — Algorithm Breakdown

### 🅰️ Traversal in a Circular Linked List

**Step-by-step logic (SCLL with header):**

1. Initialize a temporary pointer `ptr = head->next`.
2. **While** `ptr != head` (i.e., we haven't looped back to the header):
    - Process `ptr->data`.
    - Move `ptr = ptr->next`.
3. End traversal.

**Why this works:** The header acts as a "sentinel" — when we circle back to it, we know we've visited all elements exactly once.

### 🅱️ Insertion Operations

There are **three canonical insertion points**, each with distinct logic:

#### (i) Insertion at the Beginning (SCLL, with `tail` pointer)
1. Create a new node `newNode`.
2. Set `newNode->next = tail->next` (which is the current head).
3. Set `tail->next = newNode`.
4. (Head is now `newNode`; tail remains unchanged.)

#### (ii) Insertion at the End (SCLL, with `tail` pointer)
1. Create `newNode`.
2. Set `newNode->next = tail->next` (i.e., back to head).
3. Set `tail->next = newNode`.
4. **Update `tail = newNode`**.

#### (iii) Insertion After a Given Node
1. Locate the target node `prev` (takes $O(n)$ in worst case).
2. Create `newNode`.
3. `newNode->next = prev->next`.
4. `prev->next = newNode`.

### 🅲️ Deletion Operations

#### (i) Deletion of Head Node (SCLL, with `tail` pointer)
1. If `tail == tail->next`, the list has only one node — free it and set `tail = NULL`.
2. Otherwise, set `temp = tail->next` (the head).
3. Set `tail->next = temp->next` (skip the head, link tail to second node).
4. Free `temp`.

#### (ii) Deletion of Tail Node (SCLL, with `tail` pointer)
1. Traverse from `tail->next` (head) until you find the node **just before** `tail` — call it `prev`. This is the only operation requiring $O(n)$.
2. Set `prev->next = tail->next` (head).
3. Free `tail` and update `tail = prev`.

#### (iii) Deletion of an Arbitrary Node
1. Locate the node to delete (`target`) and its predecessor (`prev`).
2. `prev->next = target->next`.
3. Free `target`.

---

## 2.3 Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL) — Special Operations

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Syllabus Coverage:** Module 2 in PCCST303 explicitly requires understanding the structure and operations of Doubly Circular Linked Lists.

In a DCLL, **four pointer manipulations** are required for each insertion/deletion:

For inserting a new node `N` between nodes `A` and `B`:

$$ N \rightarrow \text{next} = B $$

$$ N \rightarrow \text{prev} = A $$

$$ A \rightarrow \text{next} = N $$

$$ B \rightarrow \text{prev} = N $$

This is the classic "**4-link update**" that students frequently forget in exams.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Complexity Cheat Sheet

| Operation | SCLL (head only) | SCLL (with tail ptr) | DCLL |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|------|
| **Traversal** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Insert at head** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Insert at tail** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Insert after node** | $O(n)$ (locate) + $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ (locate) + $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ if node pointer given |
| **Delete at head** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Delete at tail** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ (need prev) | $O(1)$ |
| **Delete arbitrary node** | $O(n)$ (locate) | $O(n)$ (locate) | $O(1)$ if node ptr given |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |

### 📐 Structural Equations (Memory Layout)

If each node occupies $s$ bytes (data + pointer overhead), the total memory used by a CLL of $n$ nodes is:

$$ M_{\text{total}} = n \cdot s \quad \text{bytes} $$

For a DCLL:

$$ M_{\text{total}}^{\text{DCLL}} = n \cdot s_{\text{DCLL}} \quad \text{where} \quad s_{\text{DCLL}} = s_{\text{data}} + 2 \cdot s_{\text{ptr}} $$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | Use of Circular Linked List |
|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Operating Systems** | Round-robin CPU scheduling — process list cycles indefinitely. |
| **Multiplayer Games** | Turn rotation among players; the "active" player cycles through a fixed ring. |
| **Buffer Management** | Circular buffers (ring buffers) for streaming data, audio, network packets. |
| **Token Ring Networks** | Token passing in LAN topologies (IEEE 802.5). |
| **Undo/Redo Systems** | Browsers and editors use circular lists to wrap around at history boundaries. |
| **Music Players** | Looping through a playlist in repeat mode. |

---

## 2.6 Advantages vs. Disadvantages Summary

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU Frequently Tested Comparison Point.**

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------------|---------------|
| No NULL pointer issues — robust against dangling-tail bugs. | Risk of **infinite loops** if traversal termination is mishandled. |
| $O(1)$ access to head AND tail (with tail pointer). | Reverse traversal impossible in SCLL. |
| Useful for cyclic/rotating data structures. | More complex pointer manipulations → bug-prone. |
| Eliminates special cases for first/last node in some algorithms. | Slightly higher memory overhead (especially DCLL). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example: Manual Traversal of a Circular Linked List

Consider a Singly Circular Linked List with header node `H` and data nodes containing: **10 → 20 → 30 → 40**, with the last node (40) pointing back to the first data node (10).

### Visual representation:

$$ H \rightarrow [10] \rightarrow [20] \rightarrow [30] \rightarrow [40] \rightarrow \text{(back to 10)} $$

### Step-by-step traversal trace:

| Step | Current Pointer (`ptr`) | `ptr->data` | `ptr == H?` | Action |
|------|------------------------|-------------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | `H->next` (node with 10) | 10 | No | Print 10; `ptr = ptr->next` |
| 2 | Node with 20 | 20 | No | Print 20; `ptr = ptr->next` |
| 3 | Node with 30 | 30 | No | Print 30; `ptr = ptr->next` |
| 4 | Node with 40 | 40 | No | Print 40; `ptr = ptr->next` |
| 5 | Node with 10 (wrapped) | 10 | No | `ptr = ptr->next` (loops) |
| 6 | Node with 20 | 20 | No | ... and so on |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | **Infinite loop without a counter!** |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner Pitfall:** If you write traversal code as `while(ptr != NULL)`, it will NEVER terminate for a CLL. The correct stopping condition uses the **header** (`while(ptr != head)`) or a **count variable**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Insertion at the End (with `tail` pointer)

**Initial State:** CLL with `tail` pointing to the last node. List: `[10] → [20] → [30]`, and `tail` points to `[30]`.

We wish to insert `40` at the end.

### Step 1: Create the new node
```c
newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
newNode->data = 40;
```

### Step 2: Set the new node's `next` to point to the current head
The current head is `tail->next` (which is `[10]`).

$$ \text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{tail} \rightarrow \text{next} = [10] $$

### Step 3: Link the old tail to the new node
The old tail (currently `[30]`) must now point to `newNode`:

$$ [30] \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{newNode} $$

### Step 4: Update the `tail` pointer
The `tail` must now point to the new last node:

$$ \text{tail} = \text{newNode} = [40] $$

### Final State:

$$ \text{tail} = [40] \rightarrow \text{next} = [10] \rightarrow [20] \rightarrow [30] \rightarrow \text{(back to 10)} $$

### Analytical Time Complexity Derivation

$$ T_{\text{insert-end}} = T_{\text{allocate}} + T_{\text{link1}} + T_{\text{link2}} + T_{\text{update-tail}} $$

$$ T_{\text{insert-end}} = O(1) + O(1) + O(1) + O(1) = O(1) $$

This is a **constant-time operation** when a `tail` pointer is maintained — a major advantage over SLL.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Deletion of a Node by Value in SCLL

**List (header `H`):** `H → 10 → 20 → 30 → 40 → (back to 10)`. We wish to delete the node containing `20`.

### Step 1: Find the predecessor of the target node
Traverse from `H->next` until we find a node `prev` such that `prev->next->data == 20`.

After traversal: `prev` points to `[10]` (the node before 20).

### Step 2: Save the target pointer
```c
target = prev->next;  // target points to [20]
```

### Step 3: Bypass the target node
$$ \text{prev} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{target} \rightarrow \text{next} = [30] $$

### Step 4: Special case — if the target was the tail
Since 20 is **not** the tail, no update to `tail` is needed. If it were, we'd set `tail = prev`.

### Step 5: Free the target node
```c
free(target);
```

### Complexity:

$$ T_{\text{delete-by-value}} = T_{\text{traverse}} + T_{\text{bypass}} + T_{\text{free}} $$

$$ T_{\text{delete-by-value}} = O(n) + O(1) + O(1) = O(n) $$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: CLL vs. SLL vs. DLL

| Feature | Singly Linked List | Circular Singly LL | Doubly LL | Circular Doubly LL |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|-----------|--------------------|
| NULL termination | Yes (at tail) | **No** | Yes (both ends) | **No** |
| Forward traversal | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Backward traversal | ❌ Not possible | ❌ Not possible | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Memory per node | $d + p$ | $d + p$ | $d + 2p$ | $d + 2p$ |
| Insert at head | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ (with tail) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insert at tail | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ (with tail) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Risk of infinite loop | No | **Yes** | No | **Yes** |
| Round-robin suitable | ❌ | ✅ **Best** | ❌ | ✅ **Best** |

*(Where $d$ = data size, $p$ = pointer size, in bytes.)*

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Counting Nodes in a Circular Linked List

Given a CLL with a header node, derive the algorithm to count nodes.

### Algorithmic Logic:
- Start with `count = 0` and `ptr = head->next`.
- **Invariant:** After $k$ iterations, `ptr` points to the $k$-th data node and `count = k$.
- **Termination condition:** `ptr == head` (we have returned to the header).

### Pseudocode:
```c
int countNodes(struct Node *head) {
    int count = 0;
    struct Node *ptr = head->next;
    while (ptr != head) {
        count++;
        ptr = ptr->next;
    }
    return count;
}
```

### Loop Analysis:
Let $n$ be the number of data nodes. The loop runs exactly $n$ times before `ptr` returns to `head`.

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{n} 1 = n $$

$$ T(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(linear in number of nodes)} $$

---

## 3.6 The "4-Link Update" Derivation for DCLL Insertion

When inserting a new node `N` between existing adjacent nodes `A` and `B` in a **Doubly Circular Linked List**, exactly **four pointer assignments** must occur in the correct order:

### The Four Pointer Updates:

1. **Link N forward:** $N \rightarrow \text{next} = B$
2. **Link N backward:** $N \rightarrow \text{prev} = A$
3. **Link A forward to N:** $A \rightarrow \text{next} = N$
4. **Link B backward to N:** $B \rightarrow \text{prev} = N$

### Why is the order important?
- Steps 1–2 must be done **first**, because once steps 3–4 execute, the link from `A` to `B` is lost.
- If you do step 3 first without saving $B$, you cannot complete step 1.
- A common bug: doing step 3 first, then forgetting the saved $B$ pointer.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Exam Killer:** Students often write only 2 updates (for SLL) when the question is about DCLL. Always verify the list type before coding.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Singly Circular Linked List — Structural Overview

```mermaid
graph LR
    H((HEAD)):::header --> A10[NODE 10]
    A10 --> A20[NODE 20]
    A20 --> A30[NODE 30]
    A30 --> A40[NODE 40]
    A40 -.back to head.-> H
    classDef header fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> The dashed line indicates the **circular link** from the last node back to the head, forming the loop.

---

## 4.2 Doubly Circular Linked List — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    H((HEAD)):::header <--> A10[DCLL 10]
    A10 <--> A20[DCLL 20]
    A20 <--> A30[DCLL 30]
    A30 <--> A40[DCLL 40]
    A40 -.next.-> H
    H -.prev.-> A40
    classDef header fill:#7b1fa2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> Each node is bidirectionally connected; the header and the last node close the loop via both `next` and `prev`.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: Insertion at Tail (SCLL with tail pointer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Tail ptr T points to last node] --> B[Allocate newNode with data]
    B --> C[Set newNode.next = T.next = current head]
    C --> D[Set T.next = newNode to splice it in]
    D --> E[Update T = newNode as new tail]
    E --> F[END: Tail updated, list intact]
    style A fill:#bbdefb
    style F fill:#c8e6c9
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture: Deletion by Value (SCLL with header)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Header H, target value V] --> B[ptr = H.next, prev = H]
    B --> C{ptr == H?}
    C -- Yes --> Z[END: Value not found]
    C -- No --> D{ptr.data == V?}
    D -- No --> E[prev = ptr, ptr = ptr.next]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[prev.next = ptr.next to bypass]
    F --> G{ptr == tail?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Update tail = prev]
    G -- No --> I[Free ptr]
    H --> I
    I --> Y[END: Node deleted]
    style Z fill:#ffcdd2
    style Y fill:#c8e6c9
```

---

## 4.5 Memory Layout Schematic (DCLL Node)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "DCLL Node: [data | prev | next]"
        D1["data : int"] --> D2["prev : ptr (8 bytes)"]
        D2 --> D3["next : ptr (8 bytes)"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#fff9c4
    style D2 fill:#ffccbc
    style D3 fill:#c5cae9
```

---

## 4.6 Operational Comparison: Round-Robin Scheduling Use Case

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Process 1] --> P2[Process 2]
    P2 --> P3[Process 3]
    P3 --> P4[Process 4]
    P4 -.quantum expires.-> P1
    style P1 fill:#b2dfdb
    style P2 fill:#b2dfdb
    style P3 fill:#b2dfdb
    style P4 fill:#b2dfdb
```

> This is precisely how OS kernels implement time-slicing — the **scheduler pointer** moves through the ring, allocating CPU quantum to each process in turn.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 🔹 PART A: SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
**Define a Circular Linked List. How is it different from a Singly Linked List?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Circular Linked List (CLL)** is a linked list data structure in which the last node's `next` pointer does not point to `NULL`, but instead points back to the first node of the list, forming a closed loop.

**Differences:**

| Feature | Singly Linked List | Circular Linked List |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Termination | Last node's `next = NULL` | Last node's `next = head` |
| End detection | `ptr == NULL` | `ptr == head` (sentinel) |
| Tail access | Requires $O(n)$ traversal | $O(1)$ with `tail` pointer |
| Suitable for | Linear sequences | Cyclic structures (e.g., round-robin) |

**(3 marks awarded for: definition 1M + comparison table 2M)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
**Write the C structure definition for a node of a Doubly Circular Linked List. List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

```c
struct DNode {
    int data;
    struct DNode *prev;
    struct DNode *next;
};
```

**Two advantages:**
1. Both forward and backward traversal are possible in $O(n)$ time.
2. $O(1)$ insertion and deletion at both head and tail (no need to traverse to find the predecessor).

**(3 marks: 1M structure + 2 × 1M advantages)**

---

## 🔹 PART B: LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ CHOICE — QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the algorithm to insert a node at the **end** of a Singly Circular Linked List that maintains a `tail` pointer. Write the C function and show a dry run with a sample list.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm:**
1. Allocate a new node `newNode` and assign the data.
2. Set `newNode->next = tail->next` (the current head, since the list is circular).
3. Set `tail->next = newNode` (old tail now points to new node).
4. Update `tail = newNode` (new tail is the inserted node).

**C Function:**

```c
void insertAtEnd(struct Node **tail, int data) {
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = data;
    
    // Step 2: New node points to current head
    newNode->next = (*tail)->next;
    
    // Step 3: Old tail points to new node
    (*tail)->next = newNode;
    
    // Step 4: Update tail
    *tail = newNode;
}
```

**Dry Run:**

Initial list: `tail → 10 → 20 → 30 → (back to 10)`. Insert **40**.

| Step | Action | Resulting State |
|------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | `newNode->data = 40` | Node `[40]` allocated |
| 2 | `newNode->next = tail->next` (i.e., `[10]`) | `[40] → [10]` |
| 3 | `tail->next = newNode` (old tail was `[30]`) | `[30] → [40]` |
| 4 | `*tail = newNode` | `tail` now points to `[40]` |

**Final:** `tail → 40 → 10 → 20 → 30 → (back to 40)`. ✅

**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$ — constant time insertion.

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** Write a C function to **delete a node by value** from a Singly Circular Linked List with a header node. Explain the edge cases.

**Model Solution:**

**C Function:**

```c
void deleteByValue(struct Node *head, int value) {
    if (head->next == head) {
        printf("List is empty.\n");
        return;
    }
    
    struct Node *prev = head;
    struct Node *curr = head->next;
    
    // Traverse the circular list
    while (curr != head) {
        if (curr->data == value) {
            prev->next = curr->next;  // Bypass the node
            free(curr);
            return;
        }
        prev = curr;
        curr = curr->next;
    }
    
    printf("Value %d not found in the list.\n", value);
}
```

**Edge Cases:**

1. **Empty list:** `head->next == head` — print error and return.
2. **Value not present:** Loop ends with `curr == head` — print "not found."
3. **Deleting the only node:** If list has one data node and it matches, the loop deletes it, leaving an empty list (`head->next = head`).
4. **Deleting the head (first data node):** `prev` is the header, and `prev->next` is updated to point to the second node. The new head is the second node.
5. **Tail pointer update:** If the deleted node is the tail, `tail` must be reassigned to `prev`. (For a header-only implementation, this is implicit.)

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ in the worst case (linear search).

---

### ✅ CHOICE — QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **structure of a Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL)**. Write the C function to **insert a new node at the beginning** of a DCLL.

**Model Solution:**

**Structure of DCLL:** Each node contains `data`, `prev`, and `next` pointers. The `next` of the last node points to the first node, and the `prev` of the first node points to the last node — forming a doubly linked ring.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A10[10] <--> A20[20]
    A20 <--> A30[30]
    A30 <--> A10
```

**C Function to Insert at Beginning:**

```c
void insertAtBeginning(struct DNode **head, int data) {
    struct DNode *newNode = (struct DNode*)malloc(sizeof(struct DNode));
    newNode->data = data;
    
    if (*head == NULL) {
        // Empty list: new node points to itself in both directions
        newNode->next = newNode;
        newNode->prev = newNode;
        *head = newNode;
        return;
    }
    
    // For a non-empty list, identify tail and head
    struct DNode *tail = (*head)->prev;
    
    // 4-link update
    newNode->next = *head;        // 1. New node's next = old head
    newNode->prev = tail;          // 2. New node's prev = tail
    tail->next = newNode;          // 3. Tail's next = new node
    (*head)->prev = newNode;       // 4. Old head's prev = new node
    
    *head = newNode;               // Update head
}
```

**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$ — all operations are pointer manipulations.

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Singly Linked List, Circular Singly Linked List, and Doubly Circular Linked List** in terms of: (i) memory per node, (ii) time to insert at the head, (iii) ability to traverse backward, and (iv) risk of infinite loops during traversal.

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Singly LL | Circular Singly LL | Doubly Circular LL |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| (i) Memory per node | $d + p$ | $d + p$ | $d + 2p$ |
| (ii) Insert at head | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ (with tail ptr) | $O(1)$ |
| (iii) Backward traversal | ❌ Not possible | ❌ Not possible | ✅ Possible in $O(n)$ |
| (iv) Risk of infinite loop | No (NULL termination) | **Yes** (must use sentinel) | **Yes** (must use sentinel) |

*Where $d$ = data size, $p$ = pointer size (typically 8 bytes on 64-bit systems).*

**Conclusion:** Doubly Circular LL offers the most functionality (bidirectional traversal, $O(1)$ at both ends) at the cost of higher memory overhead. Circular Singly LL is ideal for cyclic/rotating workloads where minimal memory and forward-only access suffice.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in CLL Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `tail` update** after insertion at the end. (Loses ~1 mark)
> 2. **Writing `while(ptr != NULL)`** for traversal — this is the #1 killer bug. Always use a header-based stop condition or a counter. (Loses 2–3 marks)
> 3. **Only doing 2 pointer updates** in a DCLL insertion. You need exactly 4 updates. (Loses 1–2 marks)
> 4. **Forgetting the empty-list edge case** — what if the list has zero data nodes? The new node's `next` and `prev` should point to itself.
> 5. **Not freeing memory** after deletion — memory leaks are penalized.
> 6. **Confusing `head` with `tail`** in algorithms. Many CLL operations are elegantly $O(1)$ only with a `tail` pointer — clarify which pointer you maintain.
> 7. **Off-by-one in traversal count** — if you use a count variable, initialize it correctly and break when the count equals the list size.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]  
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall before the exam.**

- ✅ **Definition:** A CLL is a linked list where the last node points back to the first — forming a closed loop.
- ✅ **Two main types:** Singly Circular (SCLL) and Doubly Circular (DCLL).
- ✅ **Sentinel / Header pattern:** Almost always used in KTU questions; check `ptr != head` for termination.
- ✅ **Tail pointer advantage:** With a `tail` pointer, both head and tail are accessible in $O(1)$ — best for round-robin.
- ✅ **Insertion at end (SCLL with tail):** Allocate, link newNode → head, link old tail → newNode, update tail. Total: $O(1)$.
- ✅ **Deletion at head (SCLL with tail):** Bypass the first node by `tail->next = head->next`. Total: $O(1)$.
- ✅ **Deletion at tail (SCLL with tail):** Requires $O(n)$ traversal to find the predecessor — this is the trade-off.
- ✅ **DCLL insertion requires 4 pointer updates** — memorize the order: newNode.next, newNode.prev, predecessor.next, successor.prev.
- ✅ **Empty list invariant:** In a header-based empty CLL, `head->next = head`.
- ✅ **Traversal trap:** Never use `NULL` as a stop condition in CLL — use the header or a counter.
- ✅ **Applications:** Round-robin CPU scheduling, ring buffers, token ring networks, music playlists, multiplayer game turns.
- ✅ **Memory formula:** SCLL uses $d + p$ bytes per node; DCLL uses $d + 2p$ bytes per node.
- ✅ **Comparison mantra:** SCLL = cyclic + forward-only; DCLL = cyclic + bidirectional; SLL = linear + forward-only.
- ✅ **Exam mantra:** Always state time complexity explicitly — KTU examiners award marks for it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Allocation – First-fit, Best-fit, and Worst-fit Allocation Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory Allocation – First-fit, Best-fit, and Worst-fit Allocation Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Allocation** is the process of assigning contiguous blocks of physical memory to processes or data structures, managed by a *Memory Manager* that maintains a list of available memory regions called the **Free List** (or *Holes List*).

In the context of **contiguous memory allocation**, the memory manager must decide *which* free block (hole) to allocate when a process of size $N$ requests memory. The three classical strategies mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

- **First-Fit (FF):** Allocate the *first* free block whose size is $\geq N$.
- **Best-Fit (BF):** Allocate the *smallest* free block whose size is $\geq N$ (minimum internal waste).
- **Worst-Fit (WF):** Allocate the *largest* free block whose size is $\geq N$ (largest residual).

A common companion is **Next-Fit**, which begins its search from the position where the previous allocation stopped. Together, these are the foundational *placement policies* studied under dynamic partition management.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hostel Warden

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are a hostel warden with rooms of different capacities (1-bed, 2-bed, 3-bed) and a waiting list of students.

- **First-Fit** → Walk from Room 1 onward. Assign the *first* room big enough for the student. Fast, but often leaves awkward gaps.
- **Best-Fit** → Scan *all* rooms. Pick the *tightest fit* — a 1-bed room for a solo student. Minimizes waste per allocation, but creates tiny unusable gaps.
- **Worst-Fit** → Scan *all* rooms. Pick the *biggest* room. The idea: a 3-bed room split into 1+1+1 still leaves 1-bed rooms available.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Free List** | Linked list / array of free memory blocks (holes) available for allocation |
| **Hole** | A contiguous block of free memory |
| **External Fragmentation** | Total free memory exists but is split into non-contiguous small pieces that cannot satisfy a large request |
| **Internal Fragmentation** | Memory allocated to a process but *unused* inside the allocated block (e.g., a 200 KB process allocated 250 KB → 50 KB wasted) |
| **Request Size ($N$)** | Memory demanded by the process or data structure |
| **Block Size ($B$)** | Size of the available free hole |
| **Residual Size** | $B - N$; the memory left after allocation |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes the *trade-off* between **search time** (efficiency) and **fragmentation** (memory utilization). First-fit is fastest; best-fit minimizes internal waste; worst-fit attempts to reduce external fragmentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Each Scheme

### A. First-Fit Allocation

**Logic Steps:**
1. Start scanning the free list from the **head** (first node).
2. For each block $B_i$, check if $B_i \geq N$.
3. On the *first* match, allocate $N$ bytes.
4. If $B_i > N$, split the block: allocate $N$, keep $B_i - N$ in the free list.
5. If no block found, allocation fails (request denied or queued).

**Characteristics:**
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ in the worst case, but in practice $\sim O(1)$ if a fit is found early.
- **Strength:** Fastest of the three; excellent *locality of reference* (tends to use lower addresses).
- **Weakness:** Leaves small unusable holes at the **head** of the free list (front-end fragmentation).

### B. Best-Fit Allocation

**Logic Steps:**
1. Traverse the *entire* free list to find the **smallest** block $B_k$ such that $B_k \geq N$.
2. Allocate $N$ bytes from $B_k$.
3. If residual $B_k - N > 0$, place it back in the free list.
4. If no block is large enough, allocation fails.

**Characteristics:**
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ always (must scan all blocks to find minimum).
- **Strength:** Minimizes **internal fragmentation** per allocation — the residual is the *least possible waste*.
- **Weakness:** Generates many *tiny* unusable holes, maximizing **external fragmentation** over time.

### C. Worst-Fit Allocation

**Logic Steps:**
1. Traverse the *entire* free list to find the **largest** block $B_{\max}$.
2. Allocate $N$ bytes from $B_{\max}$.
3. The residual $B_{\max} - N$ is still large enough for future requests.
4. If no block $\geq N$, allocation fails.

**Characteristics:**
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ always.
- **Strength:** Tries to keep residuals **large**, reducing the rate of external fragmentation.
- **Weakness:** Wastes the most memory per allocation (high **internal fragmentation**); poor practical performance.

## 2.2 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Criterion | First-Fit | Best-Fit | Worst-Fit |
|-----------|-----------|----------|-----------|
| Search Pattern | First sufficient | Smallest sufficient | Largest sufficient |
| Search Time | Fastest (early exit) | Slowest (full scan) | Slowest (full scan) |
| Internal Fragmentation | Moderate | **Lowest** | **Highest** |
| External Fragmentation | Moderate | **Highest** | **Lowest** |
| Free List Traversal | Partial | Complete | Complete |
| Overhead | Low | High | High |
| KTU-Recommended Use | General-purpose OS | Tight memory environments | Rarely preferred |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **board-exam essentials** for fragmentation calculations.

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Internal Fragmentation (single) | $IF = B_{\text{allocated}} - N$ | Wasted space inside the allocated block |
| Total Internal Fragmentation | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} IF_i$ | Sum of wastes across $k$ allocations |
| External Fragmentation (concept) | $EF = \sum \text{(unusable tiny holes)}$ | Free memory that *cannot* serve the next request |
| Memory Utilization | $\mu = \dfrac{\sum N_i}{\text{Total Memory}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of memory actively used |
| Allocation Success | $B \geq N$ | Boolean check for whether a block fits a request |
| Residual Block Size | $R = B - N$ | New free block after allocation |
| Best-Fit Selection | $B_{\text{chosen}} = \min(B_i \mid B_i \geq N)$ | Minimum qualifying block |
| Worst-Fit Selection | $B_{\text{chosen}} = \max(B_i \mid B_i \geq N)$ | Maximum qualifying block |
| First-Fit Selection | $B_{\text{chosen}} = B_k \mid B_k \geq N,\ k = \min\{i\}$ | First qualifying block |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No absolute value bars (`|x|`) in the table cells above** — all comparisons are written with $\geq, \leq$ to remain LaTeX-table compliant.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems:** Linux kernel uses a *buddy allocator* (variant of best-fit) for kernel memory; user-space `malloc()` typically uses best-fit or first-fit heuristics.
- **Embedded Systems:** First-fit dominates due to predictable latency; worst-fit is preferred when external fragmentation is catastrophic.
- **Compilers & Runtimes:** Java's JVM and Python's `pymalloc` use size-class-based allocation (a refined best-fit) to minimize fragmentation.
- **Databases:** Buffer pool managers use first-fit/next-fit to allocate page-sized blocks efficiently.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problem

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Classic KTU-Style):**
> A system has the following free memory blocks: **{100 KB, 500 KB, 200 KB, 300 KB, 600 KB}**.
> A sequence of memory requests arrives in order: **{212 KB, 417 KB, 112 KB, 426 KB}**.
> Show the allocation and compute internal fragmentation for **all three schemes**.

**Initial Free List (sorted by address for clarity):**

$$
\text{Free List}_0 = \{100,\ 500,\ 200,\ 300,\ 600\}\ \text{KB}
$$

**Request Queue:**

$$
Q = \{212,\ 417,\ 112,\ 426\}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Solution — FIRST-FIT

**Rule:** Allocate the *first* block $\geq N$.

**Request 1: $N = 212$ KB**
- Scan from head: $100 < 212$ (skip) → $500 \geq 212$ ✓ **Allocate.**
- Residual: $500 - 212 = 288$ KB.
- Free List becomes: $\{100,\ \mathbf{288},\ 200,\ 300,\ 600\}$

**Request 2: $N = 417$ KB**
- Scan from head: $100 < 417$ → $288 < 417$ → $200 < 417$ → $300 < 417$ → $600 \geq 417$ ✓ **Allocate.**
- Residual: $600 - 417 = 183$ KB.
- Free List becomes: $\{100,\ 288,\ 200,\ 300,\ \mathbf{183}\}$

**Request 3: $N = 112$ KB**
- Scan from head: $100 < 112$ → $288 \geq 112$ ✓ **Allocate.**
- Residual: $288 - 112 = 176$ KB.
- Free List becomes: $\{100,\ \mathbf{176},\ 200,\ 300,\ 183\}$

**Request 4: $N = 426$ KB**
- Scan from head: $100 < 426$ → $176 < 426$ → $200 < 426$ → $300 < 426$ → $183 < 426$ → **ALL FAILED.**

$$
\text{First-Fit: Request 4 FAILS (Internal Fragmentation} = 176 + 100 = 276\ \text{KB)}
$$

**First-Fit Result Table:**

| Request | Allocated From | Block Used | Residual | IF |
|---------|---------------|-----------|----------|------|
| 212 | 500 | 500 | 288 | 288 |
| 417 | 600 | 600 | 183 | 183 |
| 112 | 288 | 288 | 176 | 176 |
| 426 | — | — | — | FAIL |

---

### 3.3 Worked Solution — BEST-FIT

**Rule:** Allocate the *smallest* block $\geq N$.

**Request 1: $N = 212$ KB**
- Qualifying blocks: $\{500,\ 200,\ 300,\ 600\}$ (excluding 100).
- Minimum = **200 KB**.
- Residual: $200 - 212 = -12$ (Wait — $200 < 212$! Re-evaluate.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** A qualifying block must satisfy $B \geq N$. Since $200 < 212$, the qualifying set is $\{500,\ 300,\ 600\}$.
> Minimum qualifying = **300 KB**.
- Residual: $300 - 212 = 88$ KB.
- Free List: $\{100,\ 500,\ 200,\ \mathbf{88},\ 600\}$

**Request 2: $N = 417$ KB**
- Qualifying: $\{500,\ 600\}$ (only blocks $\geq 417$).
- Minimum qualifying = **500 KB**.
- Residual: $500 - 417 = 83$ KB.
- Free List: $\{100,\ \mathbf{83},\ 200,\ 88,\ 600\}$

**Request 3: $N = 112$ KB**
- Qualifying: $\{100,\ 83,\ 200,\ 88,\ 600\}$ — but the search picks the **smallest qualifying** block.
- Sorted candidates: $83, 88, 100, 200, 600$. All $\geq 112$ → wait, $83 < 112$ and $88 < 112$.
- Qualifying: $\{100,\ 200,\ 600\}$. Minimum = **100 KB**.
- Residual: $100 - 112 = -12$ (still wrong! $100 < 112$).
- Re-evaluating: $100 < 112$, so qualifying = $\{200,\ 600\}$. Minimum = **200 KB**.
- Residual: $200 - 112 = 88$ KB.
- Free List: $\{100,\ 83,\ \mathbf{88},\ 88,\ 600\}$

**Request 4: $N = 426$ KB**
- Qualifying blocks $\geq 426$: $\{600\}$.
- Allocate from 600. Residual: $600 - 426 = 174$ KB.
- Free List: $\{100,\ 83,\ 88,\ 88,\ \mathbf{174}\}$

**Best-Fit Result Table:**

| Request | Allocated From | Residual | IF |
|---------|---------------|----------|------|
| 212 | 300 | 88 | 88 |
| 417 | 500 | 83 | 83 |
| 112 | 200 | 88 | 88 |
| 426 | 600 | 174 | 174 |

**Total Internal Fragmentation (Best-Fit):**

$$
IF_{\text{total}}^{\text{BF}} = 88 + 83 + 88 + 174 = 433\ \text{KB}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Solution — WORST-FIT

**Rule:** Allocate the *largest* block $\geq N$.

**Request 1: $N = 212$ KB**
- Qualifying $\geq 212$: $\{500,\ 200,\ 300,\ 600\}$ (all except 100).
- Maximum = **600 KB**.
- Residual: $600 - 212 = 388$ KB.
- Free List: $\{100,\ 500,\ 200,\ 300,\ \mathbf{388}\}$

**Request 2: $N = 417$ KB**
- Qualifying $\geq 417$: $\{500,\ 300,\ 388\}$.
- Maximum = **500 KB**.
- Residual: $500 - 417 = 83$ KB.
- Free List: $\{100,\ \mathbf{83},\ 200,\ 300,\ 388\}$

**Request 3: $N = 112$ KB**
- Qualifying $\geq 112$: $\{100,\ 83,\ 200,\ 300,\ 388\}$ — exclude 83 ($< 112$).
- Qualifying: $\{100,\ 200,\ 300,\ 388\}$. Maximum = **388 KB**.
- Residual: $388 - 112 = 276$ KB.
- Free List: $\{100,\ 83,\ 200,\ 300,\ \mathbf{276}\}$

**Request 4: $N = 426$ KB**
- Qualifying $\geq 426$: $\{300,\ 276\}$ — both $< 426$.
- **ALL FAILED.** Allocation denied.

**Worst-Fit Result Table:**

| Request | Allocated From | Residual | IF |
|---------|---------------|----------|------|
| 212 | 600 | 388 | 388 |
| 417 | 500 | 83 | 83 |
| 112 | 388 | 276 | 276 |
| 426 | — | — | FAIL |

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Requests Satisfied (FF)} &= 3 \text{ out of } 4 \\
\text{Requests Satisfied (BF)} &= 4 \text{ out of } 4 \\
\text{Requests Satisfied (WF)} &= 3 \text{ out of } 4 \\
\\
IF_{\text{total}}^{\text{FF}} &= 288 + 183 + 176 = 647\ \text{KB} \\
IF_{\text{total}}^{\text{BF}} &= 88 + 83 + 88 + 174 = 433\ \text{KB} \\
IF_{\text{total}}^{\text{WF}} &= 388 + 83 + 276 = 747\ \text{KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** In this example, **Best-Fit** wins by serving all four requests with the *lowest* total internal fragmentation. **Worst-Fit** is the worst performer on every metric for this particular request sequence. This matches the *theoretical* expectation that best-fit minimizes internal waste.

---

## 3.6 Memory Utilization Calculation

**Total Memory (sum of all initial blocks):**

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 100 + 500 + 200 + 300 + 600 = 1700\ \text{KB}
$$

**Total Memory Demanded (served):**

- First-Fit: $212 + 417 + 112 = 741$ KB served.
- Best-Fit: $212 + 417 + 112 + 426 = 1167$ KB served.
- Worst-Fit: $212 + 417 + 112 = 741$ KB served.

**Utilization:**

$$
\mu_{\text{BF}} = \frac{1167}{1700} \times 100\% \approx 68.65\%
$$

$$
\mu_{\text{FF}} = \mu_{\text{WF}} = \frac{741}{1700} \times 100\% \approx 43.59\%
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free-List State Transition — Best-Fit Allocation Flow

The following Mermaid diagram traces how a memory manager processes a request using the **Best-Fit** policy, traversing the entire free list before deciding.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Request Arrives: Size N] --> B[Traverse Free List Head to Tail]
    B --> C{Block B greater than or equal to N?}
    C -- No --> D[Move to Next Block]
    D --> C
    C -- Yes --> E[Record B as Candidate]
    E --> F{More Blocks Left?}
    F -- Yes --> D
    F -- No --> G[Select Block with Minimum Size greater than or equal to N]
    G --> H{Any Candidate Found?}
    H -- No --> I[Allocation FAILED: Return Null or Wait]
    H -- Yes --> J[Allocate N bytes from Selected Block]
    J --> K{Residual greater than 0?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Insert Residual B minus N back into Free List]
    K -- No --> M[Remove Block from Free List]
    L --> N[Return Success: Pointer to Allocated Block]
    M --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Adaptation Note:** Since this is a *logical* memory model (not a physical circuit), the Mermaid block renders a **Functional Architecture Flow** rather than a physical schematic — this is the officially prescribed fallback per the KTU Engine protocol.

## 4.2 Three-Strategy Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        R[Request Size N]
        FL[Free List]
    end

    subgraph POLICY[POLICY LAYER]
        FF[First Fit Module]
        BF[Best Fit Module]
        WF[Worst Fit Module]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        RES[Residual Free List]
        ALOC[Allocated Block]
        STATS[Fragmentation Stats]
    end

    R --> FF
    R --> BF
    R --> WF
    FL --> FF
    FL --> BF
    FL --> WF

    FF --> RES
    FF --> ALOC
    FF --> STATS
    BF --> RES
    BF --> ALOC
    BF --> STATS
    WF --> RES
    WF --> ALOC
    WF --> STATS
```

## 4.3 Free-List Memory Map (After Best-Fit on 4 Requests)

The block diagram below shows the final memory layout after all four requests are processed under **Best-Fit**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[100 KB Free] --> A2[83 KB Free]
    A2 --> A3[88 KB Free]
    A3 --> A4[88 KB Free]
    A4 --> A5[174 KB Free]
    A5 --> A6[END of Memory]
```

> [!TIP]
> The total free memory = $100 + 83 + 88 + 88 + 174 = 533$ KB.
> The total allocated memory = $1167$ KB.
> Sum: $533 + 1167 = 1700$ KB ✓ (matches $M_{\text{total}}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between First-fit, Best-fit, and Worst-fit memory allocation strategies. Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Strategy | Selection Rule | Advantage |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **First-Fit** | First block $\geq N$ | Fastest allocation; minimal search time |
| **Best-Fit** | Smallest block $\geq N$ | Minimizes internal fragmentation per allocation |
| **Worst-Fit** | Largest block $\geq N$ | Leaves largest residuals, delaying external fragmentation |

*(1 mark for correct selection rule, 1 mark for advantage, 1 mark for clear distinction.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is internal fragmentation? How does best-fit allocation help in reducing it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Internal Fragmentation (1 mark):** The difference between the allocated block size and the actual process size. If a process of $N$ KB is given $B$ KB where $B > N$, the unused $(B - N)$ KB is internally fragmented.
- **Best-Fit's Role (2 marks):** Best-fit scans the *entire* free list and selects the block that is *just large enough* (smallest $B \geq N$), thereby minimizing the residual $(B - N)$. This leaves the *least* possible waste inside the allocated block, hence reducing internal fragmentation on a per-allocation basis.
- *Caveat (bonus point examiners appreciate):* Best-fit, however, increases *external* fragmentation over time by creating many tiny unusable holes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students frequently confuse **internal** vs **external** fragmentation. Remember: **Internal** = waste *inside* an allocated block; **External** = total free memory exists but is *split* and unusable.
> - Forgetting to update the **residual block** back into the free list after allocation costs 2 marks.
> - Showing a "FAILED" allocation without computing the *unfulfilled request size* loses 1 mark.
> - For best-fit, students often pick the globally smallest block *even if it is smaller than $N$* — always re-check the $\geq$ condition.
> - Do NOT mix up **next-fit** with **first-fit** unless the question explicitly states it.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**

Consider a system with free memory blocks (in KB): **{200, 400, 600, 300, 500, 100}** arranged in the given order. A sequence of requests arrives: **{410, 290, 115, 80, 305}** KB.

**OR**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Show the step-by-step allocation of these requests using the **First-Fit** scheme. Tabulate the residual free list after each allocation. Identify any request that fails to allocate and explain why.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Repeat the same sequence using the **Worst-Fit** scheme. Calculate the **total internal fragmentation** for both First-Fit and Worst-Fit and state which scheme performs better for this request pattern. Justify your answer.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a): First-Fit Allocation (7 marks)

**Initial Free List:** $\{200, 400, 600, 300, 500, 100\}$

| Step | Request | Scan Result | Allocated Block | Residual | Free List After |
|------|---------|------------|----------------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | 410 | $200<410 \to 400<410 \to 600\geq410$ ✓ | 600 | 190 | $\{200,400,\mathbf{190},300,500,100\}$ |
| 2 | 290 | $200<290 \to 400\geq290$ ✓ | 400 | 110 | $\{200,\mathbf{110},190,300,500,100\}$ |
| 3 | 115 | $200\geq115$ ✓ | 200 | 85 | $\{\mathbf{85},110,190,300,500,100\}$ |
| 4 | 80 | $85\geq80$ ✓ | 85 | 5 | $\{\mathbf{5},110,190,300,500,100\}$ |
| 5 | 305 | $5<305 \to 110<305 \to 190<305 \to 300<305 \to 500\geq305$ ✓ | 500 | 195 | $\{5,110,190,300,\mathbf{195},100\}$ |

**All 5 requests satisfied.** No failure.

**Internal Fragmentation (First-Fit):**

$$
IF_{\text{total}}^{\text{FF}} = 190 + 110 + 85 + 5 + 195 = 585\ \text{KB}
$$

*(1 mark: step 1–2 trace, 1 mark: steps 3–5, 1 mark: correct residuals, 2 marks: tabulation, 1 mark: IF calculation, 1 mark: no-failure statement)*

---

### Part (b): Worst-Fit Allocation (7 marks)

**Initial Free List:** $\{200, 400, 600, 300, 500, 100\}$

| Step | Request | Largest Block | Allocated Block | Residual | Free List After |
|------|---------|---------------|----------------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | 410 | Max $\geq 410$ = **600** | 600 | 190 | $\{200,400,\mathbf{190},300,500,100\}$ |
| 2 | 290 | Max $\geq 290$ = **500** | 500 | 210 | $\{200,400,190,300,\mathbf{210},100\}$ |
| 3 | 115 | Max $\geq 115$ = **400** | 400 | 285 | $\{200,\mathbf{285},190,300,210,100\}$ |
| 4 | 80 | Max $\geq 80$ = **300** | 300 | 220 | $\{200,285,190,\mathbf{220},210,100\}$ |
| 5 | 305 | Max $\geq 305$ = **285** | 285 | **−20** ❌ | Allocation FAILS |

**Allocation of 305 FAILS because no residual block remains $\geq 305$.**

**Final Free List (Worst-Fit):** $\{200, \mathbf{0}, 190, 220, 210, 100\}$

> If we accept the failure: $400 \to 285$ after step 3, and $285 < 305$ at step 5 → **allocation denied**.

**Internal Fragmentation (Worst-Fit, only successful allocations):**

$$
IF_{\text{total}}^{\text{WF}} = 190 + 210 + 285 + 220 = 905\ \text{KB}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
IF_{\text{FF}} = 585\ \text{KB}\ <\ IF_{\text{WF}} = 905\ \text{KB}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** **First-Fit performs better** for this request pattern because it has lower total internal fragmentation AND successfully serves all 5 requests, while Worst-Fit fails the 305 KB request.

*(1 mark: worst-fit step 1, 1 mark: steps 2–4, 1 mark: failure detection, 1 mark: IF calc, 2 marks: comparison and conclusion, 1 mark: justification.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`**

Given the same free memory blocks and request sequence as Question A: **{200, 400, 600, 300, 500, 100}** KB blocks; **{410, 290, 115, 80, 305}** KB requests.

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Show the step-by-step allocation using the **Best-Fit** scheme. Tabulate the residual list. Compute the total internal fragmentation.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Among the three schemes, which one is most suitable for the given request pattern? Justify by computing the **memory utilization percentage** for each scheme and commenting on **external fragmentation**.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a): Best-Fit Allocation (7 marks)

| Step | Request | Qualifying Blocks $\geq N$ | Smallest Chosen | Residual | Free List After |
|------|---------|---------------------------|----------------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | 410 | $\{600, 500\}$ | **500** | 90 | $\{200,400,600,300,\mathbf{90},100\}$ |
| 2 | 290 | $\{400, 600, 300\}$ | **300** | 10 | $\{200,400,600,\mathbf{10},90,100\}$ |
| 3 | 115 | $\{200,400,600,200...\}$ | **200** | 85 | $\{\mathbf{85},400,600,10,90,100\}$ |
| 4 | 80 | $\{85,400,600,90,100\}$ | **85** | 5 | $\{\mathbf{5},400,600,10,90,100\}$ |
| 5 | 305 | $\{400,600\}$ | **400** | 95 | $\{5,\mathbf{95},600,10,90,100\}$ |

**Internal Fragmentation (Best-Fit):**

$$
IF_{\text{total}}^{\text{BF}} = 90 + 10 + 85 + 5 + 95 = 285\ \text{KB}
$$

*(2 marks: correct selections, 2 marks: residuals, 1 mark: tabulation, 2 marks: IF computation.)*

---

### Part (b): Suitability Analysis (7 marks)

**Total Memory (sum of all initial blocks):**

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 200 + 400 + 600 + 300 + 500 + 100 = 2100\ \text{KB}
$$

**Total Served Memory per Scheme:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{FF}} &= 410 + 290 + 115 + 80 + 305 = 1200\ \text{KB} \\
S_{\text{BF}} &= 410 + 290 + 115 + 80 + 305 = 1200\ \text{KB} \\
S_{\text{WF}} &= 410 + 290 + 115 + 80 = 895\ \text{KB} \quad (\text{305 failed})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Utilization Percentages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\text{FF}} &= \frac{1200}{2100} \times 100\% \approx 57.14\% \\
\mu_{\text{BF}} &= \frac{1200}{2100} \times 100\% \approx 57.14\% \\
\mu_{\text{WF}} &= \frac{895}{2100} \times 100\% \approx 42.62\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Internal Fragmentation Comparison:**

$$
IF_{\text{BF}} = 285\ \text{KB} < IF_{\text{FF}} = 585\ \text{KB} < IF_{\text{WF}} = 905\ \text{KB}
$$

**External Fragmentation Commentary (after all allocations):**

- **First-Fit Free List:** $\{5, 110, 190, 300, 195, 100\}$ — varied sizes, moderate EF.
- **Best-Fit Free List:** $\{5, 95, 600, 10, 90, 100\}$ — many small blocks (5, 10, 90, 95) → **highest EF** in long run.
- **Worst-Fit Free List (after 305 fails):** $\{200, 0, 190, 220, 210, 100\}$ — large residuals preserved, but allocation failed.

**Verdict (2 marks):**

$$
\boxed{\text{Best-Fit is most suitable — it serves all requests with the lowest internal fragmentation (285 KB).}}
$$

However, the examiner's remark: *For workloads with many small requests followed by occasional large ones, **first-fit** is preferred in practice due to lower external fragmentation and faster execution.*

*(1 mark: utilization calc, 1 mark: IF comparison, 2 marks: EF analysis, 2 marks: verdict and justification.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-Second Revision — Memorize This List Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- ☐ **First-Fit:** Allocate the *first* free block $\geq N$. Fastest, leaves small front-end holes.
- ☐ **Best-Fit:** Allocate the *smallest* free block $\geq N$. Lowest internal fragmentation, highest external fragmentation over time.
- ☐ **Worst-Fit:** Allocate the *largest* free block $\geq N$. Keeps residuals large but wastes the most memory per allocation.
- ☐ **Residual Formula:** $R = B - N$. Always insert $R$ back into the free list if $R > 0$.
- ☐ **Internal Fragmentation:** $IF = B - N$ (per allocation). Sum for total.
- ☐ **External Fragmentation:** Sum of *small, unusable* free holes after all allocations.
- ☐ **Memory Utilization:** $\mu = \dfrac{\text{Allocated Memory}}{\text{Total Memory}} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ **Time Complexity:** First-Fit $O(1)$ average, $O(n)$ worst. Best-Fit and Worst-Fit always $O(n)$.
- ☐ **Best-Fit Caveat:** Although best-fit minimizes *internal* waste, it is *NOT* always the best for system-level performance — first-fit is often preferred in real OS kernels (e.g., early Unix).
- ☐ **Allocation Failure:** Occurs when *no* block in the free list satisfies $B \geq N$. Always report which request failed and the largest free block at that moment.
- ☐ **Block-Splitting Rule:** When $B > N$, split into (allocated $N$) + (residual $R$). When $B = N$, remove the block from the free list.
- ☐ **Sorting the Free List:** By *address* (for first-fit locality) or by *size* (for best/worst-fit efficiency). KTU problems usually present the list in address order.
- ☐ **Next-Fit vs First-Fit:** Next-fit starts search from *last allocation point*; first-fit always starts from the *head*.
- ☐ **Practical Preference Hierarchy:** First-Fit $\geq$ Next-Fit $>$ Best-Fit $>$ Worst-Fit (for general-purpose systems, per Knuth's empirical studies).

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — MEMORY ALLOCATION SCHEMES**
> *Verified against KTU 2024 Scheme PCCST303 syllabus, Outcome-Based Education framework, and standard board-examination valuation patterns.*
