topics.title: Garbage Collection and Compaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Garbage Collection and Compaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Garbage Collection (GC)** is the automated process of identifying and reclaiming memory blocks that are no longer reachable by any active pointer, reference, or root in a running program. In the context of KTU's PCCST303 Module 2 (Linked Lists and Memory Management), *garbage* specifically refers to heap-allocated memory nodes that have been logically deleted from a data structure but still occupy physical RAM, leading to a state known as a **dangling pointer** or **memory leak**.

**Compaction** (also called *defragmentation* or *coalescing*) is the subsequent process of physically relocating all live (in-use) memory blocks to one contiguous region of the heap, thereby consolidating all free memory into a single, large addressable block. This eliminates **external fragmentation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2, garbage collection is examined as a critical concern in *dynamic linked data structures* where `malloc()` / `free()` (in C) or `new` / `delete` (in C++) are used. Failure to handle garbage results in **memory leaks**, while failure to compact results in **fragmentation**.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **large office building with hundreds of lockers**:

- Each locker stores one employee's belongings (memory block).
- Employees come and go over time (allocation/deallocation).
- When an employee leaves the company, their locker is *logically* emptied (variable goes out of scope), but the physical locker still contains their old files (**garbage**).
- A **garbage collector** acts like a janitor who periodically walks through the building, checks which lockers are still being accessed (referenced), and clears the contents of lockers that nobody uses anymore.
- A **compactor** acts like a logistics manager who then shifts all the remaining occupied lockers to one side of the hallway, opening up one large empty stretch that can fit a big new delivery (a large allocation request that previously failed due to fragmentation).

### Key Terminology (KTU Standard Definitions)

| Term | Definition | Metric / Unit |
|------|------------|----------------|
| **Garbage** | Unreachable heap memory no longer needed by the program | Bytes |
| **Dangling Pointer** | A pointer that still holds the address of a freed memory block | Memory Address |
| **Memory Leak** | Accumulation of unreachable memory over program lifetime | Bytes leaked / sec |
| **Root Set** | All references directly accessible (globals, stack, registers) | Cardinality |
| **Live Object** | Any object reachable from the Root Set via a chain of references | Boolean |
| **Fragmentation** | Wasted memory due to scattered free blocks | Percentage (%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Bold:** In KTU board examinations, you are expected to remember the units — memory is in **bytes**, addresses are in **hexadecimal values**, and fragmentation is expressed as a **percentage** between $0\%$ and $100\%$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Garbage Collection Needed?

In Module 2 (Linked Lists), every node is dynamically created using `malloc()` in C. Consider the deletion of a node from a singly linked list:

```c
struct Node *temp = head;
head = head->next;
free(temp);
```

If the programmer forgets `free(temp)`, that memory block remains allocated forever — a **memory leak**. If the programmer `free(temp)` but keeps `temp` in another pointer, that pointer now points to garbage — a **dangling pointer**. Garbage collection automates this safety net.

### 2.2 Classification of Garbage Collection Techniques

#### A. Reference Counting
- Each heap-allocated object carries a **counter** of how many pointers reference it.
- When the counter drops to **zero**, the object is immediately reclaimed.
- **Limitation:** Cannot handle **cyclic references** (A → B → A).
- **Time complexity:** $O(1)$ per counter update, but $O(N)$ when freeing a chain.

#### B. Mark-and-Sweep Algorithm (Most Important for KTU)

Operates in **two distinct phases**:

**Phase 1 — MARK:** Starting from the Root Set, the collector performs a **Depth-First Search (DFS)** or **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** traversal of the object graph, setting a *mark bit* on every reachable object.

**Phase 2 — SWEEP:** The collector linearly scans the **entire heap**. Every block whose mark bit is **unset** is appended to the free list. The mark bits of surviving blocks are reset for the next cycle.

- **Time complexity:** $O(\text{Reachable}) + O(\text{Heap Size}) = O(R + H)$
- **Drawback:** Causes **stop-the-world (STW)** pause because the heap cannot be mutated during the sweep.

#### C. Copying Collection (Cheney's Algorithm)
- The heap is divided into two equal halves: **From-space** and **To-space**.
- Live objects are copied from From-space to To-space.
- After GC, the roles swap.
- **Advantage:** No fragmentation (compaction is free).
- **Disadvantage:** Wastes 50% of the heap.

#### D. Generational Garbage Collection
- Objects are classified by **age** (number of GC cycles survived).
- **Young generation** (small, frequently collected) and **Old generation** (large, rarely collected).
- Based on the **weak generational hypothesis**: most objects die young.
- **Major collection** is triggered when the old generation fills up.

### 2.3 Memory Compaction Algorithms

#### A. Compaction with Two-Pointer Algorithm
- Use a **forward pointer (FWD)** to find the next live object.
- Use a **backward pointer (BWD)** to indicate the destination slot.
- **Time complexity:** $O(N)$ where $N$ is the number of live objects.

#### B. Compaction with Handles (Indirect Pointers)
- Replace direct pointers with an indirection layer called a **handle table**.
- During compaction, only the handle table is updated, not the application pointers.
- Reduces relocation cost but adds an extra indirection on every access.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|------------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Memory Utilization | $U = \frac{\text{Used Memory}}{\text{Total Memory}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| 2 | External Fragmentation | $F_{ext} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Largest Free Block}}{\text{Total Free Space}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 3 | Internal Fragmentation | $F_{int} = \dfrac{\text{Allocated} - \text{Actually Used}}{\text{Allocated}}$ | Percentage (%) |
| 4 | Reference Count Decrement | $\text{Count}(x) \leftarrow \text{Count}(x) - 1$ | Integer |
| 5 | Garbage Condition (RC) | $\text{Count}(x) = 0 \implies x \text{ is garbage}$ | Boolean |
| 6 | Mark Phase Time | $T_{mark} = O(R)$, where $R$ is reachable objects | Seconds |
| 7 | Sweep Phase Time | $T_{sweep} = O(H)$, where $H$ is total heap size | Seconds |
| 8 | Total GC Time (M&S) | $T_{gc} = T_{mark} + T_{sweep} = O(R + H)$ | Seconds |
| 9 | Copying GC Space Cost | $C_{copy} = 0.5 \times H_{total}$ | Bytes |
| 10 | Compaction Relocations | $N_{reloc} = \text{Count}(\text{Live Objects})$ | Cardinality |
| 11 | New Address after Compaction | $\text{NewAddr}(x) = \text{Base} + \sum_{y < x} \text{Size}(y)$ | Bytes |
| 12 | Stop-The-World Pause | $T_{pause} = T_{mark} + T_{sweep} + T_{compact}$ | Milliseconds |
| 13 | Pointer Adjustment | $p_{new} = p_{old} - (\text{old\_addr} - \text{new\_addr})$ | Bytes |
| 14 | Reduction in Frag. | $\Delta F_{ext} = F_{ext}^{\text{before}} - F_{ext}^{\text{after}}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When writing $O(N)$ complexity for GC, students often forget to add the **heap size component** ($H$). The correct expression is always $O(R + H)$ for mark-and-sweep, not just $O(N)$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Web Servers (Java/JVM)** | G1GC, ZGC collectors for low-latency response |
| **Operating Systems (Linux)** | Kernel uses SLAB allocator with built-in compaction |
| **Databases** | Buffer pool management reclaims dirty / old pages |
| **Embedded Systems** | C lacks GC; manual `free()` is mandatory — compaction via `malloc_trim()` |
| **Game Engines (Unity C#)** | Incremental GC to prevent frame stuttering |
| **Compilers (GCC)** | Register allocation performs mini-compaction (live range coalescing) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Mark-and-Sweep Walkthrough

**Scenario:** Consider a heap with 8 memory blocks. The Root Set contains a single pointer to **Block 3**. The reference graph is:

$$\text{Block 3} \rightarrow \text{Block 5} \rightarrow \text{Block 7}$$
$$\text{Block 3} \rightarrow \text{Block 1}$$
$$\text{Block 6} \rightarrow \text{Block 7} \quad (\text{but 6 is unreachable})$$

#### Step 1: Initialize the Heap

| Block | Mark Bit | Pointer Field | Status |
|-------|----------|---------------|--------|
| 0 | 0 | NULL | FREE |
| 1 | 0 | → NULL | ALLOCATED |
| 2 | 0 | NULL | FREE |
| 3 | 0 | → Block 1, → Block 5 | ALLOCATED (Root) |
| 4 | 0 | NULL | FREE |
| 5 | 0 | → Block 7 | ALLOCATED |
| 6 | 0 | → Block 7 | ALLOCATED (Orphan) |
| 7 | 0 | NULL | ALLOCATED |

**Conversion Logic:** Every block begins with `mark = 0` (unmarked). The pointer field is read from the application's live object graph before the GC begins.

#### Step 2: Mark Phase (DFS from Root)

Starting from the **Root Set** $R = \{\text{Block 3}\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Push Block 3 onto work-stack} &\implies \text{Stack} = [3] \\
\text{Pop 3, mark: mark[3]} &\leftarrow 1 \\
\text{Follow its references: Block 1, Block 5} &\implies \text{Stack} = [1, 5] \\
\text{Pop 5, mark: mark[5]} &\leftarrow 1 \\
\text{Follow its reference: Block 7} &\implies \text{Stack} = [1, 7] \\
\text{Pop 7, mark: mark[7]} &\leftarrow 1 \\
\text{Pop 1, mark: mark[1]} &\leftarrow 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result after Mark Phase:**

| Block | Mark Bit | Status |
|-------|----------|--------|
| 0 | 0 | (not visited) |
| 1 | **1** | LIVE |
| 2 | 0 | (not visited) |
| 3 | **1** | LIVE |
| 4 | 0 | (not visited) |
| 5 | **1** | LIVE |
| 6 | 0 | (orphan — never reached) |
| 7 | **1** | LIVE |

**Conversion Logic:** Blocks 1, 3, 5, 7 are marked because the DFS traced a path from Root to them. Block 6 is unreachable from Root (no path from Block 3 to Block 6) even though it is allocated.

#### Step 3: Sweep Phase (Linear Scan of Entire Heap)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For } i = 0 \text{ to } 7: & \\
\text{If mark[i]} = 0 \text{ AND block[i] is ALLOCATED} &\implies \text{Add to free list} \\
\text{If mark[i]} = 1 &\implies \text{Reset mark[i]} \leftarrow 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Free list constructed:** Block 6 is the only unmarked allocated block, so it is freed and added to the free list.

**After Sweep:**

| Block | Mark Bit | Status |
|-------|----------|--------|
| 0 | 0 | FREE |
| 1 | 0 | LIVE (mark reset) |
| 2 | 0 | FREE |
| 3 | 0 | LIVE (mark reset) |
| 4 | 0 | FREE |
| 5 | 0 | LIVE (mark reset) |
| 6 | 0 | **FREED (was garbage)** |
| 7 | 0 | LIVE (mark reset) |

**Total time taken:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{gc} &= T_{mark} + T_{sweep} \\
&= O(R) + O(H) \\
&= O(4) + O(8) \\
&= O(12) \text{ operations}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Compaction Walkthrough

**Scenario:** After garbage collection, the heap looks like this (F = FREE, L = LIVE, 1 byte per block):

$$\underbrace{F \; F}_{0,1} \;\underbrace{L}_{2} \;\underbrace{F}_{3} \;\underbrace{L}_{4} \;\underbrace{F \; F}_{5,6} \;\underbrace{L}_{7}$$

**Goal:** Move all LIVE blocks to the low-address end to produce a single large free block.

#### Step 1: Compute New Addresses using the formula

$$\text{NewAddr}(i) = \text{Base} + \sum_{j < i, \; \text{status}(j) = L} \text{Size}(j)$$

**Conversion Logic:** We count how many LIVE blocks exist at indices strictly less than the current index $i$. This count gives the new offset of block $i$ from the base.

| Original Index | Status | Live count before | New Address |
|----------------|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| 2 | LIVE | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | LIVE | 1 | 1 |
| 7 | LIVE | 2 | 2 |

**Mapping table (old → new):**
$$2 \rightarrow 0, \quad 4 \rightarrow 1, \quad 7 \rightarrow 2$$

#### Step 2: Move Objects to New Locations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block 2} &\rightarrow \text{New location 0} \\
\text{Block 4} &\rightarrow \text{New location 1} \\
\text{Block 7} &\rightarrow \text{New location 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Update All Pointers in the Application

If the application held a pointer `p = &Block 4`, it must be updated using the pointer adjustment formula:

$$p_{new} = p_{old} - (\text{old\_addr} - \text{new\_addr}) = 4 - (4 - 1) = 1$$

**General formula for pointer adjustment:**

$$p_{new} = p_{old} - \Delta, \quad \text{where } \Delta = \text{old\_addr} - \text{new\_addr}$$

#### Step 4: Final Compacted Heap

$$\underbrace{L \; L \; L}_{0,1,2} \;\underbrace{F \; F \; F \; F \; F}_{3,4,5,6,7}$$

**External fragmentation after compaction:**

$$
F_{ext} = 1 - \frac{\text{Largest Free Block}}{\text{Total Free Space}} = 1 - \frac{5}{5} = 0
$$

**Conversion Logic:** With a single contiguous free block of 5 bytes (the entire free space), external fragmentation is now **zero**, satisfying the compaction objective.

#### Step 5: Cost Analysis

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Relocations} &= 3 \\
\text{Pointer updates} &= \text{Number of references to live blocks} \\
T_{compact} &= O(\text{Live Objects} + \text{References}) = O(R_{live} + \text{Refs})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of GC Techniques

| Algorithm | Pause Time | Handles Cycles | Performs Compaction | Heap Overhead | KTU Module Fit |
|-----------|------------|----------------|---------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Reference Counting | Short (incremental) | **No** | No | Counter per object | Module 2 (intro) |
| Mark-and-Sweep | Long (STW) | **Yes** | No | Mark bit per object | **Module 2 (core)** |
| Mark-Compact | Long (STW) | **Yes** | **Yes** | Mark bit per object | Module 2 (advanced) |
| Copying (Cheney) | Medium | **Yes** | Free | **50%** of heap | Module 4 (trees) |
| Generational | Short (young gen) | **Yes** | Optional | Survivor spaces | Beyond Module 2 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 4.1: Mark-and-Sweep Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start GC Trigger]) --> B1[Initialize Mark Bits to 0]
    B1 --> C2[Push Root Set onto Work Stack]
    C2 --> D3{Is Stack Empty?}
    D3 -- No --> E4[Pop Object X from Stack]
    E4 --> F5{Mark Bit of X is 0?}
    F5 -- Yes --> G6[Set Mark Bit of X to 1]
    G6 --> H7[Push All Children of X onto Stack]
    H7 --> D3
    F5 -- No --> D3
    D3 -- Yes --> I8[Begin Sweep Phase: i = 0]
    I8 --> J9{Mark Bit of Block i is 0 AND Block is Allocated?}
    J9 -- Yes --> K10[Free Block i, Add to Free List]
    K10 --> L11[Increment i]
    J9 -- No --> L11
    L11 --> M12{i equals Heap Size?}
    M12 -- No --> J9
    M12 -- Yes --> N13[Reset All Mark Bits to 0]
    N13 --> O14([GC Cycle Complete])
```

### Diagram 4.2: Memory Compaction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph before["Before Compaction"]
        BB0[FREE] --- BB1[FREE] --- BB2[LIVE] --- BB3[FREE] --- BB4[LIVE] --- BB5[FREE] --- BB6[FREE] --- BB7[LIVE]
    end
    subgraph process["Compaction Engine"]
        PP1[Address Calculator] --> PP2[Block Mover]
        PP2 --> PP3[Pointer Updater]
    end
    subgraph after["After Compaction"]
        AA0[LIVE] --- AA1[LIVE] --- AA2[LIVE] --- AA3[FREE] --- AA4[FREE] --- AA5[FREE] --- AA6[FREE] --- AA7[FREE]
    end
    before --> process
    process --> after
```

### Diagram 4.3: Linked List Deletion — Garbage Value Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph step1["Step 1: Original Linked List"]
        S1A[Head ptr 1000] --> S1B[Node 1000 Data=10]
        S1B --> S1C[Node 2000 Data=20]
        S1C --> S1D[Node 3000 Data=30]
        S1D --> S1E[Node 4000 Data=40]
    end
    subgraph step2["Step 2: Delete Node 2000 via free ptr"]
        S2A[Head ptr 1000] --> S2B[Node 1000 Data=10]
        S2B --> S2C[Node 3000 Data=30]
        S2C --> S2D[Node 4000 Data=40]
        S2E[Dangling ptr 2000 GARBAGE]
    end
    subgraph step3["Step 3: After Garbage Collection"]
        S3A[Head ptr 1000] --> S3B[Node 1000 Data=10]
        S3B --> S3C[Node 3000 Data=30]
        S3C --> S3D[Node 4000 Data=40]
        S3E[Block 2000 RETURNED to Free List]
    end
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
```

### Diagram 4.4: Reachability Graph for Mark Phase

```mermaid
graph LR
    R0([Root Set: Block 3]) --> N1[Block 3]
    N1 --> N2[Block 1]
    N1 --> N3[Block 5]
    N3 --> N4[Block 7]
    O1[Block 6 ORPHAN] -.->|unreachable| N4
    style N1 fill:#90EE90
    style N2 fill:#90EE90
    style N3 fill:#90EE90
    style N4 fill:#90EE90
    style O1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style R0 fill:#87CEEB
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
>
> **Q1. Define garbage collection. List any two advantages of automatic garbage collection over manual memory management in C.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> *Garbage collection* is the automated process of identifying and reclaiming memory blocks that are no longer reachable from any active pointer in a running program.
>
> **Two advantages:**
> 1. **Prevents dangling pointers** — the GC ensures freed memory is not accessed via stale references *(1 mark)*.
> 2. **Prevents memory leaks** — unreachable memory is automatically reclaimed, eliminating programmer error *(1 mark)*.
> 3. *(Bonus point)* Simplifies code by removing the need for explicit `free()` calls.
>
> *(Marks: Definition = 1, Each advantage = 1, Total = 3)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2. What is a dangling pointer? How is it different from a memory leak?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A **dangling pointer** is a pointer variable that still holds the memory address of a block that has already been freed *(1 mark)*.
>
> A **memory leak** is the gradual accumulation of dynamically allocated memory that is no longer referenced but was never freed *(1 mark)*.
>
> **Key Difference *(1 mark)*:**
> - *Dangling pointer:* Memory is freed but the pointer still exists — **danger of invalid access**.
> - *Memory leak:* Memory is allocated but the pointer is lost — **danger of heap exhaustion**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> ### Question A: 14 Marks
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Mark-and-Sweep** garbage collection algorithm with a suitable example. Discuss its time complexity and the *stop-the-world* pause problem. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** What is **reference counting**? Why does it fail to reclaim cyclic garbage? Illustrate with a diagram. **[7 marks]**

---

> **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks**
>
> **Definition *(1 mark)*:** Mark-and-Sweep is a two-phase GC algorithm that first *marks* all reachable objects from the root set, then *sweeps* (frees) all unmarked objects.
>
> **Algorithm Steps *(3 marks)*:**
>
> **Phase 1 — Mark:** Starting from the Root Set (global variables, stack pointers, CPU registers), perform a graph traversal (DFS or BFS) and set the mark bit of every reachable object to 1.
>
> **Phase 2 — Sweep:** Scan the entire heap linearly. For every block with `mark = 0`, add it to the free list. For every block with `mark = 1`, reset `mark = 0` for the next cycle.
>
> **Example *(2 marks)*:**
>
> Consider a heap of 8 blocks. Root points to Block 3. Reference graph: 3 → 1, 3 → 5, 5 → 7, and an orphan Block 6 → 7.
>
> | Block | Mark Bit Before | Mark Bit After Mark | Status After Sweep |
> |-------|----------------|---------------------|---------------------|
> | 0     | 0              | 0                   | FREE                |
> | 1     | 0              | **1**               | LIVE                |
> | 2     | 0              | 0                   | FREE                |
> | 3     | 0              | **1**               | LIVE                |
> | 4     | 0              | 0                   | FREE                |
> | 5     | 0              | **1**               | LIVE                |
> | 6     | 0              | 0                   | **FREED (garbage)** |
> | 7     | 0              | **1**               | LIVE                |
>
> **Time Complexity *(1 mark)*:**
> $$T_{gc} = O(R) + O(H) = O(R + H)$$
> where $R$ = reachable objects, $H$ = total heap size.
>
> **Stop-the-World (STW) Pause:** During mark and sweep, the application threads are halted, causing latency spikes. This is the main drawback.

---

> **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks**
>
> **Definition *(1 mark)*:** Reference counting maintains a counter on every heap-allocated object. The counter increments on every new reference and decrements when a reference is removed. When the counter reaches zero, the object is immediately reclaimed.
>
> **Algorithm *(2 marks)*:**
> ```c
> void addReference(Object *p) { p->refCount++; }
> void releaseReference(Object *p) {
>     p->refCount--;
>     if (p->refCount == 0) {
>         // recursively release references held by p
>         free(p);
>     }
> }
> ```
>
> **Cyclic Garbage Failure *(3 marks)*:**
>
> Consider two objects A and B that reference each other but are not referenced by the root set:
> $$\text{Root} \not\rightarrow A, \quad A \rightarrow B, \quad B \rightarrow A$$
>
> | Object | Reference Count |
> |--------|-----------------|
> | A      | 1 (from B)      |
> | B      | 1 (from A)      |
>
> Both counts remain 1, so neither is reclaimed, **even though both are garbage** (unreachable from Root).
>
> **Diagram *(1 mark)*:**
> ```
>     [A] <---> [B]
>      ^         |
>      |_________|
>   (cyclic loop, no external reference)
> ```
>
> **Conclusion:** Reference counting is fast but cannot handle cyclic structures — a major limitation in graph and linked data structures.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative Choice]**
>
> ### Question B: 14 Marks
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **memory compaction**. Describe the *two-pointer compaction algorithm* with an example. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** Differentiate between **internal and external fragmentation**. Show, with a numerical example, how compaction reduces external fragmentation. **[7 marks]**

---

> **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks**
>
> **Definition *(1 mark)*:** Compaction is the process of moving all live memory blocks to one contiguous region of the heap, consolidating all free memory into a single large block.
>
> **Two-Pointer Algorithm *(4 marks)*:**
>
> - **FWD pointer** scans the heap from low to high, looking for the next LIVE block.
> - **BWD pointer** also starts from the low end and indicates the *next free slot* to write to.
> - When FWD finds a LIVE block, it is moved to BWD's position, and BWD is incremented.
>
> **Pseudocode:**
> ```
> FWD = base_address;
> BWD = base_address;
> while (FWD < end_of_heap) {
>     if (FWD->status == LIVE) {
>         move_block(FWD, BWD);
>         update_pointers(FWD, BWD);
>         BWD = BWD + FWD->size;
>     }
>     FWD = FWD + FWD->size;
> }
> ```
>
> **Example *(2 marks)*:**
>
> Initial heap (L = Live, F = Free):
> $$F \; F \; L \; F \; L \; F \; F \; L$$
>
> After compaction:
> $$L \; L \; L \; F \; F \; F \; F \; F$$
>
> Free space is now a single 5-block region, ready to serve any allocation request up to 5 blocks.

---

> **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks**
>
> **Internal Fragmentation *(2 marks)*:**
> - Wasted memory *inside* an allocated block.
> - Occurs when the allocator rounds up the requested size to a fixed boundary.
> - Example: A request for 13 bytes is given a 16-byte block → 3 bytes wasted.
>
> **External Fragmentation *(2 marks)*:**
> - Wasted memory *between* allocated blocks.
> - Sufficient total free memory exists, but no single contiguous block is large enough.
> - Example: Free blocks of sizes 3, 5, 7 bytes — total 15 bytes free, but a request for 10 bytes cannot be served.
>
> **Numerical Example *(3 marks)*:**
>
> Suppose total heap = 100 bytes, 60 bytes used, 40 bytes free split as 10 + 15 + 15.
>
> **Before Compaction:**
> $$F_{ext} = 1 - \frac{\text{Largest Free Block}}{\text{Total Free Space}} = 1 - \frac{15}{40} = 0.625 \; (62.5\%)$$
>
> **After Compaction:** All 40 free bytes become one block.
> $$F_{ext} = 1 - \frac{40}{40} = 0 \; (0\%)$$
>
> **Reduction in fragmentation:**
> $$\Delta F_{ext} = 0.625 - 0 = 0.625 \; (62.5\% \text{ reduction})$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing Mark-and-Sweep with Mark-Compact** *(lose 2 marks)*: Mark-and-Sweep only reclaims memory; it does **not** compact. Compaction is a separate phase.
> 2. **Forgetting the Root Set** *(lose 1 mark)*: Always mention the Root Set as the starting point of the Mark phase.
> 3. **Writing $O(N)$ for GC complexity** *(lose 1 mark)*: Correct expression is $O(R + H)$ because we sweep the *entire heap*, not just reachable objects.
> 4. **Stating that reference counting handles cycles** *(lose 3 marks)*: It does NOT — this is a frequently asked conceptual trap.
> 5. **Omitting pointer updates in compaction** *(lose 2 marks)*: Failing to update all references after relocation will crash the program.
> 6. **Mixing up internal and external fragmentation examples** *(lose 2 marks)*: Be precise — internal = *inside* the block, external = *between* blocks.
> 7. **Not resetting mark bits after sweep** *(lose 1 mark)*: Forgetting the `mark[i] = 0` reset step corrupts the next GC cycle.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2):**
>
> - [x] **Garbage** = unreachable heap memory; **Reachable** = traceable from Root Set via pointers.
> - [x] **Dangling pointer** = points to freed memory; **Memory leak** = allocated memory with no pointer.
> - [x] **Mark-and-Sweep** has two phases: Mark (DFS/BFS from Root) and Sweep (linear heap scan).
> - [x] **Time complexity of M&S:** $T_{gc} = O(R + H)$.
> - [x] **Reference counting** is fast but **cannot handle cyclic garbage** (A → B → A loops).
> - [x] **Compaction** moves LIVE blocks together; uses **two-pointer (FWD/BWD)** or **handle table** approach.
> - [x] **Compaction cost:** $O(\text{Live Objects} + \text{References to update})$.
> - [x] **External fragmentation formula:** $F_{ext} = 1 - \frac{\text{Largest Free Block}}{\text{Total Free Space}}$.
> - [x] **Internal fragmentation** = wasted space *inside* an allocated block (rounding up).
> - [x] **External fragmentation** = scattered free blocks that cannot serve a large request.
> - [x] **Copying GC** wastes 50% heap space but offers compaction for free.
> - [x] **Generational GC** uses the *weak generational hypothesis* — most objects die young.
> - [x] **Stop-The-World (STW) pause** is the main drawback of tracing GCs (M&S, M&C).
> - [x] **Root Set** consists of: global variables, CPU registers, stack frames, and active thread pointers.
> - [x] **Compaction necessity in C:** `malloc()` does not auto-compact; `malloc_trim()` may be called manually.
> - [x] **New address after compaction:** $p_{new} = p_{old} - (\text{old} - \text{new})$.
> - [x] **Mark bit must be reset** after sweep, otherwise the next GC cycle will incorrectly mark objects as live.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trees – Representation of Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Trees – Representation of Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tree (Data Structure):** A finite, non-empty set of one or more **nodes** such that there is a designated node called the **root** of the tree, and the remaining nodes are partitioned into $n \geq 0$ disjoint sub-trees $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$, each of which is itself a tree. A tree is a hierarchical, non-linear, **acyclic**, and **connected** data structure.

A **General Tree** (also called a *m-ary* or *k-ary* tree) is a tree in which each node may have an arbitrary number of children. Since the number of children is not restricted, a generalized structure is required to represent it efficiently in memory.

### Standard Tree Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Root** | The topmost node; the only node with no parent. |
| **Node** | A fundamental unit that stores data and links to sub-trees. |
| **Edge** | The connecting line between a parent and a child. |
| **Leaf / Terminal Node** | A node with **zero** children. |
| **Internal Node** | A node with **at least one** child. |
| **Degree of a Node** | The number of children of that node. |
| **Degree of a Tree** | The maximum degree among all nodes. |
| **Depth of a Node** | Length of the path from the **root** to that node. |
| **Height of a Node** | Length of the longest path from that node to a **leaf**. |
| **Height of a Tree** | Height of the root node. |
| **Sibling** | Nodes that share the **same parent**. |
| **Ancestor / Descendant** | Any node on the path from the root to a given node (excluding the node itself) is an ancestor; the reverse is a descendant. |
| **Forest** | An ordered set of $n \geq 0$ disjoint trees. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In a tree with $N$ nodes, the number of edges is always exactly **$N - 1$**. This is a direct consequence of the acyclic and connected property — there is exactly one path between any two nodes.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Real World" Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Tree like a Company Organization Chart** 🏢.
> - The **CEO** sits at the top → that is the **root node**.
> - Managers report directly to the CEO → they are the **children** of the root, and the CEO is their **parent**.
> - Managers at the same level (e.g., three Senior Managers) are **siblings**.
> - A team lead who has no reports under them is a **leaf node**.
> - The whole company hierarchy, if you remove the CEO, splits into independent divisions → this collection is a **forest**.
>
> Just as an organization chart can be drawn in different ways (boxes-and-arrows, table format, JSON structure), a computer must also store a tree in memory using one of several **representations** — and choosing the right one affects how fast operations like searching, inserting, or traversing become.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need a "Representation"?

A tree is an **abstract data type (ADT)** — it defines *what operations* must be supported (insertion, deletion, traversal). However, to make a computer perform these operations, the tree must be mapped to actual memory cells. This mapping is called the **Representation (or Storage Structure)** of the tree. There are three principal representations studied in PCCST303:

1. **General (Linked) Representation** — each node carries pointers to all its children.
2. **Binary Tree Representation** — convert the general tree to a binary tree using *first-child / next-sibling* mapping.
3. **Sequential (Array) Representation** — store the tree in a 1-D or 2-D array, exploiting the **positional relationship** between parent and child.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General (Linked) Representation

In this representation, each node contains:
- A **data field** (the payload)
- A **pointer to the first child** of the node
- A **pointer to the next sibling** of the node (in a left-to-right order)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Node Structure (Conceptual):**
> A general tree node is typically stored as a record with **two link fields**:
> 1. `firstChild` — points to the leftmost child.
> 2. `nextSibling` — points to the next sibling to the right.
>
> This is the most memory-efficient **general-purpose** representation because it only uses $2N$ pointers regardless of how many children each node actually has.

### Algorithmic Logic for First-Child / Next-Sibling Method
- **Step 1:** Traverse the root's children left-to-right.
- **Step 2:** Connect them as a linked list using the `nextSibling` pointer.
- **Step 3:** Recursively repeat for each subtree.
- The `firstChild` of a node always points to the **leftmost** of these siblings.

## 2.2 Binary Tree Representation (Left-Child Right-Sibling)

Any general tree can be **transformed** into a unique binary tree using the rule:
- The **left child** in the binary tree = the **first child** in the general tree.
- The **right child** in the binary tree = the **next sibling** in the general tree.

> [!NOTE]
> This bijection is a **theorem** (proved by Harry F. Sanders, and later popularized in Knuth's *The Art of Computer Programming, Vol. 1*). It guarantees that any forest of general trees can be represented as one binary tree — and vice-versa.

### Why Is This Useful?
It allows us to **reuse all binary tree algorithms** (inorder, preorder, postorder) on a general tree. The **preorder traversal** of the binary representation yields the **preorder traversal of the original general tree**.

## 2.3 Sequential (Array) Representation

A tree can be stored in a **1-D array** using a simple mathematical indexing formula. For the **root at index 1**:

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Formula | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total nodes vs edges** | $E = N - 1$ | Always true for any tree. |
| **Parent index** | $\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$ | Valid for $i > 1$. |
| **Left child index** | $\text{leftChild}(i) = 2i$ | Valid if $2i \leq N$. |
| **Right child index** | $\text{rightChild}(i) = 2i + 1$ | Valid if $2i + 1 \leq N$. |
| **Leaves via degree** | $n_0 = 1 + \sum_{i \geq 2}(i - 1)\,n_i$ | For tree with max degree $k$. |
| **Total pointers in linked repr.** | $2N$ | For first-child/next-sibling. |
| **Wasted slots in array repr.** | $2^k - N$ (for complete binary tree of height $k$) | Wasteful for *skewed* trees. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe symbol escaping rule:** Whenever absolute value or similar vertical bar appears, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to keep the markdown table intact. Example: $\vert \log_2 N \vert$ — not $\vert \log_2 N \vert$ with raw pipes.

### 2-D Array (Adjacency / Parent-Child Matrix) Representation

In the 2-D array form, we use an **$N \times 2$** array where:
- `tree[i][0]` = **first child** of node $i$ (or $-1$ if none).
- `tree[i][1]` = **next sibling** of node $i$ (or $-1$ if none).
- The root is conventionally stored at index **0** or **1**.

This is a compact, cache-friendly representation that avoids pointers entirely.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Applications of Tree Representation Choice:**
> - **File Systems (NTFS, ext4):** Use a B+ tree internally — a generalization of the binary tree representation.
> - **Database Indexing:** Sequential (array) representation is used in heap-backed priority queues.
> - **Compiler Design (Abstract Syntax Trees):** Use *first-child / next-sibling* linked representation because the number of children per node is unpredictable.
> - **HTML / XML DOM:** Linked representation with `parentNode`, `firstChild`, `nextSibling` is exactly the W3C DOM model.
> - **Routing Algorithms:** Use a trie (a 26-ary general tree) with array-based child indexing for O(1) per character.

The choice of representation thus directly dictates the **time complexity** of operations and the **memory footprint** of the program.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Leaves in Terms of Node Degrees

**Given:**
A general tree $T$ with $N$ nodes. Let $n_i$ denote the number of nodes of degree $i$ (i.e., having exactly $i$ children). The tree has maximum degree $k$.

**We must prove:** $n_0 = 1 + n_2 + 2n_3 + 3n_4 + \dots + (k-1)n_k$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total node count:**

$$N = n_0 + n_1 + n_2 + n_3 + \dots + n_k$$

This is simply the partition of all $N$ nodes by their degree.

**Step 2 — Total edge count using degree:**

Each node of degree $i$ contributes exactly $i$ edges as a parent. Hence, summing the degrees of all nodes gives the total number of edges:

$$E = 0 \cdot n_0 + 1 \cdot n_1 + 2 \cdot n_2 + 3 \cdot n_3 + \dots + k \cdot n_k$$

**Step 3 — Apply the fundamental tree property:**

For any tree, $E = N - 1$ (a tree with $N$ nodes has exactly $N - 1$ edges). Substituting Step 1 and Step 2:

$$1 \cdot n_1 + 2 \cdot n_2 + 3 \cdot n_3 + \dots + k \cdot n_k = (n_0 + n_1 + n_2 + \dots + n_k) - 1$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate $n_0$:**

Move $n_1$ from the LHS to RHS:

$$2n_2 + 3n_3 + \dots + k n_k = n_0 + n_2 + n_3 + \dots + n_k - 1$$

**Step 5 — Group by $n_i$ on the RHS:**

Subtract $n_2 + n_3 + \dots + n_k$ from both sides:

$$(2-1)n_2 + (3-1)n_3 + (4-1)n_4 + \dots + (k-1)n_k = n_0 - 1$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\,n_0 = 1 + n_2 + 2n_3 + 3n_4 + \dots + (k-1)n_k\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the number of leaves $n_0$ is **completely determined** by the count of internal nodes of degree $\geq 2$. For a *binary tree* where $k = 2$, this collapses beautifully to $n_0 = n_2 + 1$ — meaning a binary tree always has **one more leaf than internal nodes with two children** (a famous KTU result!).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Representations

| Criterion | General Linked (First-Child / Next-Sibling) | Binary Tree (Left-Child / Right-Sibling) | Sequential Array (1-D) | 2-D Array (Parent-Child Matrix) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Memory per node** | $2$ pointers + data | $2$ pointers + data | $1$ data slot per node | $2$ data slots per node |
| **Total memory** | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(2^k)$ (worst case) | $O(2N)$ |
| **Wasted space** | None | None | **High** for skewed trees | Minimal |
| **Find $i$-th child** | $O(i)$ traversal | $O(i)$ | $O(1)$ via $2i+k$ | $O(1)$ direct lookup |
| **Find parent** | Not directly (need extra pointer) | $O(\text{height})$ | $O(1)$ via $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$ | $O(1)$ via `tree[i][1]` |
| **Insertion cost** | $O(1)$ at known position | $O(1)$ at known position | $O(N)$ shifting | $O(1)$ |
| **Best for** | Variable-degree trees, ASTs | Reusing binary tree algorithms | **Complete / nearly complete** trees | Small trees, static data |
| **Cache-friendliness** | Poor (pointer chasing) | Poor | **Excellent** | Excellent |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Indexing a Complete Binary Tree in Array Form

Consider a complete binary tree of height $h = 3$ with $N = 15$ nodes. Store it in array `T[1..15]`.

**Step 1 — Root at index 1:** `T[1] = ROOT`.

**Step 2 — Level 2:** Children of node $i$ go to positions $2i$ and $2i+1$.
- Left child of root: $T[2]$.
- Right child of root: $T[3]$.

**Step 3 — Level 3:**
- Children of `T[2]`: $T[4]$, $T[5]$.
- Children of `T[3]`: $T[6]$, $T[7]$.

**Step 4 — Level 4 (leaves):**
- Children of `T[4]`: $T[8]$, $T[9]$.
- Children of `T[5]`: $T[10]$, $T[11]$.
- Children of `T[6]`: $T[12]$, $T[13]$.
- Children of `T[7]`: $T[14]$, $T[15]$.

**Step 5 — Verify the parent formula:** Take $i = 11$. Its parent is $\lfloor 11 / 2 \rfloor = 5$, which is correct since $T[11]$ is the right child of `T[5]`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** If a question says "store the following tree in array form," always start indexing from **1** (not 0) so the parent/child formulas hold. Starting from 0 would force you to use $(i-1)/2$ for parent — a common error students make.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Converting a General Tree to Binary Tree

**Given General Tree:**

```
            A
          / | \
         B  C  D
        /\     |
       E  F    G
              / \
             H   I
```

**Step 1 — A's first child is B** → B becomes the **left child** of A in the binary tree.

**Step 2 — A's next siblings after B are C, D** → C becomes the **right child** of B, D becomes the **right child** of C.

**Step 3 — Recurse for B:** B's first child is E → left child of B in binary tree is E. B's next sibling is none (since B is leftmost) → no right child.

**Step 4 — Recurse for E, F:** Both have no children. No left/right pointers.

**Step 5 — Recurse for D:** D's first child is G → left child of D = G. G's children H, I → H is left child of G, I is right child of H.

**Resulting Binary Tree:**

```
        A
       /
      B
     / \
    E   C
     \   \
      F   D
         /
        G
       /
      H
       \
        I
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key observation:** The **right pointer** in the binary tree always chains *siblings*, while the **left pointer** always dives into the *first child*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Tree Representation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputTree["INPUT GENERAL TREE NODES"]:::input
    Decision{"REPRESENTATION TYPE"}:::decision

    Path1["GENERAL LINKED N"]:::path
    Path2["BINARY TREE LEFT CHILD RIGHT SIBLING"]:::path
    Path3["SEQUENTIAL ARRAY 1D"]:::path
    Path4["2D ARRAY PARENT CHILD MATRIX"]:::path

    OpFC["firstChild pointer"]:::op
    OpNS["nextSibling pointer"]:::op
    OpLC["leftChild = first child"]:::op
    OpRC["rightChild = next sibling"]:::op
    OpIdx["Index i maps to 2i and 2i+1"]:::op
    OpPr["Index floor i/2 maps to parent"]:::op
    OpRow1["tree i 0 = first child"]:::op
    OpRow2["tree i 1 = next sibling"]:::op

    Out1["Output: Linked forest of 2N pointers"]:::out
    Out2["Output: Single binary tree"]:::out
    Out3["Output: 1D array T 1..N"]:::out
    Out4["Output: 2D array N x 2"]:::out

    InputTree --> Decision
    Decision -->|Option 1| Path1
    Decision -->|Option 2| Path2
    Decision -->|Option 3| Path3
    Decision -->|Option 4| Path4

    Path1 --> OpFC
    Path1 --> OpNS
    Path2 --> OpLC
    Path2 --> OpRC
    Path3 --> OpIdx
    Path3 --> OpPr
    Path4 --> OpRow1
    Path4 --> OpRow2

    OpFC --> Out1
    OpNS --> Out1
    OpLC --> Out2
    OpRC --> Out2
    OpIdx --> Out3
    OpPr --> Out3
    OpRow1 --> Out4
    OpRow2 --> Out4

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef path fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic: Memory Layout of a General Tree Node (Linked Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NodeX["NODE STRUCTURE for node X"]
        A["DATA FIELD"]:::data
        B["firstChild POINTER"]:::ptr
        C["nextSibling POINTER"]:::ptr
    end

    FC["CHILD LIST HEAD"]:::target
    NS["NEXT SIBLING NODE"]:::target

    B -->|points to| FC
    C -->|points to| NS

    classDef data fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef ptr fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic: First-Child / Next-Sibling Mapping (Conversion to Binary Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["A ROOT"]:::root
    B["B child 1 of A"]:::node
    C["C child 2 of A"]:::node
    D["D child 3 of A"]:::node
    E["E child 1 of B"]:::leaf
    F["F child 2 of B"]:::leaf
    G["G child 1 of D"]:::node
    H["H child 1 of G"]:::leaf
    I["I child 2 of G"]:::leaf

    A --- B
    A --- C
    A --- D
    B --- E
    B --- F
    D --- G
    G --- H
    G --- I

    classDef root fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Rule (recap):**
> - **Left arrow in binary tree** = first child in general tree.
> - **Right arrow in binary tree** = next sibling in general tree.

---

## 4.4 Schematic: Sequential Array Storage of a Complete Binary Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Array["ARRAY T 1 to 15"]
        T1["T 1 A ROOT"]:::root
        T2["T 2 B"]:::node
        T3["T 3 C"]:::node
        T4["T 4 D"]:::node
        T5["T 5 E"]:::node
        T6["T 6 F"]:::node
        T7["T 7 G"]:::node
        T8["T 8 H LEAF"]:::leaf
        T9["T 9 I LEAF"]:::leaf
        T10["T 10 J LEAF"]:::leaf
        T11["T 11 K LEAF"]:::leaf
        T12["T 12 L LEAF"]:::leaf
        T13["T 13 M LEAF"]:::leaf
        T14["T 14 N LEAF"]:::leaf
        T15["T 15 O LEAF"]:::leaf
    end

    T1 -.2i.-> T2
    T1 -.2i+1.-> T3
    T2 -.2i.-> T4
    T2 -.2i+1.-> T5
    T3 -.2i.-> T6
    T3 -.2i+1.-> T7
    T4 -.2i.-> T8
    T4 -.2i+1.-> T9
    T5 -.2i.-> T10
    T5 -.2i+1.-> T11
    T6 -.2i.-> T12
    T6 -.2i+1.-> T13
    T7 -.2i.-> T14
    T7 -.2i+1.-> T15

    classDef root fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway from the Schematic:**
> - The dotted arrow labelled `2i` shows the **left child rule**.
> - The dotted arrow labelled `2i+1` shows the **right child rule**.
> - Notice that for a *skewed tree* (e.g., every node has only a right child), this representation wastes nearly half the array slots — a critical reason to prefer linked representation for unbalanced trees.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Indexing error:** Students often start array indexing from 0, which breaks the parent formula. Always use **1-based** indexing unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 2. **Mixing up first-child with left-child:** The two are **NOT identical** in the binary representation — left-child = first-child, but right-child = next-sibling (NOT second child).
> 3. **Forgetting the $-1$ in the leaf formula:** The constant term `1` in $n_0 = 1 + n_2 + 2n_3 + \dots$ is frequently dropped. Show it explicitly in derivations to earn full marks.
> 4. **Not drawing the resulting binary tree:** Conversion questions require both the **mapping** and a **diagram** of the final binary tree.
> 5. **Edge case for the array representation:** When asked "is the array representation suitable for a skewed tree?", the correct answer is **NO** — justify with the $O(2^k)$ space complexity.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Degree of a node
(b) Height of a tree
(c) Sibling nodes

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per definition with example):**

- **(a) Degree of a node:** The number of children that a node has. **Example:** In the tree $A \to \{B, C, D\}$, the degree of node $A$ is $3$.
- **(b) Height of a tree:** The length of the longest path from the root node to a leaf node. **Example:** A tree with a single root has height $0$; a tree with one root and one child has height $1$.
- **(c) Sibling nodes:** Nodes that share the **same parent** are called siblings. **Example:** $B, C, D$ are siblings if they all have $A$ as their parent.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between a general tree and a binary tree. List the three standard representations of a general tree.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | General Tree | Binary Tree |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Degree | Any node can have $0, 1, 2, \dots, n$ children | Each node has **at most 2** children |
| Sub-tree count | $n$ arbitrary sub-trees | At most $2$ sub-trees (left and right) |
| Use case | File systems, organization charts | Expression trees, BSTs, Heaps |

**Three standard representations of a general tree:**
1. **General (Linked) Representation** using `firstChild` and `nextSibling` pointers.
2. **Binary Tree Representation** using the first-child/next-sibling conversion.
3. **Sequential (Array) Representation** using parent-child index formulas. (2 marks for the list)

---

## Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ➤ Question A (14 Marks) — *Linked & Binary Representation Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **General (Linked) Representation** of a tree using the first-child and next-sibling pointers. Draw the node structure and illustrate with an example tree of at least 6 nodes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the given general tree into a **binary tree** representation and show all intermediate steps.

**Given General Tree:**

```
              P
            / | \
           Q  R  S
          /|     |
         T U     V
               / | \
              W  X  Y
```

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Node Structure Definition (2 marks):**

Each node $N$ in the linked representation has the following fields:
- `data` — stores the payload.
- `firstChild` — a pointer to the leftmost child of $N$.
- `nextSibling` — a pointer to the next sibling (to the right) of $N$.

**Step 2 — Construction Algorithm (2 marks):**
- For each node, link all its children into a **horizontal linked list** using `nextSibling`.
- Set `firstChild` of the parent to point to the **leftmost** child in this list.
- Apply this recursively for every internal node.

**Step 3 — Example Illustration (3 marks):** Taking the tree of part (b), the linked structure for node $P$ has:
- `data = P`, `firstChild → Q`, `nextSibling → null` (root has no sibling).
- Node $Q$: `data = Q`, `firstChild → T`, `nextSibling → R`.
- Node $R$: `data = R`, `firstChild → null`, `nextSibling → S`.
- Node $T$: `data = T`, `firstChild → null`, `nextSibling → U`.
- Node $U$: `data = U`, `firstChild → null`, `nextSibling → null` (last child).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Root:** $P$ remains the root. $P$ has first child $Q$, so $Q$ becomes the **left child** of $P$ in the binary tree.

**Step 2 — Siblings of Q:** $R$ and $S$ are next siblings of $Q$. So $R$ becomes **right child of $Q$**, and $S$ becomes **right child of $R$**.

**Step 3 — Q's children:** $T$ and $U$ are $Q$'s first and next children. $T$ becomes **left child of $Q$**; $U$ becomes **right child of $T$**.

**Step 4 — S's children:** $V$ is the only child. $V$ becomes **left child of $S$**.

**Step 5 — V's children:** $W, X, Y$. $W$ becomes **left child of $V$**; $X$ becomes **right child of $W$**; $Y$ becomes **right child of $X$**.

**Final Binary Tree:**

```
            P
           /
          Q
         / \
        T   R
         \   \
          U   S
             /
            V
           /
          W
           \
            X
             \
              Y
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marking Note:** Students must show **at least 4 of the 5 conversion steps** to earn the full 7 marks. Forgetting the recursive application on sub-trees is the most common reason for losing 2-3 marks.

---

### ➤ Question B (14 Marks) — *Array Representation & Properties Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Sequential (Array) Representation** of a binary tree. Give the formulas for finding the parent, left child, and right child of a node stored at index $i$. Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of this representation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a tree $T$ with $N = 14$ nodes, the degree distribution is: $n_0 = 8$ leaves, $n_1 = 1$ node of degree 1, $n_2 = 3$ nodes of degree 2, $n_3 = 2$ nodes of degree 3. **Verify the leaf-count formula** $n_0 = 1 + n_2 + 2n_3$ and confirm the tree is valid.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
In the sequential representation, the nodes of a binary tree are stored level-by-level in a 1-D array. The root is stored at index **1** (by convention). Each subsequent level is filled left-to-right.

**Step 2 — Formulas (3 marks):**

For a node stored at index $i$:
- **Parent of $i$:** $\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$, provided $i > 1$.
- **Left child of $i$:** $\text{leftChild}(i) = 2i$, provided $2i \leq N$.
- **Right child of $i$:** $\text{rightChild}(i) = 2i + 1$, provided $2i + 1 \leq N$.

**Step 3 — Advantages and Disadvantages (2 marks):**

**Advantages:**
- No pointers are required — saving memory.
- Random access to any node in $O(1)$ time via index.
- Excellent cache locality, ideal for heaps.

**Disadvantages:**
- For a **skewed** tree, up to $2^k - N$ array slots are wasted.
- Insertion/deletion in the middle requires shifting $O(N)$ elements.
- The size must be known in advance (or the array must be dynamically resized).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State the given data (1 mark):**
$$N = 14, \quad n_0 = 8, \quad n_1 = 1, \quad n_2 = 3, \quad n_3 = 2$$

**Step 2 — Verify total node count (2 marks):**
$$N \stackrel{?}{=} n_0 + n_1 + n_2 + n_3$$
$$14 \stackrel{?}{=} 8 + 1 + 3 + 2 = 14 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Compute edges using degree sum (1 mark):**
$$E = 0 \cdot n_0 + 1 \cdot n_1 + 2 \cdot n_2 + 3 \cdot n_3$$
$$E = 0 + 1 + 6 + 6 = 13$$

**Step 4 — Verify the tree property $E = N - 1$ (1 mark):**
$$E = 13 \stackrel{?}{=} N - 1 = 14 - 1 = 13 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Apply the leaf formula (1 mark):**
$$n_0 \stackrel{?}{=} 1 + n_2 + 2 n_3$$
$$8 \stackrel{?}{=} 1 + 3 + 2(2) = 1 + 3 + 4 = 8 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion (1 mark):** All checks pass, so the tree is a **valid general tree** with $N = 14$ nodes, $E = 13$ edges, and the given degree distribution.

---

## 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Pin These Points:**

- A tree is a **connected, acyclic** graph with $N$ nodes and exactly $N - 1$ edges.
- **Three representations:** (1) Linked (first-child / next-sibling), (2) Binary tree (left-child / right-sibling), (3) Sequential array.
- **Linked representation** uses exactly $2N$ pointers for an $N$-node tree — no memory wasted.
- **Binary tree representation** is unique and bijective: it lets you apply binary tree algorithms to general trees.
- **Array representation formulas** (with 1-based indexing):
  - $\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$
  - $\text{leftChild}(i) = 2i$
  - $\text{rightChild}(i) = 2i + 1$
- **Leaf-count formula (KTU favorite):** $n_0 = 1 + n_2 + 2n_3 + 3n_4 + \dots + (k-1)n_k$
- For a **binary tree**, this simplifies to: $n_0 = n_2 + 1$.
- **Array representation is best for complete/nearly complete trees** — terrible for skewed trees.
- **Conversion rule:** General tree → Binary tree: left-pointer = first child, right-pointer = next sibling.
- **Preorder traversal of binary representation = preorder of original general tree.**
- Always verify both $N = \sum n_i$ and $E = N - 1$ when checking the validity of a tree's degree distribution.
- **2-D array form** uses $N \times 2$: column 0 = first child, column 1 = next sibling — efficient for small/medium trees.
- For **compiler ASTs** and **HTML DOM**, the linked representation is the de-facto industry standard due to variable degree.
- For **heaps and priority queues**, the array representation is preferred because of $O(1)$ parent access and cache efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Binary Trees – Types and Properties, Binary Tree Representation, Tree Operations, Tree Traversals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — BINARY TREES: TYPES, PROPERTIES, REPRESENTATION, OPERATIONS & TRAVERSALS

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Tree (KTU Definition):** A **Binary Tree** $T$ is a finite set of nodes which is either *empty* or consists of a distinguished node called the **root** and two disjoint subtrees $T_L$ (left subtree) and $T_R$ (right subtree), each of which is itself a binary tree. Formally, $T = \{ \text{Root}, T_L, T_R \}$ where $T_L \cap T_R = \varnothing$.

The defining characteristic is the **degree constraint of at most 2**, meaning every node has **0, 1, or 2 children** — distinguished as **Left Child** and **Right Child**. The order of children is *significant*, making binary trees an **ordered** data structure.

### 1.2 Vocabulary of a Binary Tree (KTU Standard Terminology)

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Root** | The topmost node with no parent (level 0). |
| **Parent** | A node that has one or more children. |
| **Child** | A node that descends from another node (parent). |
| **Leaf / External Node** | A node with **zero children**. |
| **Internal Node** | A node with **at least one child**. |
| **Sibling** | Nodes that share the same parent. |
| **Ancestor** | Any node on the unique path from root to a given node. |
| **Descendant** | Any node in the subtree rooted at a given node. |
| **Depth of Node** | Length of the path from the root to that node (root has depth **0**). |
| **Height of Node** | Length of the longest downward path from that node to a leaf. |
| **Height of Tree** | Height of the root node. |
| **Degree of Node** | Number of children of that node (in a binary tree: **0, 1, or 2**). |
| **Level** | All nodes at the same depth form a level. |
| **Edge** | A connecting link between a parent and a child. |
| **Subtree** | A node together with all its descendants forms a subtree. |
| **Forest** | A collection of disjoint trees (obtained by removing the root). |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree of Questions" 🌳

Imagine you are designing a **"20 Questions"** guessing game where the computer tries to guess an animal you are thinking of. The first question is always "**Is it a mammal?**" — this is the **root**. Depending on your answer, the conversation branches into two paths: yes leads to "Does it live on land?", no leads to "Does it have feathers?". Each question can spawn at most **two follow-up questions**, and the chain ends when a final guess (a leaf) is reached.

This branching, two-way decision structure is exactly what a **binary tree** is — every internal node poses a question with **≤ 2** possible continuations, and the leaves represent final outcomes. The crucial rule: the **left child and right child are not interchangeable** — asking "land?" vs "water?" leads to completely different guesses, just like in a binary tree where left and right subtrees have **distinct meanings**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are binary trees foundational in KTU DSA?** Almost every advanced structure you'll study — **BSTs, Heaps, AVL Trees, Red-Black Trees, Huffman Coding, Expression Parsers, and Segment Trees** — is built upon the binary tree framework. Mastering traversals and representations here unlocks 70% of Module 4 and Module 5 content.

---

## 2. CLASSIFICATION OF BINARY TREES (KTU High-Yield Types)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the distinction between the following 6 binary-tree types. Memorize both their **structural definitions** and the **mathematical relationships** (formulas in §3).

### 2.1 Full (Strict/Proper) Binary Tree
A binary tree in which **every node has either 0 or 2 children** — no node has exactly one child.

```
       A             ← Root (2 children)
      / \
     B   C           ← Both internal nodes have 2 children
    / \
   D   E             ← Leaves
```

### 2.2 Complete Binary Tree (CBT)
A binary tree in which **all levels are completely filled except possibly the last**, which is filled from **left to right** with no gaps.

```
       A
      / \
     B   C
    / \  /
   D  E F          ← Last level filled left-to-right
```

### 2.3 Perfect Binary Tree
A binary tree in which **all internal nodes have exactly 2 children** AND **all leaves are at the same level** (depth). Every perfect binary tree is also full and complete, but the converse is not true.

```
       A
      / \
     B   C
    / \ / \
   D  E F  G      ← All leaves at depth 2
```

### 2.4 Degenerate (Pathological) Binary Tree
A tree where **every internal node has exactly one child**, so the tree effectively behaves like a **linked list**. The worst case for $O(h)$ tree operations.

```
     A
      \
       B
        \
         C
          \
           D
```

### 2.5 Skewed Binary Tree (Special case of Degenerate)
A degenerate tree that is **purely left-skewed** (every node has only a left child) or **purely right-skewed** (every node has only a right child).

### 2.6 Balanced Binary Tree
A tree where the **height difference between the left and right subtrees of every node is at most 1**. (Examples: AVL trees, Red-Black trees — studied in detail in Module 4.)

# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

---

## 3. FUNDAMENTAL PROPERTIES OF BINARY TREES

### 3.1 Maximum Number of Nodes at Level $i$

Let $L_i$ denote the number of nodes at level $i$ (root at level 0).

$$L_i \leq 2^i$$

> **Intuition:** Each node can have at most 2 children, so the tree **doubles** in maximum size as you descend one level — exponential growth, the same principle behind binary search.

### 3.2 Maximum Number of Nodes in a Tree of Height $h$

The total maximum node count is the sum of a **geometric series**:

$$N_{\max}(h) = \sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^i = 2^{h+1} - 1$$

> **Engineering Utility:** This is the upper bound used when computing the **space complexity** of array-based tree representations and when deriving the **lower bound on height** for $n$ nodes.

### 3.3 Minimum Number of Nodes for Height $h$

A skewed tree achieves the minimum:

$$N_{\min}(h) = h + 1$$

### 3.4 Height of a Binary Tree with $n$ Nodes — Bounds

Combining the two extremes:

$$\log_2(n+1) - 1 \leq h \leq n - 1$$

Equivalently:

$$\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor \leq h \leq n-1$$

---

## 4. SPECIAL-PROPERTY FORMULAS (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following identities are **favorite KTU questions**. They appear every year in Part A (3 marks) and as sub-parts of Part B.

### Table 4.1 — Master Formula Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Applicable To |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Max nodes at level $i$ | $L_i = 2^i$ | Any binary tree |
| 2 | Max total nodes of height $h$ | $N_{\max} = 2^{h+1} - 1$ | Any binary tree |
| 3 | Min total nodes of height $h$ | $N_{\min} = h + 1$ | Skewed tree |
| 4 | Height lower bound | $h \geq \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | Any binary tree |
| 5 | Height upper bound | $h \leq n - 1$ | Skewed tree |
| 6 | Leaves in **Full** tree | $L = I + 1$ | Full binary tree |
| 7 | **Full tree** total nodes | $n = 2I + 1$ | Full binary tree |
| 8 | **Full tree** leaves | $L = (n+1)/2$ | Full binary tree |
| 9 | Internal nodes relation | $n = I + L$ | Any tree |
| 10 | **Perfect tree** of height $h$ — nodes | $n = 2^{h+1} - 1$ | Perfect tree |
| 11 | **Perfect tree** of height $h$ — leaves | $L = 2^h$ | Perfect tree |
| 12 | **Complete tree** — parent index of $i$ | $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$ | CBT (1-indexed array) |
| 13 | **Complete tree** — left child of $i$ | $2i$ | CBT (1-indexed array) |
| 14 | **Complete tree** — right child of $i$ | $2i+1$ | CBT (1-indexed array) |
| 15 | **Complete tree** — parent-child for 0-indexed array | parent $= \lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$, left $= 2i+1$, right $= 2i+2$ | CBT (0-indexed) |

### 4.2 The Famous "Full Tree" Proof ($L = I + 1$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** In a binary tree where every node has either 0 or 2 children, $L = I + 1$, where $L$ = leaves, $I$ = internal nodes.

**Proof Sketch (often asked for 3-7 marks in KTU):**

Let $T$ be the total number of edges. Every edge connects one parent to one child, so $T = n - 1$ (where $n$ = total nodes).

- **Counting by edges from leaves:** Every leaf contributes exactly **1 edge** upward, so leaf-edges $= L$.
- **Counting by edges from internal nodes:** Every internal node has exactly **2 children**, so internal-edges $= 2I$.

Since both count the same set of edges:

$$L = 2I - (I - 1) \implies \text{(rearranging via } T = n-1 \text{)}$$
$$\boxed{L = I + 1}$$

---

## 5. BINARY TREE REPRESENTATIONS (KTU Two Standard Methods)

### 5.1 Array-Based Representation (Sequential)

Best suited for **Complete Binary Trees** — wastes memory for skewed trees.

**Storage Rules (0-indexed array):**

| Node at index $i$ | Stored at |
|--------------------|-----------|
| Root | $0$ |
| Left child of $i$ | $2i + 1$ |
| Right child of $i$ | $2i + 2$ |
| Parent of $i$ | $\lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$ |

**1-indexed variant (used in CLRS textbook):**
- Left child: $2i$, Right child: $2i+1$, Parent: $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Memory Wastage in Skewed Trees:** For a right-skewed tree of height $h$, the array size needed is $2^h - 1$, but only $h+1$ slots are used. **Space complexity = $O(2^h)$** even though tree has $O(h)$ nodes — KTU examiners frequently test this!

### 5.2 Linked Representation (Dynamic)

The standard KTU/C implementation uses a `struct` with `data`, `left`, and `right` pointers.

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *left;
    struct Node *right;
};
```

**Advantages:** No memory wastage for sparse/skewed trees; dynamic growth. **Disadvantages:** Extra memory for two pointers per node; no random access.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized Tree representations** (Left-Child Right-Sibling) are covered in earlier modules — binary trees are a *special case* of $k$-ary trees where $k=2$.

---

## 6. TREE OPERATIONS (KTU-Aligned Pseudocode & Complexity)

| Operation | Description | Time Complexity |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Creation** | Allocate root, recursively build subtrees | $O(n)$ |
| **Insertion** (BST-like) | Recursive descent to find leaf position | $O(h)$ |
| **Deletion** | Three cases: leaf, one child, two children | $O(h)$ |
| **Search** | Recursive comparison and descent | $O(h)$ |
| **Height Calculation** | $h = 1 + \max(h_L, h_R)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Node Count** | $n = 1 + n_L + n_R$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Leaf Count** | Base case + recursive accumulation | $O(n)$ |
| **Mirror / Inversion** | Swap left and right recursively | $O(n)$ |
| **Copy Tree** | Deep copy via recursion | $O(n)$ |
| **Level-order Traversal** | BFS using a Queue | $O(n)$ |

> Where $h$ = height of the tree. For a balanced tree $h = O(\log n)$; for a skewed tree $h = O(n)$.

---

## 7. TREE TRAVERSALS — THE HEART OF MODULE 3

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Traversal** = visiting every node **exactly once** in a specific order. KTU tests **4 traversals** deeply: 3 recursive DFS (Preorder, Inorder, Postorder) + 1 iterative BFS (Level Order).

### 7.1 The Three Recursive DFS Traversals

The notation $D, L, R$ means when we visit a node we record the action at that step:

| Traversal | Order | Use Case |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| **Preorder** | $D \to L \to R$ (Root → Left → Right) | Copying a tree, prefix expression |
| **Inorder** | $L \to D \to R$ (Left → Root → Right) | BST → sorted order, infix expression |
| **Postorder** | $L \to R \to D$ (Left → Right → Root) | Deleting tree, postfix expression |
| **Level Order** | Level by level, left to right | BFS, shortest path in unweighted tree |

### 7.2 Inorder Traversal — Recursive Logic

```
INORDER(node):
    if node == NULL: return
    INORDER(node.left)
    VISIT(node)
    INORDER(node.right)
```

### 7.3 Preorder Traversal — Recursive Logic

```
PREORDER(node):
    if node == NULL: return
    VISIT(node)
    PREORDER(node.left)
    PREORDER(node.right)
```

### 7.4 Postorder Traversal — Recursive Logic

```
POSTORDER(node):
    if node == NULL: return
    POSTORDER(node.left)
    POSTORDER(node.right)
    VISIT(node)
```

### 7.5 Level Order Traversal — Iterative BFS

```
LEVELORDER(root):
    create Queue Q
    Q.enqueue(root)
    while Q is not empty:
        node = Q.dequeue()
        VISIT(node)
        if node.left  != NULL: Q.enqueue(node.left)
        if node.right != NULL: Q.enqueue(node.right)
```

### 7.6 Construction of Binary Tree from Traversals (KTU Classic Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A binary tree **cannot** be uniquely reconstructed from a *single* traversal. The valid combinations are:
>
> | Given Traversals | Unique Tree? | Required Extra Info |
> |------------------|--------------|---------------------|
> | Preorder + Inorder | ✅ Yes | — |
> | Postorder + Inorder | ✅ Yes | — |
> | Preorder + Postorder | ❌ No (except full trees) | — |
> | Level + Inorder | ✅ Yes | — |
>
> **Logic:** Inorder gives the **root's position** (it separates L and R subtrees); the other gives the **root's identity** (the first/last node). Recurse.

### 7.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of Traversals

| Traversal | Engineering Application |
|-----------|------------------------|
| Preorder | Directory listing with full path, file-system printing, AST evaluation |
| Inorder | Generating sorted output from BST, expression evaluation (infix) |
| Postorder | Memory deallocation (delete children before parent), postfix calculators |
| Level Order | BFS in AI search, serialization/deserialization of trees, peer-to-peer networks |

# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

## 8. EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATIONS OF KEY TREE FORMULAS

### 8.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of Nodes at Level $i$

> **Claim:** $L_i \leq 2^i$ for level $i$ (root at $i=0$).

**Step 1 — Base case:** At level 0 (the root), there is exactly **1 node**, and $2^0 = 1$. So $L_0 = 1 \leq 2^0$. ✓

**Step 2 — Inductive hypothesis:** Assume $L_i \leq 2^i$ holds for some level $i$.

**Step 3 — Bound for level $i+1$:** Every node at level $i$ can have at most **2 children**. So:

$$L_{i+1} \leq 2 \cdot L_i$$

**Step 4 — Apply hypothesis:**

$$L_{i+1} \leq 2 \cdot 2^i = 2^{i+1}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** By mathematical induction, $L_i \leq 2^i$ for all $i \geq 0$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 8.2 Derivation: Maximum Total Nodes $N_{\max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1$

**Step 1 — Sum nodes across all levels from 0 to $h$:**

$$N_{\max}(h) = \sum_{i=0}^{h} L_i \leq \sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^i$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the geometric series** $S = 1 + r + r^2 + \dots + r^n = \dfrac{r^{n+1} - 1}{r - 1}$:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{h} 2^i = \frac{2^{h+1} - 1}{2 - 1} = 2^{h+1} - 1$$

**Step 3 — Therefore:**

$$\boxed{N_{\max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1}$$

This bound is **tight** — achieved by the **perfect binary tree**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 8.3 Derivation: Lower Bound on Height $\lfloor \log_2(n+1) \rfloor \leq h$

**Step 1 — Start with the upper bound on nodes:**

$$n \leq N_{\max}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1$$

**Step 2 — Add 1 to both sides:**

$$n + 1 \leq 2^{h+1}$$

**Step 3 — Take $\log_2$ on both sides:**

$$\log_2(n + 1) \leq h + 1$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $h$:**

$$h \geq \log_2(n+1) - 1$$

**Step 5 — Since $h$ is an integer, take the floor (and the maximum with 0):**

$$\boxed{h \geq \lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil - 1 \quad \text{equivalently} \quad h \geq \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 8.4 Derivation: $L = I + 1$ in a Full Binary Tree

> **Claim:** In a full (strict) binary tree, the number of leaves $L$ equals the number of internal nodes $I$ plus 1.

**Step 1 — Let** $n = I + L$ (total node count).
**Step 2 — Count edges via the tree property:** A tree with $n$ nodes has exactly $n - 1$ edges.

$$T = n - 1 = I + L - 1$$

**Step 3 — Count edges by contribution from each node:**

- Every **leaf** contributes **1 edge** upward (to its parent) → leaf-edges = $L$.
- Every **internal node** has exactly **2 children** → contributes **2 edges** downward → internal-edges = $2I$.

**Step 4 — Equate (since both count the same set of edges):**

$$L + 2I = T + (\text{root's missing edge})$$

Wait — careful: each internal node has 2 outgoing edges, and leaves have 0 outgoing edges. The total number of edges is the sum of outgoing edges:

$$2I + 0 \cdot L = T$$

But this overcounts: we need to subtract the **edges to leaves counted from leaves**. The cleaner formulation uses the standard identity:

**Step 4 (correct) — Bijection argument:** Every node except the root has exactly **1 incoming edge** (from its parent). The root has 0 incoming edges. Therefore:

$$T = n - 1$$

But we can also count **outgoing edges**: each internal node has 2 outgoing edges (since it's a *full* binary tree), each leaf has 0.

$$\text{Total outgoing edges} = 2I + 0 \cdot L = 2I$$

This must equal $T$ (since edges = outgoing from parents to children):

$$2I = n - 1 = I + L - 1$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**

$$2I - I = L - 1$$
$$\boxed{L = I + 1}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 8.5 Worked Example: Complete Tree Indexing (CBT)

> **Problem:** A complete binary tree is stored in a 1-indexed array `A[]`. If a node is at index `i`, find indices of its left child, right child, and parent. Also given `A = [_, 50, 30, 70, 10, 40, 60, 80]`, determine the tree.

**Step 1 — Apply formulas** (1-indexed CBT):

| Node at $i$ | Left child | Right child | Parent |
|-------------|------------|-------------|--------|
| 1 (root) | 2 | 3 | — |
| 2 | 4 | 5 | 1 |
| 3 | 6 | 7 | 1 |
| 4 | 8 | 9 | 2 |
| 5 | 10 | 11 | 2 |

**Step 2 — Map array to tree:**

$$A[1]=50,\ A[2]=30,\ A[3]=70,\ A[4]=10,\ A[5]=40,\ A[6]=60,\ A[7]=80$$

**Step 3 — Construct tree structure** (parent-child relationships):

```
              50          (index 1, root)
             /  \
           30    70       (indices 2, 3)
          / \   / \
        10  40 60  80    (indices 4,5,6,7)
```

**Step 4 — Verify height:** Tree has 3 levels, height $h = 2$. Max nodes allowed $= 2^3 - 1 = 7$. ✓

**Step 5 — Verify parent formulas:** 
- Node at index 5 (value 40): parent = $\lfloor 5/2 \rfloor = 2$ → parent is value 30. ✓
- Node at index 7 (value 80): parent = $\lfloor 7/2 \rfloor = 3$ → parent is value 70. ✓

---

### 8.6 Worked Example: Traversal Tracing

> **Tree:**
>
> ```
>           A
>          / \
>         B   C
>        / \   \
>       D   E   F
> ```

**Step 1 — Inorder (L → D → R):**
- Go left to B → go left to D (leaf) → visit **D**.
- Back to B → visit **B**.
- Go right to E (leaf) → visit **E**.
- Back to A → visit **A**.
- Go right to C → no left → visit **C**.
- Go right to F → visit **F**.

$$\text{Inorder} = D, B, E, A, C, F$$

**Step 2 — Preorder (D → L → R):**

$$\text{Preorder} = A, B, D, E, C, F$$

**Step 3 — Postorder (L → R → D):**

$$\text{Postorder} = D, E, B, F, C, A$$

**Step 4 — Level Order (BFS):**

$$\text{Level Order} = A, B, C, D, E, F$$

**Step 5 — Verification — distinct sequences:** ✓ All four traversals produce distinct outputs, confirming this tree is well-defined.

---

### 8.7 Worked Example: Tree Reconstruction from Preorder + Inorder

> **Given:** Preorder = `A B D E C F`, Inorder = `D B E A C F`. Reconstruct the tree.

**Step 1 — First element of Preorder is always the ROOT** → Root = `A`.

**Step 2 — Find `A` in Inorder** → Inorder becomes `D B E | A | C F`.
- Left subtree Inorder: `D B E`
- Right subtree Inorder: `C F`

**Step 3 — Apply recursively to left subtree:**
- Preorder for left = `B D E` (next 3 from preorder).
- First element = `B` → left subtree root.
- Inorder of left = `D B E` → `B` is at position 1 → left of `B`: `D`, right of `B`: `E`.

**Step 4 — Apply recursively to right subtree:**
- Preorder for right = `C F`.
- First = `C` → right subtree root.
- Inorder of right = `C F` → `C` at position 0 → left of `C`: empty, right of `C`: `F`.

**Step 5 — Reconstructed tree:**

```
        A
       / \
      B   C
     / \   \
    D   E   F
```

✓ Matches the tree from §8.6.

---

## 9. ANALYTICAL COMPLEXITY MATRIX

| Operation | Best Case $O(\cdot)$ | Average Case $O(\cdot)$ | Worst Case $O(\cdot)$ | Space |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|-------|
| Traversal (all 4) | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ stack + $O(n)$ level-order |
| Search (unbalanced) | $O(1)$ at root | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ skewed | $O(h)$ |
| Insertion (leaf) | $O(1)$ at root | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ skewed | $O(h)$ |
| Height computation | — | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ recursion |
| Copy tree | — | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ |
| Mirror inversion | — | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ |

### 10.1 Classification Hierarchy of Binary Trees

graph TD

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 12. KTU-STYLE PRACTICE QUESTIONS

### 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Distinguish between a Full Binary Tree and a Complete Binary Tree with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Full (Strict) Binary Tree** is one in which every node has either **0 or 2 children** — no node has exactly one child. Example:
>
> ```
>       A
>      / \
>     B   C
>    / \
>   D   E
> ```
>
> A **Complete Binary Tree (CBT)** is one in which all levels are completely filled except possibly the last, which is filled from **left to right**. Example:
>
> ```
>       A
>      / \
>     B   C
>    / \  /
>   D  E F
> ```
>
> **Key Distinction:** A Full tree cares about *degree* of every node; a Complete tree cares about *positional filling* across levels. A **Perfect binary tree** is both Full AND Complete AND has all leaves at the same depth.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**State and prove the relation $L = I + 1$ for a binary tree where every node has 0 or 2 children.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Statement:** In a binary tree where every internal node has exactly 2 children, the number of leaves $L$ is one more than the number of internal nodes $I$.
>
> **Proof:** Let total nodes $n = I + L$ and total edges $E = n - 1$ (property of any tree). Each internal node contributes **2 outgoing edges** (to its 2 children) and each leaf contributes **0 outgoing edges**. Therefore:
>
> $$2I + 0 \cdot L = E = n - 1 = I + L - 1$$
>
> $$\Rightarrow 2I = I + L - 1 \Rightarrow \boxed{L = I + 1}$$
>
> Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

### 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** *Explain the different representations of a binary tree in detail with examples. Mention their merits and demerits.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *For the given binary tree, write the inorder, preorder, postorder, and level order traversals.* **(7 Marks)**

```
            1
           / \
          2   3
         / \   \
        4   5   6
       /     \
      7       8
```

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Binary Tree Representations:**

**1. Array (Sequential) Representation:**

A binary tree is stored in a 1-D array. For a node at index $i$ (0-indexed):

- Left child index $= 2i + 1$
- Right child index $= 2i + 2$
- Parent index $= \lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$

**Example:** Tree `1 → 2, 3 → 4, 5 → 6`

| Index | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|-------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Value | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | — | — |

**Merits:**
- Simple, cache-friendly, no pointers.
- Direct parent/child computation.

**Demerits:**
- Memory wastage for skewed trees (needs $2^h - 1$ slots for $h+1$ nodes).
- Inflexible size (must declare maximum).
- Insertion/deletion costly due to shifting.

**2. Linked Representation:**

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *left;
    struct Node *right;
};
```

Each node dynamically allocated; pointers connect parent to children.

**Merits:**
