# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Min-Heap Construction & Operations

**Problem:** Insert the keys $5, 12, 3, 8, 7, 1, 9$ into an **initially empty Min-Heap**, one by one. After all insertions, perform **two successive `EXTRACT-MIN` operations** and show the final heap.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Insertions (with Mermaid Visualizations)

**Step 1 — Insert 5:** Heap contains only root. Property trivially holds.

**Step 2 — Insert 12:** Place at next available position (left child of 5). No violation.

**Step 3 — Insert 3:** Place at right child of 5. Compare with parent 5: $3 < 5$ → **violates** Min-Heap order. Swap 3 ↔ 5. New root is 3.

**Step 4 — Insert 8:** Place at left child of 12. Compare with parent 12: $8 < 12$ → violates. Swap 8 ↔ 12.

**Step 5 — Insert 7:** Place at right child of 12. Compare with parent 12: $7 < 12$ → violates. Swap 7 ↔ 12.

**Step 6 — Insert 1:** Place at left child of 8. Compare with parent 8: $1 < 8$ → violates. Swap 1 ↔ 8. Now at root position; compare with parent (none). Stop.

**Step 7 — Insert 9:** Place at right child of 8. Compare with parent 8: $9 > 8$ → **no violation**. Stop.

### 3.1.2 Final Min-Heap State (After All 7 Insertions)

The final array (1-indexed) is:

$$
A = [\_,\ 1,\ 3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 7,\ 12,\ 9]
$$

> *(Index 0 is unused/dummy in 1-indexed representation.)*

### 3.1.3 `EXTRACT-MIN` Operation #1

**Step A:** Root value $1$ is saved (this is the minimum). 
**Step B:** Move the last leaf (9) to the root. Heap size becomes 6. 
**Step C:** Heapify root: $9$ has children $3$ and $5$. Smaller child is $3$. $9 > 3$ → swap 9 ↔ 3. 
**Step D:** At index 3, $9$ has children $8$ and $7$. Smaller is $7$. $9 > 7$ → swap 9 ↔ 7. 
**Step E:** $9$ is now at index 6 (a leaf). Stop.

Array after 1st extract-min:

$$
A = [\_,\ 3,\ 7,\ 5,\ 8,\ 9,\ 12]
$$

### 3.1.4 `EXTRACT-MIN` Operation #2

**Step A:** Save root $3$. 
**Step B:** Move last leaf (12) to root. Heap size 5. 
**Step C:** Heapify root: children are $7$ and $5$. Smaller is $5$. $12 > 5$ → swap. 
**Step D:** At index 3, $12$ has child $9$ (left) and no right child. $12 > 9$ → swap. 
**Step E:** $12$ now at index 6 (leaf). Stop.

Final array after 2nd extract-min:

$$
A = [\_,\ 5,\ 7,\ 9,\ 8,\ 12]
$$

---

## 3.2 `HEAPIFY` Algorithm — Pseudocode & Trace

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 expects algorithmic tracing on paper.** Memorize both the pseudocode and the array-state evolution. The following pseudocode operates on a Min-Heap.

```
HEAPIFY(A, i):
    left  ← 2 * i
    right ← 2 * i + 1
    smallest ← i

    if left ≤ heap_size and A[left] < A[smallest]:
        smallest ← left
    if right ≤ heap_size and A[right] < A[smallest]:
        smallest ← right

    if smallest ≠ i:
        swap A[i] ↔ A[smallest]
        HEAPIFY(A, smallest)   // Recurse on the affected subtree
```

### 3.2.1 Trace of `HEAPIFY(A, 2)` on Array $A = [\_, 10, 5, 3, 4, 2, 7]$

| Call | $i$ | $L(i)$ | $R(i)$ | `smallest` | Action | Array After |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 2 | 4 (`A[4]=4`) | 5 (`A[5]=2`) | 5 | $A[5]=2 < A[2]=10$ | `[_, 10, 5, 3, 4, 2, 7]` |
| 2 | 5 | 10 | 11 | 5 | No swap (out of bounds) | `[_, 10, 5, 3, 4, 2, 7]` |
| **Back to Call 1** | 2 | — | — | 5 | Swap $A[2] \leftrightarrow A[5]$ | `[_, 10, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7]` |
| Recurse | 5 | — | — | 5 | No children in subtree | Done |

> [!NOTE]
> **Depth of recursion = 1 swap.** In a heap with $n$ elements, the **maximum recursion depth** of `HEAPIFY` is $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$, because the call moves strictly downward toward the leaves.

---

## 3.3 `BUILD-HEAP` Algorithm — Pseudocode

```
BUILD-HEAP(A):
    heap_size ← A.length
    for i ← ⌊heap_size / 2⌋ down to 1:
        HEAPIFY(A, i)
```

**Why start at $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$?**
Because the elements at indices $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1, \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 2, \ldots, n$ are all **leaves** of the complete binary tree. A leaf is a heap of size 1 — no work needed. We only need to "fix" the **internal nodes**, of which there are exactly $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$.

---

## 3.4 The Rigorous Proof: Why `BUILD-HEAP` is $O(n)$, Not $O(n \log n)$

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 3. We derive the tight time bound by computing the total work performed by all `HEAPIFY` calls.

### 3.4.1 Setup

- Total nodes: $n$.
- Height of tree: $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$.
- The number of nodes at height $k$ (counting from bottom, where leaves are at height 0) is at most $\lceil n / 2^{k+1} \rceil$.
- A `HEAPIFY` call on a node at height $k$ performs at most $O(k)$ work.

### 3.4.2 The Summation (Summing the Worst-Case Work)

Total work $T(n)$ is bounded above by:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{h} k \cdot (\text{number of nodes at height } k)
$$

Substituting the upper bound for the number of nodes at height $k$:

$$
T(n) \leq \sum_{k=0}^{h} k \cdot \left\lceil \frac{n}{2^{k+1}} \right\rceil \leq \sum_{k=0}^{h} k \cdot \frac{n}{2^{k}}
$$

**Factoring out the constant $n$:**

$$
T(n) \leq n \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{h} \frac{k}{2^{k}}
$$

### 3.4.3 Evaluating the Infinite Geometric-Arithmetic Series

The infinite sum $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{k}{2^k}$ converges to a finite constant. We derive it using the standard identity for $\sum k x^k$ with $x = 1/2$:

Let $S = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k x^k$. Using the known closed form $S = \frac{x}{(1-x)^2}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^k = \frac{1/2}{(1 - 1/2)^2} = \frac{1/2}{1/4} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.4 The Final Bound

Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &\leq n \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{h} \frac{k}{2^k} \leq n \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{k}{2^k} = n \cdot 2 = 2n
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $T(n) = O(n)$. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Behind the $O(n)$ Bound:** Most of the work in `BUILD-HEAP` is done at the **leaves** (the bottom of the tree). But wait — `HEAPIFY` is *not* called on leaves! The work is done at the **internal nodes**, and the number of internal nodes decreases exponentially as we go up. Conversely, while the root takes $O(\log n)$ time, there is only **1 root**. The "many cheap calls + few expensive calls" combination yields a linear total.

---

## 3.5 Full `HEAPSORT` Trace

**Input Array (1-indexed):** $A = [\_, 4, 10, 3, 5, 1, 8, 7, 9, 2]$

**Phase 1 — `BUILD-HEAP` (Build Max-Heap):** $n = 9$, start $i = 4$.

| $i$ | Subtree Action | Array After |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 4 | Children 9, 8. Swap 5 ↔ 9. | `[_, 4, 10, 3, 9, 1, 8, 7, 5, 2]` |
| 3 | Children 7, 2. Swap 3 ↔ 7. | `[_, 4, 10, 7, 9, 1, 8, 3, 5, 2]` |
| 2 | Children 9, 1. Swap 10 ↔ 9; recurse on 4. | `[_, 4, 9, 7, 10, 1, 8, 3, 5, 2]` |
| 2 | At 4: children 5, 2. Swap 10 ↔ 5. | `[_, 4, 9, 7, 5, 1, 8, 3, 10, 2]` |
| 1 | Children 9, 7. Swap 4 ↔ 9; recurse on 2. | `[_, 9, 4, 7, 5, 1, 8, 3, 10, 2]` |
| 1 | At 2: children 5, 1. Swap 4 ↔ 5. | `[_, 9, 5, 7, 4, 1, 8, 3, 10, 2]` |

**Phase 2 — Repeated Extract-Max:** Swap root with last, shrink, heapify. After full execution, the array becomes:

$$
A_{\text{sorted}} = [\_, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10]
$$

---

## 3.6 The Priority Queue ADT — Specification & Heap Implementation

### 3.6.1 Abstract Specification

A **Priority Queue** is an ADT that manages a set of elements, each carrying a **priority (key)**, supporting the following operations:

| Operation | Description | Return Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `INSERT(S, x)` | Insert element $x$ into set $S$ | None |
| `MINIMUM(S)` / `MAXIMUM(S)` | Return element with smallest/largest key | Element reference (no removal) |
| `EXTRACT-MIN(S)` / `EXTRACT-MAX(S)` | Remove and return the extreme element | Element |
| `DECREASE-KEY(S, x, k)` / `INCREASE-KEY(S, x, k)` | Decrease/increase $x$'s key to $k$ | None |

### 3.6.2 Heap-Based Realization of All Operations

| ADT Operation | Heap Realization | Time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `INSERT` | Add at last leaf, bubble up | $O(\log n)$ |
| `MINIMUM` / `MAXIMUM` | Read root | $O(1)$ |
| `EXTRACT-MIN` / `EXTRACT-MAX` | Save root, move last leaf up, bubble down | $O(\log n)$ |
| `DECREASE-KEY` | Update, bubble up | $O(\log n)$ |
| `INCREASE-KEY` | Update, bubble down | $O(\log n)$ |
| `DELETE` (arbitrary) | `DECREASE-KEY` to $-\infty$, then `EXTRACT-MIN` | $O(\log n)$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Heap Engine

The following diagram maps the internal functional architecture of a heap-powered Priority Queue, showing how user-level ADT calls map to internal heap operations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Application Layer] --> B[Priority Queue ADT Interface]
    B --> C1[INSERT element, key]
    B --> C2[EXTRACT-MIN]
    B --> C3[MINIMUM peek]
    B --> C4[DECREASE-KEY]
    C1 --> D1[Add to array end at index n plus 1]
    D1 --> D2[Percolate UP bubble up to restore heap order]
    C2 --> E1[Save A1 root value]
    E1 --> E2[Copy A n to A1]
    E2 --> E3[Decrement heap size]
    E3 --> E4[Max Heapify at root to restore order]
    C3 --> F1[Return A1 without modification]
    C4 --> G1[Update A i to new smaller key]
    G1 --> G2[Percolate UP from index i]
    D2 --> H[Heap Order Property Maintained]
    E4 --> H
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Array A 1 to n as Min Heap]
```

The following diagram illustrates the final tree structure of a Min-Heap built from keys $5, 12, 3, 8, 7, 1, 9$ (matches §3.1.2).

```mermaid
    node1[1]

```

## 4.3 The `EXTRACT-MIN` Operation Flow

The following diagram traces the first `EXTRACT-MIN` operation from the heap in §3.1.3, showing how the last leaf is moved to the root and then sifted down.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Heap root equals 1] --> B[Save root 1 as output]
    B --> C[Move last leaf 9 to root position]
    C --> D[Reduce heap size from 7 to 6]
    D --> E[Heapify root 9]
    E --> F{Compare 9 with children 3 and 5}
    F --> G[Swap 9 with smaller child 3]
    G --> H{Compare 9 with children 8 and 7}
    H --> I[Swap 9 with smaller child 7]
    I --> J[9 reaches leaf position at index 6]
    J --> K[Heap property restored final heap root equals 3]
```
