## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Working Example Graph

To make derivations concrete, we use the following **undirected, unweighted graph** with 7 vertices and 8 edges throughout. The adjacency list is in **alphabetical order** (standard KTU convention).

**Edges:**  
$\{A,B\},\ \{A,C\},\ \{B,D\},\ \{B,E\},\ \{C,F\},\ \{C,G\},\ \{D,E\},\ \{E,F\}$

**Adjacency List Representation:**

$$Adj(A) = \{B,\ C\}$$
$$Adj(B) = \{A,\ D,\ E\}$$
$$Adj(C) = \{A,\ F,\ G\}$$
$$Adj(D) = \{B,\ E\}$$
$$Adj(E) = \{B,\ D,\ F\}$$
$$Adj(F) = \{C,\ E\}$$
$$Adj(G) = \{C\}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive BFS Trace from Source $s = A$

We maintain three structures: `Queue Q`, `visited[]`, and `distance[]`.

**Initial State:**
$$visited = [A: F,\ B: F,\ C: F,\ D: F,\ E: F,\ F: F,\ G: F]$$
$$distance = [A: \infty,\ B: \infty,\ C: \infty,\ D: \infty,\ E: \infty,\ F: \infty,\ G: \infty]$$
$$Q = \emptyset$$

**Step 1 — Process A (enqueue A):**
$$Q = [A], \quad visited[A] = T, \quad distance[A] = 0$$

**Step 2 — Dequeue A; process its unvisited neighbours B, C:**
$$Q = [B,\ C], \quad visited[B] = T, \ distance[B] = 1, \ parent[B] = A$$
$$visited[C] = T, \ distance[C] = 1, \ parent[C] = A$$

**Step 3 — Dequeue B; its neighbours are A, D, E. A is visited. Process D, E:**
$$Q = [C,\ D,\ E], \quad visited[D] = T,\ distance[D] = 2,\ parent[D] = B$$
$$visited[E] = T,\ distance[E] = 2,\ parent[E] = B$$

**Step 4 — Dequeue C; its neighbours are A, F, G. A is visited. Process F, G:**
$$Q = [D,\ E,\ F,\ G], \quad visited[F] = T,\ distance[F] = 2,\ parent[F] = C$$
$$visited[G] = T,\ distance[G] = 2,\ parent[G] = C$$

**Step 5 — Dequeue D; neighbours B, E already visited. No new enqueues:**
$$Q = [E,\ F,\ G]$$

**Step 6 — Dequeue E; neighbours B, D, F all visited. No new enqueues:**
$$Q = [F,\ G]$$

**Step 7 — Dequeue F; neighbours C, E all visited. No new enqueues:**
$$Q = [G]$$

**Step 8 — Dequeue G; neighbour C is visited. No new enqueues:**
$$Q = [\ ] \ \Rightarrow \ \textbf{HALT}$$

**BFS Final Output Order (Visit Order):**

$$\boxed{A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow G}$$

**BFS Distance Vector (Shortest Edge-Distance from A):**

$$d(A)=0,\ d(B)=1,\ d(C)=1,\ d(D)=2,\ d(E)=2,\ d(F)=2,\ d(G)=2$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that vertices at distance 1 (B, C) come first, then all at distance 2 (D, E, F, G). This is the signature "ripple" property of BFS.

### 3.3 Exhaustive DFS Trace (Recursive) from Source $s = A$

We maintain `visited[]` and `parent[]` and a **discovery time** `d[]` and **finish time** `f[]` for KTU-style edge classification.

**Global Time Counter:** `time = 0`

**Call DFS(A):**
- Mark A visited. $d[A] = 1$. `time = 1`.
- Neighbours of A in order: B, C.
  - **Call DFS(B):**
    - Mark B visited. $d[B] = 2$. `time = 2`. $parent[B] = A$.
    - Neighbours of B in order: A, D, E.
      - A already visited → skip.
      - **Call DFS(D):**
        - Mark D visited. $d[D] = 3$. `time = 3`. $parent[D] = B$.
        - Neighbours of D: B, E.
          - B already visited.
          - **Call DFS(E):**
            - Mark E visited. $d[E] = 4$. `time = 4`. $parent[E] = D$.
            - Neighbours of E: B, D, F.
              - B visited.
              - D visited.
              - **Call DFS(F):**
                - Mark F visited. $d[F] = 5$. `time = 5`. $parent[F] = E$.
                - Neighbours of F: C, E.
                  - C not visited → **Call DFS(C):**
                    - Mark C visited. $d[C] = 6$. `time = 6`. $parent[C] = F$.
                    - Neighbours of C: A, F, G.
                      - A visited.
                      - F visited.
                      - **Call DFS(G):**
                        - Mark G visited. $d[G] = 7$. `time = 7`. $parent[G] = C$.
                        - Neighbours of G: C. Visited.
                        - $f[G] = 8$. `time = 8`. **Return from DFS(G).**
                    - $f[C] = 9$. `time = 9`. **Return from DFS(C).**
                - $f[F] = 10$. `time = 10`. **Return from DFS(F).**
            - $f[E] = 11$. `time = 11`. **Return from DFS(E).**
        - $f[D] = 12$. `time = 12`. **Return from DFS(D).**
    - Neighbours of B left: E (already visited).
    - $f[B] = 13$. `time = 13`. **Return from DFS(B).**
  - Neighbours of A left: C (already visited).
  - $f[A] = 14$. `time = 14`. **Return from DFS(A).**

**DFS Final Output Order (Visit Order):**

$$\boxed{A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow C \rightarrow G}$$

**Discovery/Finish Time Table:**

| Vertex | $d$ (discovery) | $f$ (finish) | Parent |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1 | 14 | NIL |
| B | 2 | 13 | A |
| D | 3 | 12 | B |
| E | 4 | 11 | D |
| F | 5 | 10 | E |
| C | 6 | 9 | F |
| G | 7 | 8 | C |

**Verification using the Parenthesis Theorem:**
For every two vertices $u$ and $v$, exactly one of the following holds:
- Intervals $[d(u), f(u)]$ and $[d(v), f(v)]$ are **disjoint** (no ancestor relation), OR
- One interval is **strictly nested** inside the other (one is the ancestor of the other).

For example, $A$'s interval $[1, 14]$ contains $B$'s $[2, 13]$, which contains $D$'s $[3, 12]$, which contains $E$'s $[4, 11]$, etc. — the **nesting property is satisfied** ✅.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix for Our Example

| Property | BFS Result | DFS Result |
|---|---|---|
| Output Order | A, B, C, D, E, F, G | A, B, D, E, F, C, G |
| Shortest dist to D | **2** (A→B→D) | 2 (same here, but not guaranteed in general) |
| Shortest dist to G | **2** (A→C→G) | 6 (A→B→D→E→F→C→G) — **not shortest!** |
| Queue Max Size | 4 (at end of step 4) | N/A (uses stack) |
| Stack Max Depth | N/A | 7 (A→B→D→E→F→C→G) |
| Back Edge Found? | No | No (the graph is acyclic in DFS tree) |

> [!TIP]
> **Notice** that DFS found a path of length **6** to $G$, but BFS found a path of length **2**. This single example concretely proves that **DFS is NOT optimal for shortest path**, while BFS is.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reference Graph (Logical Layout)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[A] --- nodeB[B]
    nodeA[A] --- nodeC[C]
    nodeB[B] --- nodeD[D]
    nodeB[B] --- nodeE[E]
    nodeC[C] --- nodeF[F]
    nodeC[C] --- nodeG[G]
    nodeD[D] --- nodeE[E]
    nodeE[E] --- nodeF[F]
```

### 4.2 BFS Traversal Flow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START BFS from A]) --> initQ[Initialise Queue Q = empty]
    initQ --> markA[Mark visited A = true]
    markA --> enqA[Enqueue A into Q]
    enqA --> checkQ{Q is empty?}
    checkQ -- No --> deq[Dequeue front vertex u]
    deq --> procU[Process / Print u]
    procU --> iter[For each v in Adj of u]
    iter --> checkV{v is visited?}
    checkV -- No --> markV[Mark visited v = true]
    markV --> setDist[Set distance of v = distance of u + 1]
    setDist --> setPar[Set parent of v = u]
    setPar --> enqV[Enqueue v into Q]
    enqV --> moreN{More neighbours?}
    checkV -- Yes --> moreN
    moreN -- Yes --> iter
    moreN -- No --> checkQ
    checkQ -- Yes --> stopNode([HALT: All reachable vertices visited])
```

### 4.3 DFS Traversal Flow — Block Architecture (Recursive)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START DFS from A]) --> callA[Call DFS A]
    callA --> markA[Mark A visited, set d of A = 1]
    markA --> iterA[For each v in Adj of A]
    iterA --> checkV{v is visited?}
    checkV -- No --> callV[Call DFS v]
    callV --> recurse[Mark v visited, set d of v, parent of v = u]
    recurse --> moreU{More neighbours of u?}
    checkV -- Yes --> moreU
    moreU -- Yes --> iterA
    moreU -- No --> finishU[Set f of u, return to caller]
    finishU --> checkRoot{Root call done?}
    checkRoot -- No --> iterA
    checkRoot -- Yes --> stopNode([HALT: DFS Forest complete])
```

### 4.4 BFS Level Tree (BFS Tree Edges Highlighted)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[A - Level 0] --> level1B[B - Level 1]
    rootA[A - Level 0] --> level1C[C - Level 1]
    level1B[B - Level 1] --> level2D[D - Level 2]
    level1B[B - Level 1] --> level2E[E - Level 2]
    level1C[C - Level 1] --> level2F[F - Level 2]
    level1C[C - Level 1] --> level2G[G - Level 2]
```

### 4.5 DFS Tree (Tree Edges Highlighted, from Section 3.3)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[A] --> childB[B]
    childB[B] --> childD[D]
    childD[D] --> childE[E]
    childE[E] --> childF[F]
    childF[F] --> childC[C]
    childC[C] --> childG[G]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The **BFS tree** is wide and shallow — every level is fully explored before descending. The **DFS tree** is narrow and deep — it descends all the way to a leaf before backtracking.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**  
**Differentiate between Breadth First Search (BFS) and Depth First Search (DFS) in terms of the data structure used and the order of vertex traversal. Mention one application where BFS is preferred over DFS and justify your answer in 2–3 lines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| Data Structure | **Queue (FIFO)** | **Stack (LIFO) or Recursion** |
| Traversal Order | Level-by-level (visit all neighbours before going deeper) | Branch-by-branch (go as deep as possible, then backtrack) |

**Application where BFS is preferred:** Finding the **shortest path in an unweighted graph**.  
**Justification:** BFS explores vertices in non-decreasing order of their distance from the source. The first time a vertex is dequeued, the path traversed is provably the shortest. DFS may find *a* path but not the *shortest* one, as it commits to one branch before exploring alternatives.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for data structures, 1 mark for traversal order, 1 mark for application + justification.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**  
**State the time and space complexity of BFS and DFS. With the help of a small example, explain why DFS alone cannot be used to find the shortest path between two vertices in an unweighted graph.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Complexity (1 mark):** Both BFS and DFS run in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ time and $O(\vert V \vert)$ auxiliary space.

**Why DFS fails for shortest path (2 marks):**  
Consider a graph with vertices $\{A, B, C, D\}$ and edges $\{A-B,\ A-C,\ B-D,\ C-D\}$. Starting DFS from $A$ with neighbours processed in order $(B, C)$, the order of discovery is $A, B, D, C$. The path to $D$ found is $A \to B \to D$ (length 2), which happens to be shortest here. But if edges were $\{A-B,\ A-C,\ C-D,\ B-D\}$ and the DFS order visits $C$ first, then $A \to C \to D$ (length 2) — also shortest. The flaw becomes evident in deeper graphs: a DFS that descends deep into a long branch may reach a destination via a long path, when a BFS would have found a much shorter alternative route. Thus, **DFS is not optimal for unweighted shortest paths.**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]`**

**(a)** For the graph shown below, construct the **adjacency list** representation and perform **BFS starting from vertex $A$**. Show the state of the queue, the `visited[]` array, and the `distance[]` array at every step. **List the order in which vertices are visited.** **(7 marks)**

**Graph Edges (undirected, unweighted):**  
$\{A, B\},\ \{A, C\},\ \{B, D\},\ \{B, E\},\ \{C, F\},\ \{C, G\},\ \{D, E\},\ \{E, F\}$

**(b)** Using the BFS tree constructed in part (a), reconstruct the **shortest path from $A$ to $G$**. Show how the `parent[]` array enables path reconstruction. **State and prove** the property of BFS that guarantees optimality in an unweighted graph. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Adjacency List (1 mark):**

| Vertex | Neighbours (alphabetical) |
|---|---|
| A | B, C |
| B | A, D, E |
| C | A, F, G |
| D | B, E |
| E | B, D, F |
| F | C, E |
| G | C |

**BFS Trace (5 marks):**

| Step | Dequeue (Process) | Queue After Enqueue | Visited Set | Distance (A,B,C,D,E,F,G) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | [B, C] | {A} | (0, 1, 1, ∞, ∞, ∞, ∞) |
| 2 | B | [C, D, E] | {A, B, C} | (0, 1, 1, 2, 2, ∞, ∞) |
| 3 | C | [D, E, F, G] | {A, B, C, D, E} | (0, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2) |
| 4 | D | [E, F, G] | same | same |
| 5 | E | [F, G] | same | same |
| 6 | F | [G] | same | same |
| 7 | G | [ ] | {A, B, C, D, E, F, G} | (0, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2) |

**Visit Order (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow G}$$

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Parent[] array from BFS (1 mark):**

| Vertex | A | B | C | D | E | F | G |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| parent | NIL | A | A | B | B | C | C |

**Shortest Path from A to G (2 marks):**  
Trace backwards from $G$ using `parent[]`:  
$G \xleftarrow{\text{parent}} C \xleftarrow{\text{parent}} A$  
**Path:** $A \to C \to G$ (length 2).

**Proof of BFS Optimality (4 marks):**

> **Theorem:** In an unweighted, undirected graph, BFS computes the shortest path (minimum number of edges) from the source to every reachable vertex.

> **Proof (by contradiction):**  
> Suppose BFS first discovers a vertex $v$ via a path of length $k+1$ from source $s$, but there exists an alternative path from $s$ to $v$ of length $k$.  
> Consider the moment $v$ is first discovered. BFS discovers vertices in non-decreasing order of distance because every newly enqueued vertex has distance exactly one more than its predecessor in the queue.  
> At step $k$, all vertices at distance $k$ from $s$ have been enqueued (and possibly processed). Hence, $v$ (which is at distance $k$) **must already have been enqueued by step $k$**, contradicting that $v$ is first discovered at step $k+1$.  
> Therefore, the first discovery distance is the minimum distance, and the BFS parent chain gives the shortest path. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`**

**(a)** For the same graph (edges $\{A,B\},\ \{A,C\},\ \{B,D\},\ \{B,E\},\ \{C,F\},\ \{C,G\},\ \{D,E\},\ \{E,F\}$), perform **DFS starting from vertex $A$** using the **recursive formulation**. Show the **call stack** at every recursion, the discovery time $d[v]$ and finish time $f[v]$ of every vertex, and list the **visit order**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify every edge of the graph as a **Tree, Back, Forward, or Cross edge** using the discovery and finish times from part (a). Using the edge classification, **prove whether the graph contains a cycle**. Also, list the **DFS forest** produced. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Recursive Call Stack Trace (5 marks):**

| Step | Active Call (top = current) | Visited Added | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DFS(A) | A | d[A]=1, push A |
| 2 | DFS(A) → DFS(B) | A, B | d[B]=2, push B |
| 3 | DFS(A) → DFS(B) → DFS(D) | A, B, D | d[D]=3, push D |
| 4 | DFS(A) → DFS(B) → DFS(D) → DFS(E) | A, B, D, E | d[E]=4, push E |
| 5 | ... → DFS(F) | A, B, D, E, F | d[F]=5, push F |
| 6 | ... → DFS(F) → DFS(C) | A, B, D, E, F, C | d[C]=6, push C |
| 7 | ... → DFS(C) → DFS(G) | A, B, D, E, F, C, G | d[G]=7, push G |
| 8 | Return DFS(G) | — | f[G]=8, pop G |
| 9 | Return DFS(C) | — | f[C]=9, pop C |
| 10 | Return DFS(F) | — | f[F]=10, pop F |
| 11 | Return DFS(E) | — | f[E]=11, pop E |
| 12 | Return DFS(D) | — | f[D]=12, pop D |
| 13 | Return DFS(B) | — | f[B]=13, pop B |
| 14 | Return DFS(A) | — | f[A]=14, pop A |

**Discovery/Finish Time Table (1 mark):**

| Vertex | A | B | C | D | E | F | G |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| d[v] | 1 | 2 | 6 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 7 |
| f[v] | 14 | 13 | 9 | 12 | 11 | 10 | 8 |
| parent | NIL | A | F | B | D | E | C |

**DFS Visit Order (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow C \rightarrow G}$$

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Edge Classification (4 marks):**

For undirected graph edges, BFS/DFS treats them as a pair (one directed copy in each direction). The classification rules using timestamps are:

- **Tree edge** $(u, v)$: $v$ is discovered via $u$ in the DFS tree.
- **Back edge** $(u, v)$: $v$ is an ancestor of $u$ in the DFS tree.
- **Forward edge** $(u, v)$: $v$ is a descendant of $u$ (not direct tree edge).
- **Cross edge** $(u, v)$: $v$ is neither ancestor nor descendant of $u$.

| Edge | Lower endpoint first discovered? | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| A–B | d[B]=2 via A → Tree | Tree | d[A]=1 < d[B]=2; B is direct child |
| A–C | d[C]=6 via F → Tree(of F) but A → C is back | **Back** | A's interval [1,14] contains C's [6,9] |
| B–D | d[D]=3 via B → Tree | Tree | Direct child |
| B–E | d[E]=4 via D (not B) → B→E is forward | **Forward** | E is descendant of B (B's interval contains E's), but not direct child |
| C–F | d[F]=5 via E (not C) → C→F is back | **Back** | F's interval [5,10] is inside C's [6,9]? No — 5 < 6. C's interval [6,9] is inside F's [5,10], so C is descendant of F. Thus F→C is Tree, and C→F is **Back**. |
| C–G | d[G]=7 via C → Tree | Tree | Direct child |
| D–E | d[E]=4 via D → Tree | Tree | Direct child |
| E–F | d[F]=5 via E → Tree | Tree | Direct child |

> **Reclassification in Summary Form (undirected graph):**
> - **Tree edges:** (A,B), (B,D), (D,E), (E,F), (C,G) — 5 edges
> - **Back edges:** (A,C), (B,E), (C,F) — 3 edges
> - **Forward edges:** (B,E) — already in back (for undirected graphs, back/forward collapse)
> - **Cross edges:** None

**For an undirected graph, every non-tree edge is classified as a Back edge** (the distinction between back/forward/cross only matters for *directed* graphs in KTU's Module 3). The 3 back edges are: (A,C), (B,E), (C,F).

**Cycle Detection via Back Edges (2 marks):**

> **Theorem:** An undirected graph contains a cycle **if and only if** the DFS tree contains at least one **back edge**.

> **Proof (⇒):** If the graph has a cycle, pick any vertex $v$ on the cycle. As DFS visits the cycle, the first non-tree edge encountered must point to an ancestor (the vertex on the cycle that was already visited), forming a back edge.
>
> **Proof (⇐):** A back edge $(u, v)$ with $v$ as ancestor of $u$ together with the tree edges from $v$ to $u$ forms a closed walk, i.e., a cycle. $\blacksquare$

**Conclusion:** Since the DFS produced **3 back edges** — (A,C), (B,E), (C,F) — the graph contains **at least one cycle** (in fact, multiple). ✅

**DFS Forest (1 mark):** The DFS Forest consists of a single tree rooted at $A$ (since the graph is connected):

$$\text{Root: } A \to \{B \to \{D \to \{E \to \{F \to \{C \to \{G\}\}\}\}\}\}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Tree and Back Edges in Undirected Graphs:** In undirected DFS, every non-tree edge appears *twice* (once in each direction). The edge is a Tree edge if it leads to a new vertex; otherwise it is a Back edge. Forward and Cross edges only arise in *directed* graphs.
> 2. **Forgetting to mark `visited[]` when *enqueueing* in BFS:** A vertex must be marked visited **at the moment of enqueue**, not at the moment of dequeue. Otherwise, the same vertex may be enqueued multiple times, inflating time complexity.
> 3. **Confusing BFS distance with weighted shortest path:** BFS optimality holds only for **unweighted** graphs. For weighted graphs, use **Dijkstra's** (positive weights) or **Bellman-Ford** (negative weights).
> 4. **Skipping the queue/stack trace in BFS/DFS questions:** KTU examiners award **2–3 marks** specifically for the step-by-step queue/stack state. Skipping this costs you a substantial chunk of marks.
> 5. **Stating BFS/DFS are $O(n^2)$ or $O(n \log n)$:** The correct complexity is always $\mathbf{O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}$. Writing $O(n^2)$ for a dense graph is acceptable **only** if the adjacency matrix form is explicitly used; the default for adjacency list is $\mathbf{O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — BFS & DFS**

- ✅ **BFS** uses a **Queue (FIFO)**; **DFS** uses a **Stack (LIFO)** or **recursion**.
- ✅ BFS and DFS both run in **$O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$** time and **$O(\vert V \vert)$** space.
- ✅ BFS is **optimal for shortest path in unweighted graphs**; DFS is **not optimal** for shortest path.
- ✅ BFS is **complete** for finite graphs; DFS is **complete** for finite graphs but may get stuck in infinite branches in infinite graphs.
- ✅ **Back edge in DFS ⇒ cycle** (in undirected graphs, any non-tree edge is a back edge).
- ✅ **Topological sort** is naturally derived from DFS by sorting vertices in **decreasing order of finish time** $f[v]$.
- ✅ **Bipartite test:** A graph is bipartite iff BFS/DFS can 2-colour it with no conflict at any edge.
- ✅ **Connected components:** Run BFS/DFS from every unvisited vertex; each BFS/DFS tree is one component.
- ✅ **BFS tree edges** correspond to shortest paths; **DFS tree edges** correspond to one possible (but not necessarily shortest) path.
- ✅ **Discovery time** $d[v]$ and **finish time** $f[v]$ satisfy $1 \le d[v] < f[v] \le 2\vert V \vert$ — every vertex occupies a unique time interval.
- ✅ **Parenthesis Theorem:** DFS intervals are either **disjoint** or **strictly nested** — never partially overlapping.
- ✅ KTU favourite trick: Always **show the queue/stack state at every step** in BFS/DFS problems — it carries the bulk of the marks.
- ✅ For **undirected graphs**, ignore the Forward/Cross edge distinction — only **Tree** and **Back** edges exist.
- ✅ For **directed graphs**, the **White-Path Theorem** states: edge $(u, v)$ is a Tree/Forward/Back/Cross edge depending on the colours of $v$ at the time $u$ is explored.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Graphs – Single Source All Destination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications of Graphs – Single Source All Destination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Single Source All Destination (SSSP)** problem is a classical graph optimization problem that asks: *"Given a weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with $|V|$ vertices and $|E|$ edges, and a designated source vertex $s \in V$, find the minimum cumulative cost (or distance) from $s$ to every other vertex $v \in V$."*

Formally, for every vertex $v \in V$, we must compute:

$$d[v] = \min_{P \in \mathcal{P}_{s \to v}} \sum_{(u_i, u_{i+1}) \in P} w(u_i, u_{i+1})$$

where $\mathcal{P}_{s \to v}$ denotes the set of all simple paths from $s$ to $v$. The output is a **distance array** $d[\,]$ of size $\vert V \vert$ and (optionally) a **predecessor array** $\pi[\,]$ from which the actual shortest paths can be reconstructed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Locked-In for KTU Valuation:**
> - **Source Vertex (s):** The unique starting node from which all paths originate.
> - **Distance Label d[v]:** Current best-known shortest distance from $s$ to $v$.
> - **Predecessor $\pi[v]$:** The immediate predecessor of $v$ on the shortest path from $s$.
> - **Final State:** When the algorithm terminates, $d[v]$ holds the **true shortest path distance** $\delta(s, v)$.
> - **Negative Weight Cycle:** A directed cycle whose edge weights sum to a negative value; SSSP is **undefined** in such a graph.

### 1.2 Variants Classified by KTU Board Standards

| Variant | Constraint | Recommended Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Unweighted graph | $w(e) = 1 \; \forall e \in E$ | **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** |
| Non-negative weights | $w(e) \geq 0$ | **Dijkstra's Algorithm** |
| General (including negative) | No sign restriction | **Bellman-Ford Algorithm** |
| DAG (Directed Acyclic) | Acyclic structure | **Topological-Order Relaxation** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Kerala Road Trip Intuition

Imagine you are a KTU student starting from **CET Trivandrum** (the source) and you need to determine the shortest driving time to *every other engineering college in Kerala* on a single trip.

- **Cities** → **Vertices** of the graph.
- **Roads with distance/travel-time labels** → **Weighted Edges**.
- **Your starting city** → **Source vertex $s$**.
- **The shortest possible travel time to every city** → **The SSSP distance array $d[\,]$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SSSP matters in engineering:** It is the computational backbone of **Google Maps**, **OSPF routing in the Internet**, **airline fare optimization**, **robot motion planning**, **VLSI wire-length minimization**, and even **dynamic programming on graphs**. KTU examiners frequently use network/road-map analogies to test your understanding.

### 1.4 Graph Model and Notation

Let $G = (V, E, w)$ where:

- $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ with $n = \vert V \vert$
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ for directed graphs; $E \subseteq \{\{u, v\} : u \neq v\}$ for undirected graphs
- $w : E \to \mathbb{R}$ is the weight function

Two fundamental operations define **every** SSSP algorithm:

1. **Initialization:** $d[s] \leftarrow 0$; $d[v] \leftarrow \infty \; \forall v \neq s$; $\pi[v] \leftarrow \text{NIL}$.
2. **Edge Relaxation** (on edge $(u, v, w)$): 
   $$d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u, v), \;\;\pi[v] \leftarrow u$$

The **relaxation** operation is the atomic unit of all SSSP algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Dijkstra's Algorithm — The Greedy Standard

**Dijkstra's Algorithm** solves the SSSP problem for graphs with **non-negative edge weights**. It maintains a set $S$ of vertices whose final shortest distances from $s$ are already determined, and a **min-priority queue (min-heap)** keyed on $d[\,]$.

#### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted)

1. Initialize $d[s] = 0$, $d[v] = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$, and the priority queue $Q \leftarrow V$.
2. While $Q \neq \emptyset$:
   - **Extract-Min:** $u \leftarrow \text{argmin}_{v \in Q} d[v]$ (the unvisited vertex with smallest tentative distance).
   - Add $u$ to $S$ (the finalized set).
   - **Relax all outgoing edges** from $u$: for every edge $(u, v)$ with weight $w(u, v)$, attempt to improve $d[v]$.
3. Output the array $d[\,]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why greedy correctness holds:** Once a vertex $u$ is extracted (with the smallest $d$-value among unvisited vertices), no future path through another unvisited vertex can yield a shorter distance to $u$. This relies *critically* on $w(e) \geq 0$.

**Complexity Analysis:**
- Using an **array / unsorted list**: $O(\vert V \vert^2 + \vert E \vert)$ → best for **dense graphs**.
- Using a **binary min-heap**: $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ → standard choice.
- Using a **Fibonacci heap**: $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ → asymptotically optimal.

### 2.2 Bellman-Ford Algorithm — The Dynamic-Programming Workhorse

**Bellman-Ford** solves the SSSP for **general weighted directed graphs**, including those with **negative edge weights**, and **detects negative-weight cycles** reachable from $s$.

#### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted)

1. Initialize $d[s] = 0$, $d[v] = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$.
2. **Repeat $\vert V \vert - 1$ times:** for *every* edge $(u, v) \in E$, attempt relaxation.
3. **Negative-cycle check:** perform one more pass over all edges. If any $d[v]$ can still be improved, the graph contains a negative-weight cycle reachable from $s$ → **SSSP is undefined**.

**Intuition:** Any shortest path in a graph without negative cycles contains at most $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges (otherwise a cycle could be removed to yield a shorter path). After $k$ iterations, $d[v]$ equals the shortest path using at most $k$ edges.

**Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ time, $O(\vert V \vert)$ space. This is asymptotically slower than Dijkstra but far more **versatile**.

### 2.3 BFS — The Unweighted Special Case

For an **unweighted** graph (or a graph where all weights are equal), **Breadth-First Search** solves SSSP in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ time. The level at which a vertex is first discovered equals its shortest-path distance from $s$.

### 2.4 DAG Shortest Path — The Linear-Time Gem

For a **Directed Acyclic Graph**, perform a **topological sort** in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$, then relax all outgoing edges of each vertex in topologically sorted order. The result is a single-pass solution in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ — and it works **even with negative weights** (provided there is no cycle).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d[v] = d[u] + w(u, v)$ | Tentative new distance to $v$ via $u$ | All SSSP algorithms |
| 2 | $\delta(s, v) = \min \sum w(e_i)$ | True shortest path distance from $s$ to $v$ | Definition |
| 3 | **#Iterations (Bellman-Ford)** $= \vert V \vert - 1$ | Required passes for convergence | Bellman-Ford |
| 4 | **#Passes (negative cycle test)** $= \vert V \vert$ | Extra pass detects $\exists$ relaxation | Bellman-Ford |
| 5 | $T_{\text{Dijkstra}} = O(\vert V \vert^2)$ (array) | Dense-graph complexity | Dijkstra |
| 6 | $T_{\text{Dijkstra}} = O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ (heap) | Sparse-graph complexity | Dijkstra |
| 7 | $T_{\text{Bellman-Ford}} = O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | Always, regardless of sign | Bellman-Ford |
| 8 | $T_{\text{BFS}} = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Unweighted only | BFS |
| 9 | $T_{\text{DAG}} = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | After topological sort | DAG-SSSP |
| 10 | **Triangle inequality** $\delta(s, v) \leq \delta(s, u) + w(u, v)$ | Invariant preserved on all SSSP | Proofs |
| 11 | **Upper-bound property** $d[v] \geq \delta(s, v)$ always | Invariant of initialization + relaxation | Dijkstra proof |
| 12 | **Convergence property** $u$ finalized $\Rightarrow$ $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$ | Valid only when $w \geq 0$ | Dijkstra proof |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Routing Protocols:** OSPF and IS-IS use a Dijkstra-like algorithm (the *Link-State* approach) to build routing tables.
- **GPS Navigation:** Modern navigation systems pre-compute SSSP on a contracted road graph (Contraction Hierarchies) for sub-millisecond queries.
- **Robotic Path Planning:** Bellman-Ford variants are used in partially-observable environments where terrain costs can be negative (e.g., energy-recharge nodes).
- **Game AI:** A* search — an SSSP extension — guides non-player characters in video games.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Dijkstra's Algorithm (Full Trace)

Consider the following directed weighted graph with source vertex $s = A$:

$$V = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$$

| Edge | Weight |
|---|---|
| $A \to B$ | $4$ |
| $A \to C$ | $2$ |
| $B \to C$ | $1$ |
| $B \to D$ | $5$ |
| $C \to D$ | $8$ |
| $C \to E$ | $10$ |
| $D \to E$ | $2$ |

**Initial State:**
$$d[A] = 0, \;\; d[B] = d[C] = d[D] = d[E] = \infty$$

**Iteration 1:** Extract $A$ (min $= 0$). Relax outgoing edges $A \to B$, $A \to C$:

$$d[B] = \min(\infty, 0 + 4) = 4$$
$$d[C] = \min(\infty, 0 + 2) = 2$$

$$d[\,] = [A:0,\; B:4,\; C:2,\; D:\infty,\; E:\infty]$$

**Iteration 2:** Extract $C$ (min $= 2$). Relax $C \to B$, $C \to D$, $C \to E$:

$$d[B] = \min(4, 2 + 1) = 3 \quad (\text{improved!})$$
$$d[D] = \min(\infty, 2 + 8) = 10$$
$$d[E] = \min(\infty, 2 + 10) = 12$$

$$d[\,] = [A:0,\; B:3,\; C:2,\; D:10,\; E:12]$$

**Iteration 3:** Extract $B$ (min $= 3$). Relax $B \to C$, $B \to D$:

$$d[C] = \min(2, 3 + 1) = 2 \quad (\text{no improvement})$$
$$d[D] = \min(10, 3 + 5) = 8 \quad (\text{improved!})$$

$$d[\,] = [A:0,\; B:3,\; C:2,\; D:8,\; E:12]$$

**Iteration 4:** Extract $D$ (min $= 8$). Relax $D \to E$:

$$d[E] = \min(12, 8 + 2) = 10 \quad (\text{improved!})$$

$$d[\,] = [A:0,\; B:3,\; C:2,\; D:8,\; E:10]$$

**Iteration 5:** Extract $E$ (min $= 10$). No outgoing edges.

**Final Shortest Distances from $A$:**

$$\boxed{\delta(A,A) = 0, \; \delta(A,B) = 3, \; \delta(A,C) = 2, \; \delta(A,D) = 8, \; \delta(A,E) = 10}$$

**Predecessor array for path reconstruction:**

$$\pi[\,] = [A:\text{NIL},\; B:C,\; C:A,\; D:B,\; E:D]$$

The shortest path $A \to E$ is recovered by back-tracing: $E \to D \to B \to C \to A$, i.e., $A \to C \to B \to D \to E$ (total weight $2+1+5+2 = 10$).

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Bellman-Ford with Negative Weights (Full Trace)

Consider a directed graph with source $S$ and the following edges:

| Edge | Weight |
|---|---|
| $S \to A$ | $4$ |
| $S \to B$ | $5$ |
| $A \to B$ | $-2$ |
| $A \to C$ | $5$ |
| $B \to C$ | $3$ |
| $C \to A$ | $1$ |

Note the **negative edge** $A \to B$ (weight $-2$); Dijkstra would fail here.

**Initial State:**

$$d[S] = 0, \quad d[A] = d[B] = d[C] = \infty$$

We require $\vert V \vert - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3$ iterations.

**Iteration 1 (in edge order):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \to A &: \quad d[A] = \min(\infty, \, 0 + 4) = 4 \\
S \to B &: \quad d[B] = \min(\infty, \, 0 + 5) = 5 \\
A \to B &: \quad d[B] = \min(5, \, 4 + (-2)) = 2 \\
A \to C &: \quad d[C] = \min(\infty, \, 4 + 5) = 9 \\
B \to C &: \quad d[C] = \min(9, \, 2 + 3) = 5 \\
C \to A &: \quad d[A] = \min(4, \, 5 + 1) = 4 \quad (\text{no change})
\end{aligned}
$$

After Iteration 1: $d = [S:0,\; A:4,\; B:2,\; C:5]$.

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \to A &: \quad d[A] = \min(4, 4) = 4 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
S \to B &: \quad d[B] = \min(2, 5) = 2 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
A \to B &: \quad d[B] = \min(2, 4 + (-2)) = 2 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
A \to C &: \quad d[C] = \min(5, 4 + 5) = 5 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
B \to C &: \quad d[C] = \min(5, 2 + 3) = 5 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
C \to A &: \quad d[A] = \min(4, 5 + 1) = 4 \quad (\text{no change})
\end{aligned}
$$

After Iteration 2: $d = [S:0,\; A:4,\; B:2,\; C:5]$ — **stable**.

**Iteration 3:** Same as Iteration 2 — still stable.

**Iteration 4 (Negative-Cycle Detection Pass):**

Re-scan all edges. No $d[v]$ can be further reduced. Therefore, **no negative-weight cycle reachable from $S$ exists**.

**Final Shortest Distances:**

$$\boxed{\delta(S,S) = 0, \; \delta(S,A) = 4, \; \delta(S,B) = 2, \; \delta(S,C) = 5}$$

**Path Reconstruction via $\pi[\,]$:**

$$S \to A \to B \to C \quad \text{with total weight} \quad 4 + (-2) + 3 = 5 \;\; \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Comparative Trace Matrix — When to Use Which Algorithm

| Property | Dijkstra | Bellman-Ford | BFS | DAG-Topological |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Handles negative weights | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ (acyclic) |
| Detects negative cycle | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Time complexity | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ or $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
| Strategy | Greedy (Extract-Min) | Dynamic Programming | Level-order | Topological sweep |
| Data structure | Min-heap | Edge list | FIFO queue | Stack / DFS finish times |
| Predecessor built? | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| KTU exam weightage | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Example Graph Used in Section 3.1 (Visualized)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA((A))
    nodeB((B))
    nodeC((C))
    nodeD((D))
    nodeE((E))
    nodeA -->|4| nodeB
    nodeA -->|2| nodeC
    nodeB -->|1| nodeC
    nodeB -->|5| nodeD
    nodeC -->|8| nodeD
    nodeC -->|10| nodeE
    nodeD -->|2| nodeE
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Node $A$ is the source. The numeric labels on edges represent the weights used in the Dijkstra trace of Section 3.1.

### 4.2 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start])
    initA[Initialize: d source 0, others infinity, Q contains all vertices]
    checkEmptyA{Q empty?}
    extractA[Extract vertex u with minimum d from Q]
    relaxA[For each edge u to v relax d v and update predecessor]
    updateA[Add u to finalized set S]
    endA([Output d array and predecessor array])
    startA --> initA
    initA --> checkEmptyA
    checkEmptyA -- No --> extractA
    extractA --> relaxA
    relaxA --> updateA
    updateA --> checkEmptyA
    checkEmptyA -- Yes --> endA
```

### 4.3 Bellman-Ford Algorithm — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start])
    initB[Initialize: d source 0, others infinity]
    iterInit[Set iteration counter i 1]
    checkIterB{i less than V minus 1?}
    scanAllB[For every edge u to v attempt relaxation]
    incrementB[Increment i]
    negCyclePass[Perform one more pass over all edges]
    negDecision{Any distance reduced?}
    reportNeg[Report negative weight cycle detected]
    endB([Output shortest distances and predecessors])
    noNeg[No negative cycle: Output d array and predecessor array]
    startB --> initB
    initB --> iterInit
    iterInit --> checkIterB
    checkIterB -- Yes --> scanAllB
    scanAllB --> incrementB
    incrementB --> checkIterB
    checkIterB -- No --> negCyclePass
    negCyclePass --> negDecision
    negDecision -- Yes --> reportNeg
    negDecision -- No --> noNeg
    reportNeg --> endB
    noNeg --> endB
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Real-World SSSP in a Network Router

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pkt1[Incoming Packets] --> rt1[Routing Table Lookup]
    rt1 --> dijkModule[Dijkstra Engine with Min Heap]
    topology[(Network Topology DB)]
    topology --> dijkModule
    dijkModule --> distTable[(Shortest Distance Table)]
    distTable --> rt1
    rt1 --> pkt2[Forward Packets on Shortest Path]
```

> **Engineering Insight:** In an OSPF router, the topology database is the graph, the Dijkstra engine is the SSSP solver, and the distance table is the routing table that gets installed in hardware. The same architecture is mirrored in Software-Defined Networking (SDN) controllers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the Single Source All Destinations problem. State the conditions under which Dijkstra's algorithm fails to produce a correct solution.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Single Source All Destinations (SSSP)** problem on a weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with source $s$ asks for the shortest-path distance $\delta(s, v)$ from $s$ to every other vertex $v \in V$.

Dijkstra's algorithm **fails to produce a correct solution when the graph contains negative-weight edges** (i.e., $w(u, v) < 0$ for some edge). This is because Dijkstra's correctness proof relies on the **greedy choice property** — once a vertex is extracted with the smallest tentative distance, no future relaxation through a different path can yield a shorter distance. This property breaks down when a future edge has negative weight, since a later (unvisited) vertex could potentially *reduce* the distance of an already-finalized vertex. **(3 Marks: Definition 1M, Condition 1M, Reason 1M)**

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is edge relaxation? Write the pseudocode-style expression for relaxing an edge $(u, v)$ of weight $w$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Edge relaxation** is the fundamental operation in SSSP algorithms that *attempts* to improve the current best-known distance $d[v]$ to a vertex $v$ by routing through an adjacent vertex $u$.

$$\text{RELAX}(u, v, w): \quad \textbf{if } d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v) \textbf{ then } d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u, v); \; \pi[v] \leftarrow u$$

The operation "relaxes" (tightens) the upper bound $d[v]$ on the true distance $\delta(s, v)$. Every SSSP algorithm (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, BFS, DAG-SSSP) is fundamentally a *strategic sequence of edge relaxations*. **(3 Marks: Definition 1M, Condition 1M, Pseudocode 1M)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Apply **Dijkstra's algorithm** to the graph below with source vertex $S$. Show the distance array $d[\,]$ and predecessor array $\pi[\,]$ after each iteration. Finally, write the shortest path from $S$ to every other vertex. **(7 Marks)**

| Edge | Weight |
|---|---|
| $S \to A$ | 10 |
| $S \to C$ | 5 |
| $A \to B$ | 1 |
| $A \to C$ | 2 |
| $B \to D$ | 4 |
| $C \to A$ | 3 |
| $C \to B$ | 9 |
| $C \to D$ | 2 |
| $D \to B$ | 6 |
| $D \to E$ | 12 |

**(b)** Prove the **upper-bound property** and the **convergence property** of Dijkstra's algorithm. State clearly where the assumption of non-negative edge weights is used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Initial:** $d[S]=0$, $d[A]=d[B]=d[C]=d[D]=d[E]=\infty$. Min-heap: $\{S, A, B, C, D, E\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Extract $S$ ($d=0$):**

$$d[A] = \min(\infty, 0+10) = 10, \;\; d[C] = \min(\infty, 0+5) = 5$$

$d[\,] = [S:0,\; A:10,\; B:\infty,\; C:5,\; D:\infty,\; E:\infty]$, $\pi = [A:S,\; C:S]$.

**Iteration 2 — Extract $C$ ($d=5$):**

$$d[A] = \min(10, 5+3) = 8, \quad d[B] = \min(\infty, 5+9) = 14, \quad d[D] = \min(\infty, 5+2) = 7$$

$d[\,] = [S:0,\; A:8,\; B:14,\; C:5,\; D:7,\; E:\infty]$, $\pi = [A:C,\; B:C,\; D:C]$.

**Iteration 3 — Extract $D$ ($d=7$):**

$$d[B] = \min(14, 7+6) = 13, \quad d[E] = \min(\infty, 7+12) = 19$$

$d[\,] = [S:0,\; A:8,\; B:13,\; C:5,\; D:7,\; E:19]$, $\pi = [B:D,\; E:D]$.

**Iteration 4 — Extract $A$ ($d=8$):**

$$d[B] = \min(13, 8+1) = 9$$

$d[\,] = [S:0,\; A:8,\; B:9,\; C:5,\; D:7,\; E:19]$, $\pi = [B:A]$.

**Iteration 5 — Extract $B$ ($d=9$):** No outgoing edges.

**Iteration 6 — Extract $E$ ($d=19$):** No outgoing edges.

**Final Shortest Paths:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \to A &: \quad S \to C \to A, \quad \delta(S,A) = 8 \\
S \to B &: \quad S \to C \to A \to B, \quad \delta(S,B) = 9 \\
S \to C &: \quad S \to C, \quad \delta(S,C) = 5 \\
S \to D &: \quad S \to C \to D, \quad \delta(S,D) = 7 \\
S \to E &: \quad S \to C \to D \to E, \quad \delta(S,E) = 19
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Mark split: Initial setup 1M, Iterations 1–3 = 3M, Iterations 4–6 = 2M, Final paths = 1M.)**

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

> **Upper-Bound Property:** For every vertex $v \in V$ and at every point during the algorithm's execution, $d[v] \geq \delta(s, v)$.

*Proof by induction on the number of edge relaxations.* **Base case:** after initialization, $d[s] = 0 = \delta(s, s)$ and $d[v] = \infty \geq \delta(s, v)$ for $v \neq s$. ✓ **Inductive step:** relaxation updates $d[v]$ only to $d[u] + w(u, v)$. By the inductive hypothesis, $d[u] \geq \delta(s, u)$, so the new value satisfies $d[u] + w(u, v) \geq \delta(s, u) + w(u, v) \geq \delta(s, v)$ (the last inequality is the **triangle inequality** for shortest paths). Hence the upper-bound property is preserved. **Q.E.D.**

> **Convergence Property:** Once a vertex $u$ is added to the finalized set $S$ (i.e., is extracted from the min-heap), $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$ and this value never changes thereafter.

*Proof.* Let $u$ be the first vertex extracted from the priority queue. By the **min-heap property**, $d[u]$ is the smallest among all unvisited vertices. Consider any path $P$ from $s$ to $u$. Let $x$ be the first vertex on $P$ that is *not* in $S$ (such an $x$ exists because $u \notin S$ when extracted). The predecessor of $x$ on $P$ lies in $S$, so when that predecessor was extracted, the edge into $x$ was relaxed, giving $d[x] \leq d[\text{pred}] + w \leq \delta(s, x)$ (using the path-so-far being shortest). Combining with $d[u] \leq d[x]$ (min-heap), and the fact that $w \geq 0$ on the subpath $x \rightsquigarrow u$:

$$d[u] \leq d[x] \leq \delta(s, x) \leq \delta(s, x) + \delta(x, u) = \delta(s, u)$$

But by the upper-bound property, $d[u] \geq \delta(s, u)$. Hence $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$. **Q.E.D.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** The non-negativity $w \geq 0$ is **crucially** used in the last inequality $\delta(s, x) + \delta(x, u) = \delta(s, u)$ for the subpath $x \rightsquigarrow u$. With a negative-weight edge, the subpath distance $\delta(x, u)$ could be smaller than the sum of non-shortest sub-distances, breaking the chain. Many KTU students lose 2–3 marks by writing the proof without explicitly identifying where $w \geq 0$ is invoked.

---

> **Question B.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Apply the **Bellman-Ford algorithm** on the directed graph below with source $S$. Show the distance table after every iteration and detect whether any negative-weight cycle exists. **(7 Marks)**

| Edge | Weight |
|---|---|
| $S \to A$ | 6 |
| $S \to B$ | 7 |
| $A \to C$ | 5 |
| $A \to D$ | $-4$ |
| $B \to A$ | 8 |
| $B \to C$ | $-3$ |
| $C \to B$ | 7 |
| $D \to C$ | 2 |

**(b)** Compare **Dijkstra's** and **Bellman-Ford** algorithms across five dimensions: negative-weight handling, time complexity, greedy vs DP strategy, cycle detection, and graph-type applicability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Initial:** $d[S] = 0$, $d[A] = d[B] = d[C] = d[D] = \infty$. Need $\vert V \vert - 1 = 3$ iterations.

**Iteration 1 (in edge order):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \to A &: \quad d[A] = \min(\infty, 0+6) = 6 \\
S \to B &: \quad d[B] = \min(\infty, 0+7) = 7 \\
A \to C &: \quad d[C] = \min(\infty, 6+5) = 11 \\
A \to D &: \quad d[D] = \min(\infty, 6+(-4)) = 2 \\
B \to A &: \quad d[A] = \min(6, 7+8) = 6 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
B \to C &: \quad d[C] = \min(11, 7+(-3)) = 4 \\
C \to B &: \quad d[B] = \min(7, 4+7) = 7 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
D \to C &: \quad d[C] = \min(4, 2+2) = 4 \quad (\text{no change})
\end{aligned}
$$

After Iter 1: $d = [S:0,\; A:6,\; B:7,\; C:4,\; D:2]$.

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \to A &: \quad d[A] = 6 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
S \to B &: \quad d[B] = 7 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
A \to C &: \quad d[C] = \min(4, 6+5) = 4 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
A \to D &: \quad d[D] = \min(2, 6+(-4)) = 2 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
B \to A &: \quad d[A] = \min(6, 7+8) = 6 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
B \to C &: \quad d[C] = \min(4, 7+(-3)) = 4 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
C \to B &: \quad d[B] = \min(7, 4+7) = 7 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
D \to C &: \quad d[C] = \min(4, 2+2) = 4 \quad (\text{no change})
\end{aligned}
$$

After Iter 2: $d = [S:0,\; A:6,\; B:7,\; C:4,\; D:2]$ — **stable**.

**Iteration 3:** Same — stable.

**Iteration 4 — Negative Cycle Test Pass:**

Re-scan all edges — no relaxation succeeds. **No negative-weight cycle is reachable from $S$**.

**Final Shortest Distances from $S$:**

$$\boxed{\delta(S,S) = 0, \; \delta(S,A) = 6, \; \delta(S,B) = 7, \; \delta(S,C) = 4, \; \delta(S,D) = 2}$$

**(Mark split: Initial + Iter 1 = 4M, Iter 2 & 3 = 2M, Negative cycle check = 1M.)**

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Dimension | Dijkstra | Bellman-Ford |
|---|---|---|
| **Negative-weight handling** | ❌ Fails; produces incorrect (over-confident) distances | ✅ Handles; correctness proven for any real weights |
| **Time complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ array; $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert)$ heap | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ always |
| **Strategy** | **Greedy** — extract minimum, finalize, never revisit | **Dynamic Programming** — iteratively extend path length by 1 edge |
| **Negative cycle detection** | ❌ Cannot detect | ✅ One extra pass detects any reachable negative cycle |
| **Graph-type applicability** | Best for non-negative weights (dense, sparse) | Best for general directed graphs; mandatory for negative weights |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A *very common* KTU mistake is to claim that Dijkstra "fails" on negative edges because "it goes into an infinite loop" — this is **false**. Dijkstra terminates, but the *answer it produces is incorrect*. Use precise wording in your KTU answer scripts. Another frequent slip: forgetting the **predecessor array** $\pi[\,]$ — the examiner's key requires path reconstruction to award the full 7 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SSSP =** compute shortest distance from **one** source to **all** other vertices in a weighted graph. **(vs APSP which is all-pairs, e.g., Floyd-Warshall.)**
- **Edge relaxation** is the atomic operation common to *every* SSSP algorithm.
- **Dijkstra** = greedy, requires **non-negative weights**, runs in $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ with array or $O((V+E)\log V)$ with heap; finalizes one vertex per extraction.
- **Bellman-Ford** = dynamic programming, requires $\vert V \vert - 1$ **iterations**; handles negative edges; one extra pass detects **negative-weight cycles**.
- **BFS** solves unweighted SSSP in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
- **DAG-SSSP** (topological order + relaxation) is the **fastest** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ and supports negative weights, but **only on acyclic graphs**.
- **Predecessor array** $\pi[\,]$ is **mandatory** for KTU's path-reconstruction sub-parts — never omit it.
- Always state the **assumptions** (non-negative, no negative cycle, etc.) before applying an algorithm.
- For KTU valuation: include the **trace table** (iteration-by-iteration distance array) — handwritten answers without a trace table typically lose **2–3 marks**.
- **Memorize the convergence and upper-bound properties** of Dijkstra — they appear in KTU's 7-mark proof questions almost every semester.
- Negative-weight cycle test: **if any edge can still be relaxed after $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations, a negative cycle exists reachable from $s$.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting Techniques – Selection Sort, Insertion Sort, Quick Sort, Merge Sort, Heap Sort, Radix Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sorting Techniques – Selection Sort, Insertion Sort, Quick Sort, Merge Sort, Heap Sort, Radix Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overviews

## 1.1 What is Sorting?

Sorting is the fundamental algorithmic process of **rearranging the elements of a finite collection (such as an array, list, or sequence) into a specific logical order**, typically ascending or descending, based on a defined comparison key. It is a cornerstone operation in computer science, forming the backbone of search engines, database indexing, operating system schedulers, and data analytics pipelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Sorting is the process of placing elements from a collection in some specific order so that they can be arranged meaningfully. The order can be numerical, lexicographical, or based on any user-defined comparator.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Card Player's Hand"
Imagine you are a rummy player holding 13 random playing cards in your hand. Your goal is to organize them from the **lowest value (A♣) to the highest (K♠)**. You have several instinctive strategies:
- **Selection Approach**: Scan the entire hand, find the smallest card, and place it on the table (left side). Repeat. *(This is Selection Sort)*
- **Insertion Approach**: Pick up cards one by one from the deck and slide them into the correct position in your already-sorted hand. *(This is Insertion Sort)*
- **Quick/Divide Approach**: Pick a "pivot" card, split the hand into two piles (smaller and larger), and recursively sort each pile. *(This is Quick Sort)*
- **Merge Approach**: Split the hand into single cards, then repeatedly merge adjacent pairs into sorted mini-hands until the whole hand is sorted. *(This is Merge Sort)*
- **Heap Approach**: Build a "priority structure" where the smallest (or largest) card is always on top, extract it, and reheapify. *(This is Heap Sort)*
- **Radix Approach**: Sort the cards into buckets based on the first digit (or suit), then re-bucket based on the next digit, and so on. *(This is Radix Sort)*

---

## 1.2 Classification of Sorting Algorithms

The six sorting techniques covered in this module are broadly classified as follows:

| Category | Algorithms | Comparison Type |
|----------|------------|-----------------|
| **Simple / Quadratic Sorts** | Selection Sort, Insertion Sort | Comparison-based, O(n²) |
| **Efficient / Divide & Conquer** | Quick Sort, Merge Sort | Comparison-based, O(n log n) |
| **Efficient / Heap-based** | Heap Sort | Comparison-based, O(n log n) |
| **Non-Comparative / Linear** | Radix Sort | Digit-based, O(nk) |

---

## 1.3 Formal Definitions of Each Algorithm

### 1.3.1 Selection Sort
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Selection Sort is an in-place comparison-based algorithm that divides the input list into two parts: a sorted sublist built up from left to right at the front (initially empty), and a sublist of the remaining unsorted elements. At each iteration, the algorithm **selects the minimum (or maximum) element** from the unsorted sublist and swaps it with the leftmost unsorted element, expanding the sorted region by one.

### 1.3.2 Insertion Sort
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Insertion Sort iterates through an input array, and at each iteration, removes one element from the unsorted portion, finds its correct position in the already-sorted left portion by **shifting larger elements one position to the right**, and inserts it there. It works exactly like how most people sort playing cards in their hands.

### 1.3.3 Quick Sort
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Quick Sort is a highly efficient **divide-and-conquer** algorithm that selects a **pivot element** from the array and partitions the other elements into two sub-arrays according to whether they are less than or greater than the pivot. The sub-arrays are then recursively sorted. Its average-case time complexity is **O(n log n)**, making it one of the most popular sorting algorithms in practice.

### 1.3.4 Merge Sort
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Merge Sort is a **divide-and-conquer** algorithm that recursively splits the array into two equal halves, sorts each half, and then **merges** the two sorted halves into a single sorted array. It guarantees **O(n log n)** time complexity in all cases and is a **stable** sort, but requires **O(n) auxiliary space**.

### 1.3.5 Heap Sort
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Heap Sort is a comparison-based algorithm that uses a **Binary Heap** data structure (a nearly complete binary tree). It first builds a **Max-Heap** (or Min-Heap) from the input array, then repeatedly extracts the maximum element from the heap root, places it at the end of the array, and **heapifies** the reduced heap. It achieves **O(n log n)** in-place sorting without recursion or auxiliary arrays.

### 1.3.6 Radix Sort
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Radix Sort is a **non-comparative**, integer-based sorting algorithm that sorts data with integer keys by grouping keys by the individual digits which share the same significant position and value (e.g., units, tens, hundreds). It uses a **stable sub-algorithm** (typically Counting Sort) as a subroutine to sort the digits. It achieves **O(nk)** time complexity, where **k** is the number of digits in the largest number.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Expected Vocabulary)

- **In-place Sort:** Uses only **O(1)** extra memory beyond the input array. *(e.g., Selection, Insertion, Quick, Heap)*
- **Stable Sort:** Preserves the relative order of equal elements. *(e.g., Insertion, Merge, Radix)*
- **Internal vs External Sort:** Internal = all data fits in RAM; External = data on disk.
- **Adaptive:** Complexity improves on already-sorted input. *(e.g., Insertion Sort becomes O(n))*
- **Comparison-based vs Non-comparison-based:** Quick/Merge/Heap use `<`, `>`, `=`; Radix uses digit decomposition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Algorithmic Logic of Each Sort

### 2.1.1 Selection Sort — Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** Set `min_idx = 0` (or current boundary).
- **Step 2:** Scan the unsorted portion `A[i+1 .. n-1]` to find the index of the minimum element.
- **Step 3:** Swap `A[i]` with `A[min_idx]`.
- **Step 4:** Increment `i` and repeat until `i = n-2`.
- **Why it works:** Each pass fixes exactly one element in its final sorted position.

### 2.1.2 Insertion Sort — Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** Mark the first element as sorted (`i = 1`).
- **Step 2:** Take `key = A[i]` (the first unsorted element).
- **Step 3:** Compare `key` with elements in the sorted region from right to left.
- **Step 4:** Shift each element that is greater than `key` one position to the right.
- **Step 5:** Insert `key` into the vacated position.
- **Why it works:** Maintains a sorted prefix that grows by one element each pass.

### 2.1.3 Quick Sort — Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** Choose a `pivot` element (first, last, middle, or random).
- **Step 2:** **Partition:** Rearrange so that elements `< pivot` are on the left, and elements `>` pivot are on the right. The pivot is now in its final sorted position.
- **Step 3:** Recursively apply Quick Sort to the left and right sub-arrays.
- **Why it works:** The partition ensures the pivot's final position is fixed; recursion handles the rest.

### 2.1.4 Merge Sort — Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** **Divide:** Split the array into two halves of roughly equal size.
- **Step 2:** **Conquer:** Recursively sort each half.
- **Step 3:** **Combine (Merge):** Merge the two sorted halves into a single sorted array using a linear-time merge procedure.
- **Why it works:** The merge of two already-sorted arrays is straightforward and linear.

### 2.1.5 Heap Sort — Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** **Build-Heap:** Transform the input array into a Max-Heap in O(n) time (using `Build-Max-Heap`).
- **Step 2:** **Sortdown:** Swap the root (maximum) with the last element of the heap, reduce the heap size by 1, and call `Max-Heapify` on the root.
- **Step 3:** Repeat Step 2 until the heap has only one element.
- **Why it works:** The Max-Heap property guarantees the root is always the largest remaining unsorted element.

### 2.1.6 Radix Sort — Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** Find the maximum number in the array to determine the number of digits `d`.
- **Step 2:** For each digit position `k = 1, 2, ..., d` (from least significant to most significant):
  - Use a stable sub-sort (Counting Sort) to sort the array based on digit `k`.
- **Why it works:** Because each pass is stable, the relative order from previous (less significant) passes is preserved, so by the final pass, the entire array is sorted.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Time & Space Complexity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **single most important table** for the KTU exam. Memorize it thoroughly. All values are derived from standard recurrence analysis and are **mathematically verified**.

| Algorithm | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space | Stable? | In-Place? | Recursive? |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|------------|-------|---------|-----------|------------|
| **Selection Sort** | $\Omega(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Insertion Sort** | $\Omega(n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Quick Sort** | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\log n)$ | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Merge Sort** | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Heap Sort** | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(1)$ | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Radix Sort** | $\Omega(nk)$ | $\Theta(nk)$ | $O(nk)$ | $O(n+b)$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |

> **Note on notation:** $b$ is the base (e.g., 10) and $k$ is the number of digits in the largest number for Radix Sort. The symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ in absolute-value contexts is written here as `\mid` to preserve table syntax.

---

## 2.3 Master Theorem Application (Recurrence Relations)

The Master Theorem applies to recurrences of the form:

$$T(n) = aT\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

where $a \geq 1$ and $b > 1$, with $f(n)$ being the cost of the work done outside the recursive calls.

### 2.3.1 Derivation of Merge Sort's Recurrence
Merge Sort splits the array into 2 halves, recursively sorts each, and merges in $O(n)$ time:

$$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n)$$

Here, $a = 2$, $b = 2$, and $f(n) = \Theta(n)$. We compute $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$. Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, this falls into **Case 2** of the Master Theorem, giving:

$$T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

### 2.3.2 Derivation of Quick Sort's Average-Case Recurrence
With a random pivot, both partitions are expected to be of size $n/2$:

$$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n)$$

This is identical to Merge Sort's recurrence, hence the average case is $\Theta(n \log n)$.

### 2.3.3 Derivation of Quick Sort's Worst-Case Recurrence
If the pivot is always the smallest (or largest) element (e.g., already-sorted input with first-element pivot):

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + \Theta(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n)$$

Unrolling: $T(n) = \Theta(n) + \Theta(n-1) + \cdots + \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^2)$.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Algorithm | Real-World Use Case |
|-----------|---------------------|
| **Selection Sort** | Embedded systems with minimal memory; small fixed-size datasets. |
| **Insertion Sort** | Nearly-sorted data; online algorithms (sorting data as it arrives); small $n$ (often used as base case in hybrid introsort). |
| **Quick Sort** | General-purpose sorting in C's `qsort`, Unix kernels, language runtimes (Java's `Arrays.sort` for primitives). |
| **Merge Sort** | External sorting (sorting huge files on disk); stable sorting needed; linked list sorting. |
| **Heap Sort** | Priority queues, scheduling, memory-constrained environments needing guaranteed $O(n \log n)$. |
| **Radix Sort** | Sorting integers, strings of equal length, fixed-length records (e.g., ID numbers, dates, phone numbers). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Trace Walkthroughs)

## 3.1 Selection Sort — Full Trace

**Input Array:** $A = [64, 25, 12, 22, 11]$, $n = 5$

**Pass 1 ($i = 0$):** Find minimum in `A[0..4]`
- Scan: $64 \to 25 \to 12 \to 22 \to 11$. Minimum is $11$ at index $4$.
- Swap `A[0]` and `A[4]`: $A = [11, 25, 12, 22, 64]$
- Sorted region: $\{11\}$

**Pass 2 ($i = 1$):** Find minimum in `A[1..4]`
- Scan: $25 \to 12 \to 22 \to 64$. Minimum is $12$ at index $2$.
- Swap `A[1]` and `A[2]`: $A = [11, 12, 25, 22, 64]$
- Sorted region: $\{11, 12\}$

**Pass 3 ($i = 2$):** Find minimum in `A[2..4]`
- Scan: $25 \to 22 \to 64$. Minimum is $22$ at index $3$.
- Swap `A[2]` and `A[3]`: $A = [11, 12, 22, 25, 64]$
- Sorted region: $\{11, 12, 22\}$

**Pass 4 ($i = 3$):** Find minimum in `A[3..4]`
- Scan: $25 \to 64$. Minimum is $25$ at index $3$. Already in place.
- No swap: $A = [11, 12, 22, 25, 64]$
- Sorted region: $\{11, 12, 22, 25\}$

**Final Sorted Array:** $[11, 12, 22, 25, 64]$ ✓

> **Total number of comparisons:** $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10$
> **Total number of swaps:** $n - 1 = 4$

---

## 3.2 Insertion Sort — Full Trace

**Input Array:** $A = [12, 11, 13, 5, 6]$, $n = 5$

**Pass 1 ($i = 1$):** $key = A[1] = 11$
- Compare $11 < 12$ → shift $12$ right → $A = [12, 12, 13, 5, 6]$
- Insert $11$ at index $0$: $A = [11, 12, 13, 5, 6]$

**Pass 2 ($i = 2$):** $key = A[2] = 13$
- Compare $13 < 12$? No. No shifts needed.
- $A = [11, 12, 13, 5, 6]$

**Pass 3 ($i = 3$):** $key = A[3] = 5$
- $5 < 13$? Yes → shift $13$ → $A = [11, 12, 13, 13, 6]$
- $5 < 12$? Yes → shift $12$ → $A = [11, 12, 12, 13, 6]$
- $5 < 11$? Yes → shift $11$ → $A = [11, 11, 12, 13, 6]$
- Insert $5$ at index $0$: $A = [5, 11, 12, 13, 6]$

**Pass 4 ($i = 4$):** $key = A[4] = 6$
- $6 < 13$? Yes → shift $13$ → $A = [5, 11, 12, 13, 13]$
- $6 < 12$? Yes → shift $12$ → $A = [5, 11, 12, 12, 13]$
- $6 < 11$? Yes → shift $11$ → $A = [5, 11, 11, 12, 13]$
- $6 < 5$? No. Stop.
- Insert $6$ at index $1$: $A = [5, 6, 11, 12, 13]$

**Final Sorted Array:** $[5, 6, 11, 12, 13]$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Quick Sort — Full Trace (Lomuto Partition)

**Input Array:** $A = [10, 80, 30, 90, 40, 50, 70]$, pivot = last element ($70$)

**Initial Call:** `QuickSort(A, 0, 6)`, pivot = $A[6] = 70$

### Partition (Lomuto) Trace:
| Step | $i$ | $j$ | Comparison | Action | Array State |
|------|-----|-----|------------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | $-1$ | 0 | $A[0]=10 < 70$ | $i=0$ | [10, 80, 30, 90, 40, 50, 70] |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | $A[1]=80 < 70$? No | — | [10, 80, 30, 90, 40, 50, 70] |
| 3 | 0 | 2 | $A[2]=30 < 70$ | swap A[1]↔A[2], $i=1$ | [10, 30, 80, 90, 40, 50, 70] |
| 4 | 1 | 3 | $A[3]=90 < 70$? No | — | [10, 30, 80, 90, 40, 50, 70] |
| 5 | 1 | 4 | $A[4]=40 < 70$ | swap A[2]↔A[4], $i=2$ | [10, 30, 40, 90, 80, 50, 70] |
| 6 | 2 | 5 | $A[5]=50 < 70$ | swap A[3]↔A[5], $i=3$ | [10, 30, 40, 50, 80, 90, 70] |
| 7 | 3 | 6 | Loop ends | swap A[4]↔A[6], $i=4$ | [10, 30, 40, 50, 70, 90, 80] |

Pivot $70$ is now at index $4$ — its **final sorted position**. Return $i = 4$.

### Recursive Calls:
- `QuickSort(A, 0, 3)` sorts $[10, 30, 40, 50]$ → already sorted
- `QuickSort(A, 5, 6)` sorts $[90, 80]$ → swap → $[80, 90]$

**Final Sorted Array:** $[10, 30, 40, 50, 70, 80, 90]$ ✓

---

## 3.4 Merge Sort — Full Trace

**Input Array:** $A = [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]$, $n = 7$

### Divide Tree (Top-Down):
```
            [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]
           /                            \
      [38, 27, 43]                  [3, 9, 82, 10]
      /        \                    /             \
   [38]      [27, 43]           [3, 9]        [82, 10]
              /    \             /    \          /    \
           [27]  [43]         [3]    [9]      [82]  [10]
```

### Merge Steps (Bottom-Up):
1. Merge $[27]$ and $[43]$ → $[27, 43]$
2. Merge $[38]$ and $[27, 43]$ → $[27, 38, 43]$
3. Merge $[3]$ and $[9]$ → $[3, 9]$
4. Merge $[82]$ and $[10]$ → $[10, 82]$
5. Merge $[3, 9]$ and $[10, 82]$ → $[3, 9, 10, 82]$
6. Merge $[27, 38, 43]$ and $[3, 9, 10, 82]$ → **Final sorted: $[3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82]$** ✓

### Detailed Merge of `[27, 38, 43]` and `[3, 9, 10, 82]`:
| $i$ (left idx) | $j$ (right idx) | $k$ (merged idx) | Left[$i$] | Right[$j$] | Action | Result Array |
|----------------|------------------|------------------|-----------|------------|--------|--------------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 27 | 3 | 3 < 27, take Right | [3] |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 27 | 9 | 9 < 27, take Right | [3, 9] |
| 0 | 2 | 2 | 27 | 10 | 10 < 27, take Right | [3, 9, 10] |
| 0 | 3 | 3 | 27 | 82 | 27 < 82, take Left | [3, 9, 10, 27] |
| 1 | 3 | 4 | 38 | 82 | 38 < 82, take Left | [3, 9, 10, 27, 38] |
| 2 | 3 | 5 | 43 | 82 | 43 < 82, take Left | [3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43] |
| 3 (exhausted) | 3 | 6 | — | — | Copy Right[3] = 82 | [3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82] |

---

## 3.5 Heap Sort — Full Trace

**Input Array:** $A = [4, 10, 3, 5, 1]$, $n = 5$

### Step 1: Build Max-Heap
Starting from the last non-leaf node at index $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1 = 1$:

**Heapify index 1 (subtree rooted at 10, children: 5, 1):**
- Largest among $\{10, 5, 1\}$ = $10$ (already at root). No swap.
- Array: $[4, 10, 3, 5, 1]$

**Heapify index 0 (subtree rooted at 4, children: 10, 3):**
- Largest among $\{4, 10, 3\}$ = $10$ at index $1$.
- Swap $A[0] \leftrightarrow A[1]$: Array = $[10, 4, 3, 5, 1]$
- Now heapify index 1 (subtree at 4, children: 5, 1):
  - Largest among $\{4, 5, 1\}$ = $5$ at index $3$.
  - Swap $A[1] \leftrightarrow A[3]$: Array = $[10, 5, 3, 4, 1]$
  - Node 1 is a leaf, stop.
- Max-Heap built: $[10, 5, 3, 4, 1]$

### Step 2: Sortdown (Repeatedly extract max)
- **Extract $10$:** Swap $A[0] \leftrightarrow A[4]$ → $[1, 5, 3, 4, 10]$, heap size = 4, Heapify(0): largest among $\{1, 5, 3\}$ = $5$ → swap → $[5, 1, 3, 4, 10]$, Heapify(1) on $\{1, 4\}$: $4 > 1$ → swap → $[5, 4, 3, 1, 10]$
- **Extract $5$:** Swap $A[0] \leftrightarrow A[3]$ → $[1, 4, 3, 5, 10]$, heap size = 3, Heapify(0): largest among $\{1, 4, 3\}$ = $4$ → swap → $[4, 1, 3, 5, 10]$
- **Extract $4$:** Swap $A[0] \leftrightarrow A[2]$ → $[3, 1, 4, 5, 10]$, heap size = 2, Heapify(0): largest among $\{3, 1\}$ = $3$ → no swap.
- **Extract $3$:** Swap $A[0] \leftrightarrow A[1]$ → $[1, 3, 4, 5, 10]$, heap size = 1. Done.

**Final Sorted Array:** $[1, 3, 4, 5, 10]$ ✓

---

## 3.6 Radix Sort — Full Trace (LSD with Counting Sort)

**Input Array:** $A = [170, 45, 75, 90, 802, 24, 2, 66]$, $n = 8$

**Maximum element:** $802$ → has $3$ digits → $d = 3$ passes.

### Pass 1: Sort by Units Digit (digit $0$)
| Number | 170 | 45 | 75 | 90 | 802 | 24 | 2 | 66 |
|--------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|----|-----|
| Units digit | 0 | 5 | 5 | 0 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 6 |

After stable Counting Sort by units digit:
**Result:** $[170, 90, 802, 2, 24, 45, 75, 66]$

### Pass 2: Sort by Tens Digit (digit $1$)
| Number | 170 | 90 | 802 | 2 | 24 | 45 | 75 | 66 |
|--------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|----|-----|
| Tens digit | 7 | 9 | 0 | 0 | 2 | 4 | 7 | 6 |

After stable Counting Sort by tens digit:
**Result:** $[802, 2, 24, 45, 66, 170, 75, 90]$

### Pass 3: Sort by Hundreds Digit (digit $2$)
| Number | 802 | 2 | 24 | 45 | 66 | 170 | 75 | 90 |
|--------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|----|-----|
| Hundreds digit | 8 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |

After stable Counting Sort by hundreds digit:
**Final Result:** $[2, 24, 45, 66, 75, 90, 170, 802]$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LSD (Least Significant Digit) works:** Because each pass is **stable**, the ordering established by less significant digits is preserved when we sort by more significant digits, yielding a fully sorted result after the final pass.

---

## 3.7 Comparative Performance Analysis (Empirical View)

For $n = 10,000$ elements on a typical machine:

| Algorithm | Approx. Time (ms) | Extra Memory | Stability |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------|-----------|
| Selection Sort | $\sim 110$ | 0 bytes | Unstable |
| Insertion Sort | $\sim 95$ (random), $\sim 0.4$ (sorted) | 0 bytes | Stable |
| Quick Sort | $\sim 12$ | ~$\log n$ stack | Unstable |
| Merge Sort | $\sim 15$ | $8n$ bytes | Stable |
| Heap Sort | $\sim 18$ | 0 bytes | Unstable |
| Radix Sort | $\sim 5$ | $O(n+b)$ | Stable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that for **already-sorted input**, Insertion Sort is the clear winner because of its $\Omega(n)$ best-case behavior. This is why hybrid algorithms (e.g., Timsort, Introsort) use Insertion Sort as a base case for small partitions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Sorting Algorithms] --> B0{Comparison Based?}
    B0 -->|Yes| C0[Comparison Sorts]
    B0 -->|No| D0[Non Comparison Sorts]
    C0 --> E0{Time Complexity}
    E0 -->|O of n squared| F0[Quadratic Sorts]
    E0 -->|O of n log n| G0[Efficient Sorts]
    F0 --> F1[Selection Sort]
    F0 --> F2[Insertion Sort]
    G0 --> G1[Quick Sort]
    G0 --> G2[Merge Sort]
    G0 --> G3[Heap Sort]
    D0 --> D1[Radix Sort]
    D1 --> D2[Uses Counting Sort as sub routine]
```

## 4.2 Selection Sort Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Selection Sort] --> S1[Set i = 0]
    S1 --> S2{i less than n minus 1?}
    S2 -->|No| S3[End Sort]
    S2 -->|Yes| S4[Set min_idx = i]
    S4 --> S5[Set j = i + 1]
    S5 --> S6{j less than n?}
    S6 -->|No| S7[Swap A i with A min_idx]
    S6 -->|Yes| S8{A j less than A min_idx?}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Set min_idx = j]
    S8 -->|No| S10[Increment j]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S6
    S7 --> S11[Increment i]
    S11 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Insertion Sort Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I0[Start Insertion Sort] --> I1[Set i = 1]
    I1 --> I2{i less than n?}
    I2 -->|No| I3[End Sort]
    I2 -->|Yes| I4[Set key = A i]
    I4 --> I5[Set j = i - 1]
    I5 --> I6{j greater than or equal to 0 AND A j greater than key?}
    I6 -->|Yes| I7[Set A j plus 1 = A j]
    I7 --> I8[Decrement j]
    I8 --> I6
    I6 -->|No| I9[Set A j plus 1 = key]
    I9 --> I10[Increment i]
    I10 --> I2
```

## 4.4 Quick Sort Recursive Architecture (Lomuto Partition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[QuickSort A low high] --> Q1{low less than high?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q2[Return base case]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3[Call Partition A low high]
    Q3 --> Q4[pi = returned pivot index]
    Q4 --> Q5[Recursively call QuickSort A low pi minus 1]
    Q5 --> Q6[Recursively call QuickSort A pi plus 1 high]
    Q6 --> Q7[Return to caller]
    Q2 --> Q7
```

## 4.5 Merge Sort Divide-and-Merge Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[MergeSort A low high] --> M1{low less than high?}
    M1 -->|No| M2[Return sorted]
    M1 -->|Yes| M3[mid = low plus high divided by 2]
    M3 --> M4[MergeSort A low mid]
    M4 --> M5[MergeSort A mid plus 1 high]
    M5 --> M6[Merge A low mid high]
    M6 --> M2
```

## 4.6 Heap Sort Architecture (Build-Heap + Sortdown)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[HeapSort A n] --> H1[Call BuildMaxHeap A n]
    H1 --> H2[Set i = n minus 1]
    H2 --> H3{i greater than 0?}
    H3 -->|No| H4[End Sort]
    H3 -->|Yes| H5[Swap A 0 with A i]
    H5 --> H6[Call MaxHeapify A 0 i]
    H6 --> H7[Decrement i]
    H7 --> H3
```

## 4.7 Radix Sort Architecture (LSD with Counting Sort)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[RadixSort A n] --> R1[Find max element in A]
    R1 --> R2[Set d = number of digits in max]
    R2 --> R3[Set exp = 1]
    R3 --> R4{exp less than or equal to d?}
    R4 -->|No| R5[End Sort]
    R4 -->|Yes| R6[CountingSort A n exp]
    R6 --> R7[Multiply exp by 10]
    R7 --> R4
```

## 4.8 Stability vs Performance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0{Need Stable Sort?} -->|Yes| P1{Data is Integer Keys?}
    P0 -->|No| P2{Memory Constrained?}
    P1 -->|Yes| P3[Use Radix Sort]
    P1 -->|No| P4[Use Merge Sort]
    P2 -->|Yes| P5{Need Worst Case Guarantee?}
    P2 -->|No| P6[Use Quick Sort for Speed]
    P5 -->|Yes| P7[Use Heap Sort]
    P5 -->|No| P6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Differentiate between a stable and an in-place sorting algorithm. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **stable sort** preserves the relative order of elements with equal keys. Example: **Merge Sort**. *(1 mark)*
- An **in-place sort** requires only $O(1)$ additional memory beyond the input array. Example: **Heap Sort**. *(1 mark)*
- Quick Sort is in-place but not stable; Merge Sort is stable but not in-place. *(1 mark)*

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Why is Quick Sort preferred over Merge Sort in practice, despite having a worse worst-case complexity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Quick Sort has superior **cache locality** because it works on contiguous partitions, making it faster in practice due to CPU cache hits. *(1 mark)*
- Quick Sort is **in-place** ($O(\log n)$ stack), whereas Merge Sort requires $O(n)$ extra space. *(1 mark)*
- Quick Sort's **average case** of $O(n \log n)$ combined with very small constants makes it empirically faster; the $O(n^2)$ worst case can be avoided using **randomized pivot selection** or **median-of-three** strategies. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ CHOICE A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A(a).** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted] **(7 marks)** Sort the following array using **Merge Sort**. Show all the divide and merge steps clearly.
> 
> **Array:** $A = [25, 14, 33, 12, 8, 71, 49, 62]$

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Divide Tree (3 marks):**
```
                    [25, 14, 33, 12, 8, 71, 49, 62]
                  /                                  \
            [25, 14, 33, 12]                  [8, 71, 49, 62]
            /            \                    /             \
       [25, 14]        [33, 12]           [8, 71]         [49, 62]
        /    \          /    \             /    \           /    \
      [25]  [14]    [33]   [12]         [8]   [71]       [49]  [62]
```

**Step 2 — Merge Steps (4 marks):**

| Merge | Inputs | Output |
|-------|--------|--------|
| M1 | [25] + [14] | [14, 25] |
| M2 | [33] + [12] | [12, 33] |
| M3 | [14, 25] + [12, 33] | [12, 14, 25, 33] |
| M4 | [8] + [71] | [8, 71] |
| M5 | [49] + [62] | [49, 62] |
| M6 | [8, 71] + [49, 62] | [8, 49, 62, 71] |
| M7 | [12, 14, 25, 33] + [8, 49, 62, 71] | **[8, 12, 14, 25, 33, 49, 62, 71]** |

**Final Sorted Array:** $[8, 12, 14, 25, 33, 49, 62, 71]$ ✓

---

> **Q.A(b).** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(7 marks)** Demonstrate the **Quick Sort** algorithm on the array below using **Lomuto Partitioning** with the **last element as pivot**. Show the state of the array after each partition.
> 
> **Array:** $A = [33, 12, 45, 8, 27, 18, 50, 6]$

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Initial Call:** `QuickSort(A, 0, 7)`, pivot = $A[7] = 6$

**Partition 1 (Lomuto):**
- $i = -1$, $j$ scans $0 \to 6$
- $j=0$: $33 < 6$? No.
- $j=1$: $12 < 6$? No.
- $j=2$: $45 < 6$? No.
- $j=3$: $8 < 6$? No.
- $j=4$: $27 < 6$? No.
- $j=5$: $18 < 6$? No.
- $j=6$: $50 < 6$? No.
- Loop ends. Swap $A[i+1]$ and $A[7]$: swap $A[0] \leftrightarrow A[7]$
- Array after: $[6, 12, 45, 8, 27, 18, 50, 33]$, pivot $6$ is at index $0$. ✓

**Partition 2:** Sort $A[1..7] = [12, 45, 8, 27, 18, 50, 33]$, pivot = $33$
- $i = 0$ (relative), $j$ scans $1 \to 7$
- $j=1$: $12 < 33$? Yes. $i=1$. Swap $A[1] \leftrightarrow A[1]$ (no change).
- $j=2$: $45 < 33$? No.
- $j=3$: $8 < 33$? Yes. $i=2$. Swap $A[2] \leftrightarrow A[3]$ → $[6, 12, 8, 45, 27, 18, 50, 33]$
- $j=4$: $27 < 33$? Yes. $i=3$. Swap $A[3] \leftrightarrow A[4]$ → $[6, 12, 8, 27, 45, 18, 50, 33]$
- $j=5$: $18 < 33$? Yes. $i=4$. Swap $A[4] \leftrightarrow A[5]$ → $[6, 12, 8, 27, 18, 45, 50, 33]$
- $j=6$: $50 < 33$? No.
- $j=7$: pivot position, stop. Swap $A[5] \leftrightarrow A[7]$ → $[6, 12, 8, 27, 18, 33, 50, 45]$
- Pivot $33$ is at index $5$. ✓

**Recursion continues on $[12, 8, 27, 18]$ and $[50, 45]$.** Final result after all partitions:
$$A = [6, 8, 12, 18, 27, 33, 45, 50] \checkmark$$

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B (14 Marks)

> **Q.B(a).** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(7 marks)** Build a **Max-Heap** for the following array and then apply **Heap Sort** to sort it in ascending order. Show the heap after every extraction.
> 
> **Array:** $A = [20, 15, 30, 5, 10, 25, 35]$

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Build Max-Heap (2 marks)**

Starting heapify from the last non-leaf node at index $\lfloor 7/2 \rfloor - 1 = 2$:

- **Heapify index 2 (value 30):** Children: 25 (idx 5), 35 (idx 6). Largest = 35. Swap $A[2] \leftrightarrow A[6]$ → $[20, 15, 35, 5, 10, 25, 30]$. Node 6 is a leaf, stop.
- **Heapify index 1 (value 15):** Children: 5 (idx 3), 10 (idx 4). Largest = 15 (root). No swap.
- **Heapify index 0 (value 20):** Children: 15 (idx 1), 35 (idx 2). Largest = 35. Swap $A[0] \leftrightarrow A[2]$ → $[35, 15, 20, 5, 10, 25, 30]$. Now heapify index 2 (value 20): children 25, 30. Largest = 30. Swap $A[2] \leftrightarrow A[6]$ → $[35, 15, 30, 5, 10, 25, 20]$.

**Max-Heap Built:** $[35, 15, 30, 5, 10, 25, 20]$ ✓

**Step 2: Sortdown — Extract Max Repeatedly (5 marks)**

| Extraction | Swap | Array After Swap | Heapify Result |
|------------|------|------------------|----------------|
| Extract 35 | A[0]↔A[6] | [20, 15, 30, 5, 10, 25, **35**] | Heapify(0): 30 largest → [30, 15, 20, 5, 10, 25, 35] |
| Extract 30 | A[0]↔A[5] | [25, 15, 20, 5, 10, **30**, 35] | Heapify(0): 25 largest → [25, 15, 20, 5, 10, 30, 35] |
